  │  │  📋 logs   │  │  │  │  📋 logs   │  │  │  │  📋 logs   │  │
  │  └────────────┘  │  │  └────────────┘  │  │  └────────────┘  │
  │  + anihpj pods   │  │  + anihpj pods   │  │  + anihpj pods   │
  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘

  ┌──────────────────┐
  │    worker-4      │  ← NEW node joins cluster!
  │  ┌────────────┐  │
  │  │  fluentd   │  │  DaemonSet controller AUTOMATICALLY
  │  │  📋 logs   │  │  schedules a pod here! 🔄
  │  └────────────┘  │
  └──────────────────┘

  ✅ Every node always has exactly 1 fluentd pod
  ✅ New nodes get a pod automatically
  ✅ Removed nodes — pod is cleaned up automatically
DaemonSet Commands 
# View DaemonSets
kubectl get daemonsets -A
# NAMESPACE     NAME         DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   UP-TO-DATE   NODE SELECTOR   AGE
# kube-system   fluentd      4         4         4       4            <none>          10d
# kube-system   kube-proxy   4         4         4       4            <none>          30d

# DaemonSet on specific nodes only (using nodeSelector)
# Add nodeSelector to template.spec to limit which nodes get the pod

# Check which nodes have DaemonSet pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=fluentd -o wide
💡 CKA Tip: No Imperative Command for DaemonSets

Unlike Deployments and Jobs, there's no kubectl create daemonset command. You must write YAML. Quick trick: generate a Deployment YAML, then change kind: Deployment to kind: DaemonSet and remove the replicas field.

DaemonSet Update Strategy

DaemonSets support two update strategies, similar to Deployments:

Strategy Behavior Use Case
RollingUpdate (default) Pods updated node by node — old pod killed, new pod started on each node sequentially Most updates — zero gap per node
OnDelete Pods only updated when you manually delete them — full control over rollout pace Critical system daemons where you want to control timing (e.g., CNI plugin)
DaemonSet Update Strategy YAML 
# In the DaemonSet spec:
spec:
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate        # or OnDelete
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1        # Update 1 node at a time (default)
      # maxSurge: 0            # Not supported for DaemonSets

16.4 StatefulSets — Stateful Applications

StatefulSets are for applications that need stable, unique identity and persistent storage. Unlike Deployments (random pod names, shared storage), StatefulSets provide predictable pod names, ordered deployment, and per-pod persistent volumes.

Feature Deployment StatefulSet
Pod Names Random: app-x7k2z, app-m9p4q Ordered: app-0, app-1, app-2
DNS Identity Only via Service (shared) Each pod gets own DNS: app-0.svc.ns
Storage Shared or none Per-pod PVC (survives pod restarts)
Creation Order All at once (parallel) Sequential: 0 → 1 → 2 (ordered)
Deletion Order Random Reverse: 2 → 1 → 0 (ordered)
Use Case Stateless apps (anihpj Django) Databases, Kafka, Redis Cluster, ZooKeeper
postgresql-statefulset.yaml — anihpj Database 
# Headless Service (required for StatefulSet DNS)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None               # Headless — no cluster IP
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432
    name: postgres
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: "postgres-headless"  # Required: links to headless service
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:16
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
        env:
        - name: POSTGRES_DB
          value: "anihpj"
        - name: PGDATA
          value: "/var/lib/postgresql/data/pgdata"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "1Gi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1"
            memory: "2Gi"
  volumeClaimTemplates:            # Per-pod PVC template
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      storageClassName: standard
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi
StatefulSet — Ordered Pods with Stable Identity & Per-Pod Storage
  StatefulSet: "postgres"    replicas: 3
  serviceName: "postgres-headless"

  Deployment Pods:             StatefulSet Pods:
  (random names)               (ordered, predictable)
  ┌──────────────┐             ┌──────────────┐
  │ app-x7k2z    │             │ postgres-0   │ ← Created FIRST (primary)
  ├──────────────┤             │  DNS: postgres-0.postgres-headless
  │ app-m9p4q    │             ├──────────────┤
  ├──────────────┤             │ postgres-1   │ ← Created SECOND (must wait for 0)
  │ app-j3r8w    │             │  DNS: postgres-1.postgres-headless
  └──────────────┘             ├──────────────┤
                               │ postgres-2   │ ← Created THIRD
                               │  DNS: postgres-2.postgres-headless
                               └──────────────┘

  Per-Pod Persistent Volumes (survive pod restarts!):
  ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐
  │postgres-0│     │postgres-1│     │postgres-2│
  │┌────────┐│     │┌────────┐│     │┌────────┐│
  ││data-   ││     ││data-   ││     ││data-   ││
  ││postgres││     ││postgres││     ││postgres││
  ││-0      ││     ││-1      ││     ││-2      ││
  ││ 10Gi   ││     ││ 10Gi   ││     ││ 10Gi   ││
  │└───┬────┘│     │└───┬────┘│     │└───┬────┘│
  └────┼─────┘     └────┼─────┘     └────┼─────┘
       ▼                ▼                ▼
  ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐
  │  PV-0   │     │  PV-1   │     │  PV-2   │
  │ (disk)  │     │ (disk)  │     │ (disk)  │
  └─────────┘     └─────────┘     └─────────┘

  Key guarantees:
  ✅ Stable network identity  — postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc
  ✅ Ordered deployment       — 0 → 1 → 2 (each must be Ready before next)
  ✅ Ordered deletion         — 2 → 1 → 0 (reverse order)
  ✅ Stable storage           — PVC survives pod restart (data preserved!)
⚠️ StatefulSet Requires a Headless Service

You must create a headless Service (clusterIP: None) for the StatefulSet. The serviceName field in the StatefulSet spec must match the Service name. This enables DNS records for each pod like pod-name.service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local.

🚨 StatefulSet Deletion — PVCs Are NOT Deleted!

When you delete a StatefulSet (even with --cascade=foreground), the PersistentVolumeClaims are preserved. This is by design to prevent data loss. You must manually delete them:

  • kubectl delete statefulset postgres → Pods deleted, PVCs remain ✅
  • kubectl get pvc → data-postgres-0, data-postgres-1, data-postgres-2 still exist
  • kubectl delete pvc data-postgres-0 data-postgres-1 data-postgres-2 → Manually clean up

This is intentional — recreating the StatefulSet will automatically reattach to existing PVCs, preserving all data!

StatefulSet Scaling — Ordered Operations 
# Scale up — pods created in order (3, 4, 5...)
kubectl scale statefulset postgres --replicas=5
# postgres-3 created (waits until Ready)
# postgres-4 created (waits until Ready)

# Scale down — pods deleted in REVERSE order (4, 3)
kubectl scale statefulset postgres --replicas=3
# postgres-4 terminated first
# postgres-3 terminated second
# postgres-0, 1, 2 untouched ✅

# PVCs from scaled-down pods are PRESERVED
kubectl get pvc
# data-postgres-3 and data-postgres-4 still exist
# Re-scaling up will reattach to them!

Choosing the Right Workload Type

🚀
Deployment

Stateless apps that run forever.
anihpj Django web server, nginx, API services.

🗄️
StatefulSet

Apps needing stable identity & storage.
PostgreSQL, MySQL, Kafka, Redis Cluster.

📋
DaemonSet

One pod on every node.
Log collectors, monitoring agents, CNI plugins.

✅
Job / CronJob

Run-to-completion / scheduled tasks.
DB migrations, backups, batch processing.

Decision Tree: Which Workload Type Do I Need?
                        ┌──────────────────┐
                        │  New Workload?   │
                        └────────┬─────────┘
                                 │
                    ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                    │                         │
              Runs forever?              Runs once/scheduled?
                    │                         │
              ┌─────┴─────┐             ┌─────┴─────┐
              │           │             │           │
        All nodes?   Some pods?    On schedule?   One-time?
              │           │             │           │
         DaemonSet  ┌─────┴─────┐   CronJob      Job
                    │           │
              Needs stable   Stateless?
              identity/PVC?     │
                    │       Deployment
              StatefulSet

  ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
  ║  anihpj Stack Mapping:                                ║
  ║                                                        ║
  ║  📦 anihpj Django      → Deployment (stateless)       ║
  ║  🗄️ PostgreSQL         → StatefulSet (needs storage)  ║
  ║  📋 Fluentd logging    → DaemonSet (every node)       ║
  ║  🔄 DB migration       → Job (run once)               ║
  ║  💾 Nightly backup     → CronJob (scheduled)          ║
  ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

🔑 Chapter 16 Quick Reference

Action Command
Create Jobkubectl create job NAME --image=IMG -- CMD
Create CronJobkubectl create cronjob NAME --image=IMG --schedule="EXPR" -- CMD
View Jobskubectl get jobs
View CronJobskubectl get cronjobs
View DaemonSetskubectl get daemonsets -A
View StatefulSetskubectl get statefulsets
Job logskubectl logs job/NAME
Delete completed jobskubectl delete jobs --field-selector status.successful=1
Suspend CronJobkubectl patch cronjob NAME -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'
Trigger CronJob nowkubectl create job NAME-manual --from=cronjob/NAME

🟡 Part 5: Services & Networking Chapters 17-20

Chapter 17: Kubernetes Networking Fundamentals

Networking is the backbone of Kubernetes. Every pod, service, and DNS lookup depends on a correctly configured network. In this chapter you'll master the 4 fundamental networking rules, understand how CNI plugins implement the network, trace packets between pods on the same and different nodes, explore how kube-proxy routes service traffic, and build a troubleshooting toolkit for common network issues — all using our anihpj/jobpost Django stack as a real-world example.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2 hours 🌐 Networking 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The 4 fundamental Kubernetes networking rules — why NAT-free networking matters
  • ✅ How CNI plugins (Calico, Cilium, Flannel) implement the network layer
  • ✅ Pod-to-Pod communication — same-node vs cross-node packet paths
  • ✅ How kube-proxy translates Service IPs into pod endpoints using iptables/IPVS
  • ✅ Network troubleshooting commands and strategies for the CKA exam
🎓 CKA Relevance
Services & Networking 20% Troubleshooting 30%
Networking Is 20% of the CKA Exam

Networking questions on the CKA range from creating Services and Ingress to troubleshooting DNS and pod connectivity. Key exam skills:

  • Know which CNI plugin is installed: ls /etc/cni/net.d/
  • Check kube-proxy mode: kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-proxy-xxxxx
  • Test connectivity: kubectl exec -it pod -- curl http://service-name
  • Debug DNS: kubectl exec -it pod -- nslookup service-name

17.1 The Kubernetes Networking Model

Kubernetes enforces a flat, NAT-free networking model. This is fundamentally different from Docker's default bridge networking, and understanding these rules is critical for every CKA topic that follows.

1️⃣
Every Pod Gets a Unique IP

No two pods share an IP address. Containers within the same pod share the pod's IP and communicate via localhost.

2️⃣
Pod-to-Pod Without NAT

Any pod can reach any other pod directly using its IP — even across nodes. No network address translation needed.

3️⃣
Node-to-Pod Without NAT

Nodes can communicate directly with any pod IP in the cluster without address translation.

4️⃣
Pod Sees Its Own Real IP

Inside a pod, the IP it sees for itself is the same IP that every other pod and node sees. No surprises.

The 4 Fundamental Kubernetes Networking Rules
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  KUBERNETES FLAT NETWORKING MODEL                                        │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Rule 1: Every Pod gets a unique IP                                     │
  │  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐            │
  │  │  anihpj-abc    │  │  postgres-0    │  │  redis-xyz     │            │
  │  │  10.244.1.5    │  │  10.244.2.8    │  │  10.244.3.2    │            │
  │  │  (Node 1)      │  │  (Node 2)      │  │  (Node 1)      │            │
  │  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Rule 2: Pod-to-Pod — direct, no NAT                                    │
  │  anihpj (10.244.1.5) ────────────────────→ postgres (10.244.2.8) ✅    │
  │                       no translation needed!                             │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Rule 3: Node-to-Pod — direct, no NAT                                  │
  │  Node 1 (192.168.1.10) ──────────────────→ postgres (10.244.2.8) ✅    │
  │                          on Node 2, still direct!                        │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Rule 4: Pod sees its own real IP                                       │
  │  Inside anihpj:     hostname -i  →  10.244.1.5                         │
  │  From postgres:     ping anihpj  →  10.244.1.5   ✅ Same IP!          │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
  │  CONTRAST: Docker default networking (WITHOUT Kubernetes)                │
  │  Container A → NAT → Host IP → NAT → Container B   ❌ Complex!        │
  │  Container sees 172.17.0.2, outside sees host IP    ❌ Different!      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📖 Why This Model Matters

The flat networking model means you can take any application that communicates over TCP/IP and run it in Kubernetes without modifying the code. Your anihpj Django app connects to postgres:5432 exactly the same way whether running locally or in a Kubernetes cluster. No port mapping gymnastics, no NAT tables to debug.

Containers Sharing a Pod Network Namespace
  ┌──────────────────────── Pod: anihpj-abc ─────────────────────────┐
  │                   IP: 10.244.1.5                                  │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐   │
  │  │  Container 1   │  │  Container 2   │  │  Container 3      │   │
  │  │  django        │  │  nginx sidecar │  │  log-collector    │   │
  │  │  :8000         │  │  :80           │  │  (no port)        │   │
  │  └───────┬────────┘  └───────┬────────┘  └────────┬──────────┘   │
  │          │                   │                     │              │
  │  ════════╧═══════════════════╧═════════════════════╧═══════════  │
  │                     Shared Network Namespace                      │
  │                     (same IP, same localhost)                      │
  │                                                                    │
  │  nginx → curl http://localhost:8000  ✅  (reaches django)        │
  │  django → curl http://localhost:80   ✅  (reaches nginx)         │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ⚠️  Port conflicts! Two containers can't both use :8000        │
  │                                                                    │
  │  eth0 ──── 10.244.1.5 ──── connected to node bridge             │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Multi-Container Pod Networking

All containers in a pod share the same network namespace — same IP, same port space, same localhost. This is why sidecar containers (like nginx reverse proxies or log collectors) can communicate with the main container via localhost. But beware: two containers can't listen on the same port.

IP Address Ranges in Kubernetes

Kubernetes Network CIDRs

🖥️ Node Network
Physical/VM network: 192.168.1.0/24 — each node gets an IP from your infrastructure
📦 Pod Network (CIDR)
10.244.0.0/16 (default for Flannel) or 192.168.0.0/16 (Calico). Each node gets a /24 subnet.
🔗 Service Network
10.96.0.0/12 (default). Virtual IPs — no real interface, handled by kube-proxy iptables rules.
Inspecting Cluster Network CIDRs 
# Pod CIDR — check kubeadm config or CNI
kubectl cluster-info dump | grep -m 1 cluster-cidr
# --cluster-cidr=10.244.0.0/16

# Service CIDR
kubectl cluster-info dump | grep -m 1 service-cluster-ip-range
# --service-cluster-ip-range=10.96.0.0/12

# Each node's pod subnet
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.podCIDR}{"\n"}{end}'
# worker-1    10.244.1.0/24
# worker-2    10.244.2.0/24
# worker-3    10.244.3.0/24

# Pod IPs — verify flat networking
kubectl get pods -o wide
# NAME           READY   IP            NODE
# anihpj-abc     1/1     10.244.1.5    worker-1
# postgres-0     1/1     10.244.2.8    worker-2

17.2 CNI Plugins — How Networking Is Implemented

The Container Network Interface (CNI) is a specification that defines how networking is set up for containers. Kubernetes itself does NOT implement networking — it delegates to a CNI plugin. As a CKA admin, you must know how to identify, install, and troubleshoot the CNI.

CNI Plugin — The Missing Piece
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  KUBERNETES says: "I need networking. Here's my spec."   │
  │                                                          │
  │  ┌──────────┐     ┌────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │ kubelet  │ ──→ │  CNI Plugin (your choice)       │    │
  │  │          │     │                                  │    │
  │  │ "Create  │     │  1. Assign pod IP               │    │
  │  │  network │     │  2. Set up veth pair             │    │
  │  │  for new │     │  3. Configure bridge/routing     │    │
  │  │  pod"    │     │  4. Enable cross-node traffic    │    │
  │  └──────────┘     └────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                          │
  │  Without CNI → pods have NO networking → cluster broken  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
CNI Plugin Networking Mode Network Policy Performance Best For
Calico BGP (Layer 3) / VXLAN overlay ✅ Full support ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Production, CKA labs, enterprise
Cilium eBPF (kernel-level) ✅ Advanced (L7) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ High-performance, observability
Flannel VXLAN overlay (Layer 2) ❌ None ⭐⭐⭐☆☆ Simple clusters, learning
Weave Net Mesh overlay ✅ Basic ⭐⭐⭐☆☆ Easy setup, small clusters
Canal Flannel networking + Calico policies ✅ Full support ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ Flannel users needing NetworkPolicy
Identifying and Inspecting the CNI Plugin 
# Which CNI is installed? Check the config directory
ls /etc/cni/net.d/
# 10-calico.conflist    ← Calico is installed

# Check CNI pods running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -E "calico|cilium|flannel|weave"
# calico-node-abc12    1/1    Running
# calico-node-def34    1/1    Running

# View CNI binary directory
ls /opt/cni/bin/
# calico  calico-ipam  bandwidth  bridge  host-local  loopback  portmap

# Check pod CIDR assigned to each node
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}: {.spec.podCIDR}{"\n"}{end}'

# If CNI pods are crashing — cluster networking is broken!
kubectl describe pod calico-node-abc12 -n kube-system
⚠️ CKA Exam: CNI Must Be Installed

After kubeadm init, nodes show NotReady until you install a CNI. The exam may ask you to install one:

  • Calico: kubectl apply -f https://docs.projectcalico.org/manifests/calico.yaml
  • Flannel: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/flannel-io/flannel/releases/latest/download/kube-flannel.yml
  • If nodes are NotReady — first check if CNI pods exist in kube-system
CNI Plugin Lifecycle — What Happens When a Pod Is Created
  kubectl run anihpj --image=anihpj:latest
         │
         ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  1. API Server      │  Stores pod spec in etcd
  └────────┬───────────┘
           ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  2. Scheduler       │  Assigns pod to worker-1
  └────────┬───────────┘
           ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  3. kubelet         │  On worker-1, sees new pod assignment
  │     (worker-1)      │
  └────────┬───────────┘
           ▼
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  4. kubelet calls CNI plugin:                       │
  │     /opt/cni/bin/calico ADD                         │
  │                                                     │
  │     CNI plugin performs:                             │
  │     ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │     │ a. Create network namespace for pod       │   │
  │     │ b. Create veth pair (pod eth0 ↔ host)    │   │
  │     │ c. Assign IP: 10.244.1.5  (from IPAM)   │   │
  │     │ d. Set up routing to/from pod            │   │
  │     │ e. Connect to bridge (cbr0/cni0)         │   │
  │     │ f. Apply any NetworkPolicy rules         │   │
  │     └──────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                     │
  │     Returns: { "ips": [{"address": "10.244.1.5"}] } │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  5. kubelet starts  │  Container runtime starts containers
  │     containers      │  Pod is now RUNNING with networking ✅
  └────────────────────┘

17.3 Pod-to-Pod Communication

Understanding how packets travel between pods is essential for troubleshooting. The path differs depending on whether pods are on the same node or different nodes.

Key Network Primitives — Building Blocks

🔌 veth pair
A virtual Ethernet cable with two ends: one inside the pod (eth0), one on the host (connected to the bridge). Like a network patch cable between two switches.
🌉 Linux bridge (cbr0/cni0)
A virtual layer-2 switch that connects all pod veth endpoints on a single node. Forwards frames between pods.
📝 Network namespace
An isolated network stack (interfaces, routes, iptables). Each pod gets its own. Containers within a pod share one.
📦 VXLAN tunnel
Overlay encapsulation: the pod packet is wrapped in an outer UDP packet to cross the physical network between nodes.
Same-Node Communication — Via Linux Bridge
  ┌──────────────────────── Node 1 ────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────┐          │
  │  │ anihpj-abc      │          │ redis-xyz        │          │
  │  │ 10.244.1.5      │          │ 10.244.1.12      │          │
  │  │ eth0 ──────┐    │          │    ┌────── eth0  │          │
  │  └────────────┼────┘          └────┼─────────────┘          │
  │               │  veth pair         │  veth pair              │
  │          ┌────▼────────────────────▼────┐                   │
  │          │       cbr0 / cni0 (bridge)   │                   │
  │          │       10.244.1.1             │                   │
  │          └──────────────────────────────┘                   │
  │                                                             │
  │  Packet path: anihpj eth0 → veth → bridge → veth → redis  │
  │  ✅ Fast — stays entirely within the node kernel           │
  │  ✅ No encapsulation overhead                               │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Cross-Node Communication — Via Overlay Network (VXLAN/BGP)
  ┌────────── Node 1 ──────────┐         ┌────────── Node 2 ──────────┐
  │                              │         │                              │
  │ ┌─────────────────┐         │         │         ┌─────────────────┐ │
  │ │ anihpj-abc      │         │         │         │ postgres-0      │ │
  │ │ 10.244.1.5      │         │         │         │ 10.244.2.8      │ │
  │ │ eth0            │         │         │         │        eth0     │ │
  │ └──────┬──────────┘         │         │         └────────▲────────┘ │
  │        │ veth                │         │                  │ veth     │
  │ ┌──────▼──────┐             │         │         ┌────────┴────┐    │
  │ │ cbr0 bridge │             │         │         │ cbr0 bridge │    │
  │ │ 10.244.1.1  │             │         │         │ 10.244.2.1  │    │
  │ └──────┬──────┘             │         │         └────────▲────┘    │
  │        │ routing             │         │                  │         │
  │ ┌──────▼──────┐             │         │         ┌────────┴────┐    │
  │ │  eth0 (NIC) │═════════════╪═════════╪═════════│  eth0 (NIC) │    │
  │ │ 192.168.1.10│   physical  │         │         │ 192.168.1.11│    │
  │ └─────────────┘   network   │         │         └─────────────┘    │
  └──────────────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────────────┘

  Packet journey:
  1. anihpj (10.244.1.5) → sends to postgres (10.244.2.8)
  2. Pod eth0 → veth → cbr0 bridge → node routing table
  3. CNI plugin encapsulates packet (VXLAN header or BGP route)
  4. Node 1 eth0 ═══ physical network ═══ Node 2 eth0
  5. Node 2 decapsulates → cbr0 → veth → postgres eth0
  6. postgres receives packet from 10.244.1.5 ✅
🌐
Overlay (VXLAN)

Pod packets wrapped in outer UDP packet. Works anywhere (cloud, bare metal). Slight overhead. Used by: Flannel, Calico (VXLAN mode), Cilium.

⚡
Routing (BGP)

Node routing tables updated to forward pod traffic directly. No encapsulation overhead. Requires L3 network. Used by: Calico (BGP mode).

Testing Pod-to-Pod Connectivity 
# Get pod IPs
kubectl get pods -o wide
# NAME           IP            NODE
# anihpj-abc     10.244.1.5    worker-1
# postgres-0     10.244.2.8    worker-2

# Test direct pod-to-pod (cross-node)
kubectl exec anihpj-abc -- curl -s --max-time 3 http://10.244.2.8:5432
# OR for TCP check
kubectl exec anihpj-abc -- nc -zv 10.244.2.8 5432
# 10.244.2.8 (10.244.2.8:5432) open

# Test same-node connectivity
kubectl exec anihpj-abc -- ping -c 2 10.244.1.12
# PING 10.244.1.12: 64 bytes, time=0.1ms  ← very fast (same node)

17.4 kube-proxy — Service Networking

kube-proxy runs on every node as a DaemonSet. Its job is to implement Service routing — when a pod sends traffic to a Service ClusterIP, kube-proxy's rules redirect that traffic to one of the Service's backend pods.

kube-proxy — How Service ClusterIP Routing Works
  anihpj pod calls: http://anihpj-svc:80   (ClusterIP: 10.96.45.2)

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Step 1: Pod sends packet to 10.96.45.2:80 (Service ClusterIP)  │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Step 2: Packet hits iptables/IPVS rules (set by kube-proxy)   │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  kube-proxy rules on this node:                          │   │
  │  │                                                          │   │
  │  │  10.96.45.2:80 → DNAT to one of:                        │   │
  │  │    ├── 10.244.1.5:8000  (anihpj-abc on worker-1) 33%   │   │
  │  │    ├── 10.244.2.3:8000  (anihpj-def on worker-2) 33%   │   │
  │  │    └── 10.244.3.7:8000  (anihpj-ghi on worker-3) 33%   │   │
  │  │                                                          │   │
  │  │  Random selection (iptables) or round-robin (IPVS)      │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Step 3: Packet destination rewritten to 10.244.2.3:8000        │
  │          → Normal pod-to-pod routing delivers it                │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Step 4: Response comes back, source NAT reverses               │
  │          Pod sees response from 10.96.45.2 (Service IP)         │
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                    │
  │  │  Key insight: 10.96.45.2 is VIRTUAL     │                    │
  │  │  No network interface has this IP!       │                    │
  │  │  It only exists in iptables/IPVS rules  │                    │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘                    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
kube-proxy Mode How It Works Performance When to Use
iptables Creates iptables NAT rules for each Service/Endpoint. Random backend selection. Good for < 1000 Services Default — most clusters
IPVS Uses Linux kernel IPVS module. Hash-based load balancing (round-robin, least connections, etc.). Excellent for 1000+ Services Large-scale production
nftables Uses nftables (successor to iptables). More efficient rule processing. Better than iptables Newer kernels (K8s 1.29+)
Inspecting kube-proxy 
# Check kube-proxy pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
# NAME              READY   STATUS    NODE
# kube-proxy-abc12  1/1     Running   worker-1
# kube-proxy-def34  1/1     Running   worker-2

# Check kube-proxy mode
kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-proxy-abc12 | grep "Using"
# I0401 Using iptables proxier    ← iptables mode

# View kube-proxy config
kubectl get configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system -o yaml | grep mode
# mode: ""   ← empty means iptables (default)

# View actual iptables rules for a Service (on a node)
# ssh worker-1
iptables -t nat -L KUBE-SERVICES | grep anihpj-svc
# KUBE-SVC-xxx  tcp  --  anywhere  10.96.45.2  tcp dpt:http
💡 CKA Tip: kube-proxy Crash = Services Stop Working

If kube-proxy pods are down, Services still resolve via DNS but traffic can't be routed to pods. Symptoms: DNS lookup works but curl http://service:port hangs. Always check kube-proxy if Service connectivity fails but DNS is fine.

kube-proxy Mode Decision Flow
                     ┌─────────────────────────┐
                     │  How many Services in    │
                     │  your cluster?            │
                     └────────┬────────────────┘
                              │
               ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
               │              │              │
          < 500         500 – 5000       > 5000
               │              │              │
               ▼              ▼              ▼
     ┌─────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
     │  iptables   │  │  Need LB   │  │    IPVS      │
     │  (default)  │  │  algos?    │  │  (required)  │
     │  ✅ Simple  │  └─────┬──────┘  │  ✅ O(1)     │
     │  ✅ Works   │    yes │ no      │  ✅ Scalable  │
     │  everywhere │        │         └──────────────┘
     └─────────────┘   ┌────┴────┐
                        │  IPVS  │
                        │ ✅ RR  │
                        │ ✅ LC  │   RR  = Round Robin
                        │ ✅ WRR │   LC  = Least Connections
                        │ ✅ SH  │   WRR = Weighted Round Robin
                        └────────┘   SH  = Source Hashing

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Running K8s 1.29+ on modern kernel?                 │
  │  Consider nftables mode — better rule processing    │
  │  Set: mode: "nftables" in kube-proxy ConfigMap      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Switching kube-proxy to IPVS Mode 
# Step 1: Edit the kube-proxy ConfigMap
kubectl edit configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system
# Change: mode: ""  →  mode: "ipvs"

# Step 2: Restart kube-proxy DaemonSet to pick up the change
kubectl rollout restart daemonset kube-proxy -n kube-system

# Step 3: Verify IPVS mode is active
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy | grep "Using"
# I0401 Using ipvs proxier    ← IPVS mode!

# Step 4: View IPVS rules (on a node via ssh)
ipvsadm -Ln
# TCP  10.96.45.2:80 rr         ← round-robin load balancing
#   -> 10.244.1.5:8000  Masq  1  0  0
#   -> 10.244.2.3:8000  Masq  1  0  0

17.5 Network Troubleshooting for Admins

The CKA exam frequently tests your ability to diagnose and fix networking issues. Here's your systematic troubleshooting playbook.

1
Check CNI

Are CNI pods running? kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico

2
Check kube-proxy

Is kube-proxy running? kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep proxy

3
Test Pod IP

Can you reach the pod directly? kubectl exec -- curl POD_IP:PORT

4
Test Service IP

Can you reach the Service? kubectl exec -- curl SVC_IP:PORT

5
Test DNS

Does name resolution work? kubectl exec -- nslookup svc-name

Symptom Likely Cause Diagnostic Command Fix
Nodes NotReady CNI not installed or crashing kubectl get pods -n kube-system Install/fix CNI plugin
Pod can't reach pod on other node CNI overlay broken, firewall blocking kubectl exec -- ping <pod-ip> Check CNI pods, node firewall rules
DNS works but Service unreachable kube-proxy down or misconfigured kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy Restart kube-proxy, check ConfigMap
Service returns no endpoints Selector doesn't match pod labels kubectl get endpoints svc-name Fix selector labels to match pods
nslookup fails CoreDNS pods down kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns Check CoreDNS pods and logs
Traffic blocked between pods NetworkPolicy denying traffic kubectl get networkpolicies -A Review/update NetworkPolicy rules
Network Troubleshooting Toolkit 
# 1. Quick connectivity test from a debug pod
kubectl run nettest --image=nicolaka/netshoot --rm -it -- bash

# Inside netshoot pod:
curl -s http://anihpj-svc:80          # Test Service by name
curl -s http://10.96.45.2:80          # Test Service by IP
ping -c 2 10.244.2.8                  # Test pod IP directly
nslookup anihpj-svc                   # Test DNS resolution
nslookup anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local  # Full FQDN
traceroute 10.244.2.8                 # Trace packet path

# 2. Check endpoints (are pods registered with the Service?)
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc
# NAME          ENDPOINTS                                    AGE
# anihpj-svc   10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000,10.244.3.7:8000   5m
# If ENDPOINTS is empty → selector doesn't match any pods!

# 3. Verify Service selector matches pod labels
kubectl get svc anihpj-svc -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector}'
# {"app":"anihpj"}
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
# Should show your pods — if empty, labels don't match!

# 4. Check if NetworkPolicies are blocking traffic
kubectl get networkpolicies -A
kubectl describe networkpolicy -n anihpj-prod

🔑 Chapter 17 Quick Reference

Action Command
Check CNI pluginls /etc/cni/net.d/
Check CNI podskubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico
Check kube-proxykubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
View pod IPskubectl get pods -o wide
View pod CIDRskubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.podCIDR}'
Test pod connectivitykubectl exec POD -- curl -s http://IP:PORT
Test DNSkubectl exec POD -- nslookup SVC-NAME
Check endpointskubectl get endpoints SVC-NAME
Debug networkingkubectl run nettest --image=nicolaka/netshoot --rm -it -- bash
Check NetworkPolicieskubectl get networkpolicies -A
🧪 CKA Practice Scenario: Network Debugging

Situation: A junior admin reports that the anihpj Django app can't connect to the postgres database. Pods are Running, but curl http://postgres-svc:5432 from the anihpj pod times out.

Step-by-Step Diagnosis 
# Step 1: Can DNS resolve the service name?
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nslookup postgres-svc
# ✅ Returns 10.96.88.5 — DNS is fine

# Step 2: Can we reach the pod directly by IP?
kubectl get pods -o wide | grep postgres
# postgres-0   10.244.2.8   worker-2
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nc -zv 10.244.2.8 5432
# ✅ open — pod-to-pod networking works

# Step 3: Can we reach the SERVICE IP?
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nc -zv 10.96.88.5 5432
# ❌ TIMEOUT — Service routing is broken!

# Step 4: Check kube-proxy
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
# kube-proxy-abc12  0/1  CrashLoopBackOff  ← FOUND IT!

# Step 5: Check kube-proxy logs
kubectl logs kube-proxy-abc12 -n kube-system
# "failed to read config: invalid mode 'ipvss'"
# Typo in config! mode should be "ipvs" not "ipvss"

# Fix: Edit the kube-proxy ConfigMap
kubectl edit configmap kube-proxy -n kube-system
# Fix the mode value, then restart kube-proxy pods
kubectl rollout restart daemonset kube-proxy -n kube-system

Chapter 18: Services Deep Dive

Services are the stable networking abstraction that makes Kubernetes applications accessible. While pods come and go with dynamic IPs, a Service provides a fixed endpoint — a virtual IP and DNS name — that automatically routes traffic to healthy pods. In this chapter you'll master every Service type, understand what happens under the hood, and learn how pods discover each other using our anihpj/jobpost Django stack throughout.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔗 Services 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ How Services work under the hood — Endpoints, selectors, and kube-proxy integration
  • ✅ ClusterIP — internal-only access (the default and most common type)
  • ✅ NodePort — expose a Service on every node's IP at a static port
  • ✅ LoadBalancer — cloud-provider external load balancers
  • ✅ ExternalName — CNAME-based redirection to external services
  • ✅ Headless Services — direct pod DNS without proxy
  • ✅ Service Discovery — DNS-based and environment-variable methods
🎓 CKA Relevance
Services & Networking 20%
Services Are Heavily Tested on the CKA

Expect 2-3 questions creating/debugging Services. Must-know imperative commands:

  • kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=ClusterIP
  • kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=NodePort
  • kubectl get svc and kubectl get endpoints — always check both
  • Debug: kubectl describe svc anihpj-svc — verify selector, endpoints, ports

18.1 How Services Work — Under the Hood

A Service is an API object that defines a logical set of pods (via a label selector) and a policy for accessing them. Here's what happens when you create a Service:

Service Creation — The Full Chain
  kubectl apply -f anihpj-svc.yaml
           │
           ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │    API Server        │  Stores Service object in etcd
  └─────────┬───────────┘
            │
            ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │  Endpoint Controller │  Watches pods matching selector
  │                      │  Creates Endpoints object:
  │                      │  anihpj-svc → [10.244.1.5:8000,
  │                      │                10.244.2.3:8000]
  └─────────┬───────────┘
            │
            ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │    kube-proxy        │  Watches Service + Endpoints
  │    (on every node)   │  Creates iptables/IPVS rules:
  │                      │  10.96.45.2:80 → DNAT → pod IPs
  └─────────┬───────────┘
            │
            ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │      CoreDNS         │  Creates DNS record:
  │                      │  anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
  │                      │  → 10.96.45.2
  └─────────────────────┘

Anatomy of a Service Object

🏷️ spec.selector
Label selector — determines which pods receive traffic. app: anihpj
📡 spec.ports
port = Service port (what clients use), targetPort = pod port (where app listens)
🔢 spec.clusterIP
Virtual IP assigned from Service CIDR (10.96.0.0/12). Immutable once created.
📋 Endpoints
Auto-populated list of pod IPs matching the selector. Updated on pod add/remove.
🌐 spec.type
ClusterIP (default), NodePort, LoadBalancer, or ExternalName
📖 Key Concept: Port vs TargetPort

port: 80 is what clients connect to on the Service IP. targetPort: 8000 is the container port where your app actually listens. These can be different! Your anihpj Django app listens on 8000, but clients access http://anihpj-svc:80.

18.2 ClusterIP Services

ClusterIP is the default and most common Service type. It assigns a virtual IP only reachable from within the cluster — pods and nodes can access it, but external clients cannot.

✅
When to Use
  • Internal microservice communication
  • Database access (postgres-svc)
  • Cache layer (redis-svc)
  • Backend APIs not exposed externally
❌
Limitations
  • Not accessible from outside the cluster
  • Cannot be reached from your laptop
  • Need NodePort/LoadBalancer/Ingress for external access
anihpj-clusterip-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    tier: web
spec:
  type: ClusterIP          # Default — can be omitted
  selector:
    app: anihpj             # Must match pod labels!
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 80              # Service port (clients connect here)
      targetPort: 8000      # Container port (Django listens here)
    - name: admin
      protocol: TCP
      port: 8080            # Second port — Django admin
      targetPort: 8000
Imperative ClusterIP Creation & Verification 
# Create ClusterIP service (imperative — fast for CKA)
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --name=anihpj-svc \
  --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=ClusterIP

# Verify Service created
kubectl get svc anihpj-svc
# NAME         TYPE        CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)   AGE
# anihpj-svc  ClusterIP   10.96.45.2    <none>        80/TCP    5s

# Check endpoints — are pods registered?
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc
# NAME         ENDPOINTS                        AGE
# anihpj-svc  10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000  5s

# Test from another pod
kubectl exec -it debug-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc:80
# HTTP 200 — Django responds!
Feature Default Behavior Configuration When to Use
Session Affinity None — random pod each request spec.sessionAffinity: ClientIP Sticky sessions, WebSocket apps
Session Timeout 10800s (3 hours) spec.sessionAffinityConfig.clientIP.timeoutSeconds: 3600 Adjust affinity window
Multi-Port Single port Multiple entries in spec.ports[] (each needs a name) App + metrics, HTTP + HTTPS
Session Affinity — Sticky Sessions 
# All requests from the same client IP go to the same pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sticky
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  sessionAffinity: ClientIP      # ← Enable sticky sessions
  sessionAffinityConfig:
    clientIP:
      timeoutSeconds: 3600       # 1 hour affinity window
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000

18.3 NodePort Services

NodePort exposes the Service on a static port on every node's IP in the cluster. External clients can access the Service via <NodeIP>:<NodePort>.

NodePort — How External Traffic Reaches Pods
  External client: http://192.168.1.10:30080

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                 │
  │  External Request → ANY node at port 30080                     │
  │                                                                 │
  │  ┌── Node 1 ──────────────┐    ┌── Node 2 ──────────────┐     │
  │  │  192.168.1.10:30080    │    │  192.168.1.11:30080    │     │
  │  │         │              │    │         │              │     │
  │  │         ▼ kube-proxy   │    │         ▼ kube-proxy   │     │
  │  │  ┌─────────────────┐   │    │  ┌─────────────────┐   │     │
  │  │  │ iptables/IPVS   │   │    │  │ iptables/IPVS   │   │     │
  │  │  │ DNAT to pod     │   │    │  │ DNAT to pod     │   │     │
  │  │  └────────┬────────┘   │    │  └────────┬────────┘   │     │
  │  │           │            │    │           │            │     │
  │  │  ┌────────▼─────────┐  │    │  ┌────────▼─────────┐  │     │
  │  │  │ anihpj-pod-abc   │  │    │  │ anihpj-pod-def   │  │     │
  │  │  │ 10.244.1.5:8000  │  │    │  │ 10.244.2.3:8000  │  │     │
  │  │  └──────────────────┘  │    │  └──────────────────┘  │     │
  │  └────────────────────────┘    └────────────────────────┘     │
  │                                                                 │
  │  Note: Request to Node 1:30080 can be routed to pod on Node 2  │
  │  NodePort range: 30000 - 32767 (configurable in API server)    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-nodeport-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-nodeport
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 80              # ClusterIP port (internal)
      targetPort: 8000      # Container port
      nodePort: 30080       # External port on every node
                            # Range: 30000-32767
                            # Omit to auto-assign
Working with NodePort Services 
# Create NodePort (imperative)
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --name=anihpj-nodeport \
  --port=80 --target-port=8000 --type=NodePort

# Service gets BOTH a ClusterIP AND a NodePort
kubectl get svc anihpj-nodeport
# NAME              TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    PORT(S)        AGE
# anihpj-nodeport  NodePort   10.96.80.12   80:31245/TCP   5s
#                                            ↑    ↑
#                                            |    └ NodePort (auto-assigned)
#                                            └ ClusterIP port

# Access from outside the cluster
curl http://192.168.1.10:31245     # via Node 1
curl http://192.168.1.11:31245     # via Node 2 — same result!
⚠️ NodePort Limitations
  • Only 1 Service per port (limited range 30000-32767)
  • No TLS termination — clients use HTTP
  • Clients must know node IPs — no load balancing across nodes
  • Not suitable for production — use LoadBalancer or Ingress instead
externalTrafficPolicy Behavior Client Source IP Trade-off
Cluster (default) Traffic can route to pods on any node — extra hop if pod is on a different node ❌ SNAT'd — original IP lost Better load distribution, but loses client IP
Local Traffic only routes to pods on the same node it arrived on ✅ Preserved — see real client IP May have uneven load if pods aren't on every node
Preserving Client Source IP 
# Important for logging, rate limiting, geo-routing
spec:
  type: NodePort
  externalTrafficPolicy: Local    # ← Preserves client IP
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
      nodePort: 30080

18.4 LoadBalancer Services

LoadBalancer is the standard way to expose Services to the internet in cloud environments (AWS, GCP, Azure). It provisions an actual external load balancer that distributes traffic across your nodes.

LoadBalancer Service — Cloud Integration
  Internet user: http://203.0.113.50:80

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                    │
  │  │  Cloud Load Balancer (AWS ELB / GCP LB) │                    │
  │  │  External IP: 203.0.113.50              │                    │
  │  │  Provisioned automatically by K8s       │                    │
  │  └────────────┬────────────────┬───────────┘                    │
  │               │                │                                 │
  │     ┌─────────▼────┐    ┌─────▼────────┐                       │
  │     │ Node 1:30080 │    │ Node 2:30080 │   (NodePort used      │
  │     │ (kube-proxy) │    │ (kube-proxy) │    under the hood)    │
  │     └──────┬───────┘    └────┬─────────┘                       │
  │            │                 │                                   │
  │     ┌──────▼──────────┐  ┌──▼──────────────┐                   │
  │     │ anihpj pod-abc  │  │ anihpj pod-def  │                   │
  │     │ 10.244.1.5:8000 │  │ 10.244.2.3:8000 │                   │
  │     └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘                   │
  │                                                                  │
  │  LoadBalancer = ClusterIP + NodePort + External LB              │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-loadbalancer-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-lb
  annotations:
    # Cloud-specific annotations
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb   # AWS NLB
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
  # Optional: restrict external access by IP
  loadBalancerSourceRanges:
    - 10.0.0.0/8
    - 203.0.113.0/24
Working with LoadBalancer Services 
# Create LoadBalancer service
kubectl apply -f anihpj-loadbalancer-svc.yaml

# Check — EXTERNAL-IP may show <pending> initially
kubectl get svc anihpj-lb -w
# NAME        TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP     PORT(S)        AGE
# anihpj-lb  LoadBalancer   10.96.90.1    <pending>       80:31890/TCP   5s
# anihpj-lb  LoadBalancer   10.96.90.1    203.0.113.50    80:31890/TCP   30s

# Access from the internet
curl http://203.0.113.50:80
# Django anihpj responds!

# On bare metal without cloud LB — use MetalLB
# EXTERNAL-IP stays <pending> forever without a LB controller
💡 Bare Metal: Use MetalLB

On-premises clusters have no cloud LB provider. MetalLB fills this gap by assigning real external IPs from a configured pool. Often used in CKA lab environments and homelab setups.

EndpointSlices — The Modern Endpoints

📋 Endpoints (legacy)
Single object listing ALL pod IPs for a Service. Becomes huge in large clusters — every pod change updates the entire object.
📦 EndpointSlices (v1)
Splits endpoints into chunks of ~100 pods each. Only the affected slice is updated — much more efficient at scale.
🔍 View them
kubectl get endpointslices -l kubernetes.io/service-name=anihpj-svc
✅ Default since
Kubernetes 1.21+. Automatically created for every Service. Endpoints still exist for backward compatibility.

18.5 ExternalName Services

ExternalName is a special Service type that maps a Service name to an external DNS name via a CNAME record. No proxying, no ClusterIP — just DNS redirection.

🔀
How It Works

When a pod resolves external-db, CoreDNS returns a CNAME pointing to the external hostname. Traffic goes directly to the external endpoint — kube-proxy is not involved.

📋
Use Cases
  • Point to an external database (RDS, Cloud SQL)
  • Migrate from external to internal service gradually
  • Abstract external dependencies behind K8s DNS
external-db-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: external-db
  namespace: default
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: mydb.example.com    # External DNS name
  # No selector!
  # No ClusterIP!
  # No ports!
Using ExternalName Services 
# From inside a pod:
nslookup external-db
# external-db.default.svc.cluster.local → CNAME → mydb.example.com

# Django settings.py can just use:
# DATABASES = { 'HOST': 'external-db' }
# If you later migrate to an internal postgres pod, just
# delete the ExternalName service and create a ClusterIP service
# with the same name — zero code changes!

18.6 Headless Services

A Headless Service is a ClusterIP Service with clusterIP: None. Instead of a single virtual IP, DNS resolution returns the individual pod IPs directly. This is essential for StatefulSets where clients need to reach specific pods.

Headless vs Normal Service — DNS Comparison
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                    │
  │  NORMAL Service (ClusterIP: 10.96.45.2)                          │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  nslookup anihpj-svc                                     │    │
  │  │  → 10.96.45.2  (single ClusterIP — kube-proxy routes)   │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                    │
  │  HEADLESS Service (clusterIP: None)                               │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  nslookup postgres-headless                               │    │
  │  │  → 10.244.1.15  (postgres-0)                             │    │
  │  │  → 10.244.2.18  (postgres-1)                             │    │
  │  │  → 10.244.3.22  (postgres-2)                             │    │
  │  │                                                           │    │
  │  │  PLUS individual pod DNS:                                 │    │
  │  │  postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local  │    │
  │  │  → 10.244.1.15                                           │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                    │
  │  Use headless when you need to reach SPECIFIC pods               │
  │  (StatefulSets, database replicas, leader election)              │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
postgres-headless-svc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None            # ← This makes it headless!
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
    - name: postgres
      port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
---
# Used with StatefulSet
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-headless    # ← Must reference headless service
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5432
Using Headless Service DNS 
# DNS resolves to ALL pod IPs
kubectl exec debug-pod -- nslookup postgres-headless
# Name: postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.15
# Address: 10.244.2.18
# Address: 10.244.3.22

# Access specific pod by ordinal name
kubectl exec debug-pod -- nslookup postgres-0.postgres-headless
# Address: 10.244.1.15    ← always the primary!

# Django connects to primary:
# DATABASES = { 'HOST': 'postgres-0.postgres-headless' }

18.7 Service Discovery — How Pods Find Services

Kubernetes provides two built-in methods for pods to discover Services. DNS is the primary method used in practice.

Method How It Works Pros Cons
DNS (Primary) CoreDNS creates records: svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local Always current, discoverable by name, works across namespaces Requires CoreDNS running
Environment Variables kubelet injects SVC_NAME_SERVICE_HOST and SVC_NAME_SERVICE_PORT into pods Works without DNS, simple Only services created BEFORE the pod; ordering-dependent
Service Discovery in Action 
# Method 1: DNS — the recommended way
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nslookup anihpj-svc
# Name:   anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.45.2

# Short names work within the same namespace:
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc:80       # ✅ same namespace
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc.prod:80  # ✅ "prod" namespace
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local:80  # ✅ FQDN

# Method 2: Environment variables
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- env | grep ANIHPJ_SVC
# ANIHPJ_SVC_SERVICE_HOST=10.96.45.2
# ANIHPJ_SVC_SERVICE_PORT=80
# ANIHPJ_SVC_SERVICE_PORT_HTTP=80
DNS Name Resolution Hierarchy
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DNS Resolution for Services                                     │
  │                                                                   │
  │  Short name:     anihpj-svc                                      │
  │                  ↓ (search domain appended)                      │
  │  Within NS:      anihpj-svc.default                              │
  │                  ↓                                                │
  │  Cluster FQDN:   anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local           │
  │                  ↓                                                │
  │  Resolves to:    10.96.45.2 (ClusterIP)                          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  Cross-namespace: anihpj-svc.prod                                │
  │                   ↓                                               │
  │  Cluster FQDN:    anihpj-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local             │
  │                   ↓                                               │
  │  Resolves to:     10.96.88.5 (prod namespace Service)            │
  │                                                                   │
  │  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  Pod DNS resolv.conf (auto-configured):                          │
  │  nameserver 10.96.0.10  (CoreDNS ClusterIP)                     │
  │  search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local│
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔑 Chapter 18 Quick Reference

Service Type ClusterIP External Access Use Case
ClusterIP✅ Virtual IP❌ Internal onlyPod-to-pod communication
NodePort✅ Virtual IP✅ via NodeIP:PortDev/test, simple external access
LoadBalancer✅ Virtual IP✅ via cloud LBProduction external access
ExternalName❌ NoneN/A (CNAME only)External service aliasing
Headless❌ None❌ Internal onlyStatefulSets, direct pod access
🧪 CKA Practice Scenario: Service Debugging

Situation: You've deployed the anihpj app and created a Service, but kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc shows <none>. The app pods are Running.

Debug: Empty Endpoints 
# Step 1: Check what the Service selector is looking for
kubectl get svc anihpj-svc -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector}'
# {"app":"anihpj-web"}     ← The selector value

# Step 2: Check what labels the pods actually have
kubectl get pods --show-labels | grep anihpj
# anihpj-abc  Running  app=anihpj,tier=web
#                      ↑ Label is "anihpj" not "anihpj-web"!

# Step 3: Fix — either update the Service selector or the pod labels
# Option A: Patch the Service selector
kubectl patch svc anihpj-svc -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"anihpj"}}}'

# Step 4: Verify endpoints are now populated
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc
# ENDPOINTS: 10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000  ✅ Fixed!

Chapter 19: DNS and CoreDNS

DNS is the service discovery backbone of Kubernetes. Every time your anihpj pod connects to postgres-svc, a DNS query resolves that name to a ClusterIP. CoreDNS is the cluster DNS server that handles all of this — and knowing how to configure and troubleshoot it is a core CKA skill.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 🔍 DNS 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ How Kubernetes DNS works end-to-end — from pod query to CoreDNS response
  • ✅ CoreDNS architecture, Corefile configuration, and plugins
  • ✅ Every type of DNS record Kubernetes creates (A, AAAA, SRV, CNAME)
  • ✅ Systematic DNS troubleshooting — the #1 exam debugging skill
🎓 CKA Relevance
Services & Networking 20% Troubleshooting 30%
DNS Questions Appear on Nearly Every CKA Exam

CoreDNS troubleshooting is one of the most tested topics. Memorize these:

  • CoreDNS pods: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
  • CoreDNS ConfigMap: kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
  • Test DNS: kubectl exec POD -- nslookup SERVICE_NAME
  • Full FQDN format: svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local

19.1 How Kubernetes DNS Works

Every pod in Kubernetes is automatically configured to use the cluster DNS server (CoreDNS). Here's the complete resolution flow:

DNS Resolution Flow — End to End
  anihpj pod runs: curl http://postgres-svc:5432

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                      │
  │  Step 1: Pod checks /etc/resolv.conf (auto-configured by kubelet)   │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  nameserver 10.96.0.10                                        │ │
  │  │  search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local           │ │
  │  │         cluster.local                                          │ │
  │  │  ndots:5                                                       │ │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                                                      │
  │  Step 2: "postgres-svc" has fewer than 5 dots → search path used   │
  │          → Queries: postgres-svc.default.svc.cluster.local          │
  │                                                                      │
  │  Step 3: Query sent to 10.96.0.10 (CoreDNS ClusterIP)             │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐                    │
  │  │  CoreDNS (kube-system namespace)           │                    │
  │  │  Pod: coredns-abc-123                      │                    │
  │  │  ClusterIP: 10.96.0.10 (kube-dns Service) │                    │
  │  │                                            │                    │
  │  │  Looks up Service "postgres-svc" in        │                    │
  │  │  namespace "default"                        │                    │
  │  │  → Found!  ClusterIP: 10.96.88.5          │                    │
  │  └──────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘                    │
  │                     │                                                │
  │  Step 4: Returns A record: 10.96.88.5                              │
  │                                                                      │
  │  Step 5: Pod connects to 10.96.88.5:5432                           │
  │          → kube-proxy DNAT → actual postgres pod IP                │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Pod's /etc/resolv.conf — Explained

🔢 nameserver
10.96.0.10 — The ClusterIP of the kube-dns Service in kube-system (points to CoreDNS pods)
🔍 search domains
default.svc.cluster.local — Allows short names like postgres-svc to resolve within the namespace
🔢 ndots:5
If the name has fewer than 5 dots, append search domains first. postgres-svc (0 dots) → search path used. api.example.com (2 dots) → also search path first!
📖 ndots:5 Performance Consideration

The default ndots:5 means external domains like api.example.com generate 4-5 DNS queries before the real one succeeds. For performance-sensitive apps, you can override this in the pod spec with dnsConfig: { options: [{ name: ndots, value: "2" }] }.

dnsPolicy resolv.conf Source Can Resolve Services? When Used
ClusterFirst (default) CoreDNS (10.96.0.10)
+ search domains		 ✅ Yes Standard pods — always use this
Default Node's /etc/resolv.conf ❌ No — can't see Services Rarely used; hostNetwork pods
ClusterFirstWithHostNet CoreDNS (10.96.0.10) ✅ Yes Pods with hostNetwork: true that need Service DNS
None Must supply dnsConfig manually Depends on config Custom DNS (e.g., external resolver only)
Custom DNS Configuration in Pod Spec 
# Override DNS settings for a specific pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-custom-dns
spec:
  dnsPolicy: None           # ← Take full control of DNS
  dnsConfig:
    nameservers:
      - 10.96.0.10           # CoreDNS
      - 8.8.8.8              # Google DNS as fallback
    searches:
      - default.svc.cluster.local
      - svc.cluster.local
    options:
      - name: ndots
        value: "2"           # ← Fewer search attempts for external domains
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:latest

19.2 CoreDNS Configuration

CoreDNS is deployed as a Deployment in the kube-system namespace, typically with 2 replicas for high availability. Its configuration lives in a ConfigMap called coredns.

CoreDNS Architecture in Kubernetes
  ┌──────────────────── kube-system namespace ────────────────────┐
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  Deployment: coredns (replicas: 2)                       │ │
  │  │  ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐              │ │
  │  │  │ coredns-abc-111 │   │ coredns-abc-222 │              │ │
  │  │  │ (Pod 1)         │   │ (Pod 2)         │              │ │
  │  │  └────────┬────────┘   └────────┬────────┘              │ │
  │  └───────────┼─────────────────────┼────────────────────────┘ │
  │              │                     │                           │
  │  ┌───────────▼─────────────────────▼────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  Service: kube-dns (ClusterIP: 10.96.0.10)              │ │
  │  │  Ports: 53/UDP, 53/TCP, 9153/TCP (metrics)              │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  ConfigMap: coredns                                      │  │
  │  │  Contains: Corefile (plugin configuration)               │  │
  │  │  Mounted at: /etc/coredns/Corefile                       │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The Default Corefile — Annotated 
# kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: coredns
  namespace: kube-system
data:
  Corefile: |
    .:53 {                          # Listen on port 53 for all zones
        errors                       # Log errors to stdout
        health {                     # Health check endpoint on :8080
            lameduck 5s              # Wait 5s before shutting down
        }
        ready                        # Readiness probe on :8181
        kubernetes cluster.local in-addr.arpa ip6.arpa {
            pods insecure            # Enable pod DNS records
            fallthrough in-addr.arpa ip6.arpa
            ttl 30                   # Cache TTL: 30 seconds
        }
        prometheus :9153             # Metrics endpoint for monitoring
        forward . /etc/resolv.conf {  # Forward external queries to
            max_concurrent 1000       # upstream DNS (node's resolv.conf)
        }
        cache 30                     # Cache all responses for 30s
        loop                         # Detect and stop DNS loops
        reload                       # Auto-reload Corefile on change
        loadbalance                  # Round-robin A/AAAA records
    }
Common CoreDNS Customizations 
# Add a custom DNS entry (stub domain)
# Edit the ConfigMap to forward *.example.com to a specific DNS server
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system

# Add this block inside the Corefile:
#   example.com:53 {
#       forward . 10.0.0.1
#   }

# Add a static host entry (hosts plugin)
# Add inside the main server block, before kubernetes:
#   hosts {
#       10.10.10.50 mydb.internal
#       fallthrough
#   }

# After editing — CoreDNS auto-reloads (reload plugin)
# Verify: check CoreDNS logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --tail=20
⚠️ CKA Exam: Never Delete the CoreDNS ConfigMap

If you're asked to modify DNS configuration, use kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system. The reload plugin means changes take effect automatically within 30 seconds. Do NOT restart CoreDNS pods unless explicitly required — they auto-reload.

CoreDNS Plugin Purpose Key Config
kubernetes Resolves Service and Pod DNS names from the K8s API kubernetes cluster.local
forward Forwards non-cluster queries to upstream DNS servers forward . /etc/resolv.conf or forward . 8.8.8.8
cache Caches DNS responses to reduce API server load cache 30 (seconds)
loop Detects DNS forwarding loops and stops CoreDNS loop (no arguments)
reload Auto-reloads Corefile when ConfigMap changes reload (default: every 30s)
hosts Serves static DNS entries (like /etc/hosts) hosts { 10.0.0.1 mydb.local }
errors Logs DNS errors to stdout (visible via kubectl logs) errors
health HTTP health check endpoint on port 8080 health { lameduck 5s }
ready Readiness probe endpoint on port 8181 ready
prometheus Exposes metrics for monitoring on port 9153 prometheus :9153

19.3 DNS Records in Kubernetes

Kubernetes automatically creates DNS records for Services and (optionally) Pods. Understanding these record formats is essential for cross-namespace communication.

Record Type DNS Name Format Resolves To Example
A Record (Service) svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local ClusterIP anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local → 10.96.45.2
A Record (Headless) svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local All Pod IPs postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local → 10.244.1.15, 10.244.2.18
A Record (Pod via Headless) pod-name.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local Specific Pod IP postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local → 10.244.1.15
SRV Record _port-name._protocol.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local Port + hostname _http._tcp.anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local → 0 10 80 ...
CNAME (ExternalName) svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local External hostname external-db.default.svc.cluster.local → mydb.example.com
A Record (Pod direct) pod-ip-dashed.namespace.pod.cluster.local Pod IP 10-244-1-5.default.pod.cluster.local → 10.244.1.5
Exploring All DNS Record Types 
# Launch a DNS debug pod
kubectl run dnstest --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it -- sh

# A record — Service ClusterIP
nslookup anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.45.2

# A records — Headless Service (returns all pod IPs)
nslookup postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.15
# Address: 10.244.2.18

# A record — specific pod via headless service
nslookup postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.15

# SRV record — discover port and protocol
nslookup -type=SRV _http._tcp.anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
# service = 0 10 80 anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local

# Pod DNS (IP dashes)
nslookup 10-244-1-5.default.pod.cluster.local
# Address: 10.244.1.5

# Cross-namespace resolution
nslookup anihpj-svc.prod.svc.cluster.local
# Address: 10.96.88.5  (Service in "prod" namespace)
💡 Short Names vs FQDN

Within the same namespace, use short names: anihpj-svc or anihpj-svc:80. Across namespaces, include the namespace: anihpj-svc.prod. Full FQDNs are rarely needed in practice but may appear on the CKA exam.

19.4 Troubleshooting DNS Issues

DNS failures are one of the most common Kubernetes problems — and one of the most tested on the CKA. Here's a systematic approach to diagnose and fix DNS issues.

1
Is CoreDNS Running?

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns — both pods should be Running

2
Is the kube-dns Service Up?

kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system — should have ClusterIP 10.96.0.10

3
Can Pods Reach CoreDNS?

kubectl exec POD -- nslookup kubernetes.default — tests basic DNS connectivity

4
Check CoreDNS Logs

kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns — look for errors

5
Check Corefile Config

kubectl get cm coredns -n kube-system -o yaml — verify configuration is correct

DNS Symptom Likely Cause Diagnostic Fix
nslookup times out CoreDNS pods not running kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns Check CoreDNS deployment, restart pods
SERVFAIL response Corefile misconfiguration kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns Fix Corefile syntax in ConfigMap
NXDOMAIN — name not found Service doesn't exist or wrong namespace kubectl get svc -A | grep svc-name Create the Service or use correct namespace prefix
External domains fail forward plugin pointing to wrong upstream Check forward . /etc/resolv.conf in Corefile Fix upstream DNS (e.g., forward . 8.8.8.8)
DNS works in some pods, not others Pod's dnsPolicy is set to Default kubectl get pod POD -o yaml | grep dnsPolicy Change to dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst (the default)
DNS loop detected Node's /etc/resolv.conf points to a local DNS that forwards back to CoreDNS CoreDNS logs: "Loop ... detected" Change forward . /etc/resolv.conf to forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4
DNS Troubleshooting Playbook 
# Step 1: Check CoreDNS pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# NAME                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# coredns-abc-111          1/1     Running   0          5d
# coredns-abc-222          1/1     Running   0          5d

# Step 2: Check kube-dns Service
kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system
# NAME       TYPE        CLUSTER-IP   PORT(S)
# kube-dns   ClusterIP   10.96.0.10   53/UDP,53/TCP,9153/TCP

# Step 3: Test DNS from a debug pod
kubectl run dnsdiag --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it -- sh
nslookup kubernetes.default     # Should resolve to API server ClusterIP
nslookup anihpj-svc             # Should resolve to Service ClusterIP
nslookup google.com             # Should resolve (tests external forwarding)

# Step 4: Check CoreDNS logs for errors
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --tail=50
# Look for: "SERVFAIL", "i/o timeout", "Loop detected"

# Step 5: Verify pod resolv.conf
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 10.96.0.10
# search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local

# Step 6: Check the Corefile
kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml

🔑 Chapter 19 Quick Reference

Action Command
Check CoreDNS podskubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
Check kube-dns Servicekubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system
Test DNS resolutionkubectl exec POD -- nslookup SERVICE
View CoreDNS configkubectl get cm coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
Edit CoreDNS configkubectl edit cm coredns -n kube-system
CoreDNS logskubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
Pod DNS configkubectl exec POD -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
Service FQDN formatsvc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local
Pod FQDN (headless)pod-name.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local
🧪 CKA Practice Scenario: DNS Broken After Cluster Upgrade

Situation: After upgrading to K8s 1.29, pods report nslookup: can't resolve 'anihpj-svc'. The CoreDNS pods are stuck in CrashLoopBackOff.

Step-by-Step DNS Recovery 
# Step 1: Confirm CoreDNS is crashing
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# coredns-abc-111  0/1  CrashLoopBackOff  5 (2m ago)
# coredns-abc-222  0/1  CrashLoopBackOff  5 (2m ago)

# Step 2: Check logs for the error
kubectl logs -n kube-system coredns-abc-111
# "Loop (127.0.0.1:43522 -> :53) detected for zone '.'"
# ← DNS forwarding loop!

# Step 3: The cause — node's /etc/resolv.conf points to
# 127.0.0.53 (systemd-resolved), which forwards back to CoreDNS
# This creates a loop!

# Step 4: Fix the Corefile — replace /etc/resolv.conf with
# explicit upstream DNS servers
kubectl edit configmap coredns -n kube-system
# Change:  forward . /etc/resolv.conf
# To:      forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4

# Step 5: Wait for auto-reload (30s) or delete pods to force restart
kubectl delete pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Step 6: Verify DNS is working again
kubectl run dnscheck --image=busybox --rm -it -- nslookup anihpj-svc
# Address: 10.96.45.2  ✅ Fixed!

Chapter 20: Ingress and Network Policies

While Services handle internal routing, Ingress manages external HTTP/HTTPS traffic with path-based routing, TLS termination, and virtual hosts. Network Policies act as a firewall for pod-to-pod traffic — controlling which pods can talk to each other. Together, these are the most heavily tested networking topics on the CKA exam.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2 hours 🛡️ Network Security 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Ingress — HTTP/HTTPS routing with path and host rules
  • ✅ Setting up an Ingress Controller (NGINX) in a cluster
  • ✅ Network Policies — the pod-level firewall for zero-trust networking
  • ✅ Policy types: Ingress (incoming), Egress (outgoing), and combined
  • ✅ Real-world YAML patterns for common Network Policy scenarios
  • ✅ Testing and verifying Network Policy enforcement
🎓 CKA Relevance
Services & Networking 20%
Ingress + NetworkPolicy = High-Value CKA Points

These are among the highest-weighted questions on the CKA. Tips:

  • Know the Ingress YAML by heart — the exam won't have auto-complete for all fields
  • NetworkPolicy: remember that empty podSelector {} selects ALL pods, and an empty ingress: [{}] allows ALL traffic
  • If asked to "deny all traffic" — apply a NetworkPolicy with podSelector: {} and policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress] with empty rules
  • An Ingress Controller must be running for Ingress resources to work

20.1 Ingress — HTTP/HTTPS Routing

An Ingress is an API object that manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to Services. It provides path-based routing, host-based routing, and TLS termination — all features that NodePort/LoadBalancer Services lack.

Ingress — How External HTTP Traffic Is Routed
  Internet user: https://anihpj.example.com/api/jobs/
                 https://anihpj.example.com/admin/

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
  │  │  Ingress Controller (NGINX / Traefik / HAProxy)      │       │
  │  │  Deployed as a pod, exposed via LoadBalancer/NodePort │       │
  │  │  Reads Ingress rules from API server                 │       │
  │  └───────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘       │
  │                      │                                           │
  │          ┌───────────┼────────────┐                             │
  │          │           │            │                              │
  │   path: /api/*  path: /admin/*  path: /static/*                │
  │          │           │            │                              │
  │   ┌──────▼─────┐ ┌──▼────────┐ ┌─▼───────────┐                │
  │   │ anihpj-svc │ │ admin-svc │ │  static-svc │                │
  │   │ port: 80   │ │ port: 80  │ │  port: 80   │                │
  │   └──────┬─────┘ └──┬────────┘ └─┬───────────┘                │
  │          │           │            │                              │
  │   ┌──────▼─────┐ ┌──▼────────┐ ┌─▼───────────┐                │
  │   │ anihpj     │ │ anihpj    │ │  nginx      │                │
  │   │ pods       │ │ pods      │ │  pods       │                │
  │   └────────────┘ └───────────┘ └─────────────┘                │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Benefits over NodePort/LoadBalancer:                           │
  │  ✅ Single external IP for multiple services                   │
  │  ✅ Path-based and host-based routing                          │
  │  ✅ TLS termination at the edge                                │
  │  ✅ Centralized SSL certificate management                     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-ingress.yaml 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: default
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx         # Which Ingress Controller to use
  tls:                             # TLS termination
    - hosts:
        - anihpj.example.com
      secretName: anihpj-tls       # TLS cert stored in a Secret
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.example.com     # Host-based routing
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /api              # Path-based routing
            pathType: Prefix        # Prefix or Exact
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc   # Route to this Service
                port:
                  number: 80
          - path: /admin
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
