                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 80
    - host: docs.example.com       # Different host → different Service
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: docs-svc
                port:
                  number: 80
pathType Match Behavior Example
Prefix Matches URL path prefix by / segments /api matches /api, /api/, /api/jobs
Exact Exact URL path match (case-sensitive) /api matches only /api, NOT /api/
ImplementationSpecific Depends on the IngressClass / controller Behavior varies — avoid on exam
Working with Ingress Resources 
# Apply the Ingress
kubectl apply -f anihpj-ingress.yaml

# Check Ingress — look for ADDRESS (external IP)
kubectl get ingress
# NAME             CLASS   HOSTS                  ADDRESS        PORTS     AGE
# anihpj-ingress  nginx   anihpj.example.com     203.0.113.50   80, 443   5s

# Describe for detailed routing info
kubectl describe ingress anihpj-ingress
# Rules:
#   Host                  Path  Backends
#   ----                  ----  --------
#   anihpj.example.com
#                         /api    anihpj-svc:80 (10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000)
#                         /admin  anihpj-svc:80 (10.244.1.5:8000,10.244.2.3:8000)

# Create TLS Secret for HTTPS
kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
  --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key -n default
TLS Termination at Ingress — HTTPS Flow
  Browser: https://anihpj.example.com/api/jobs/

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────┐     HTTPS (443)      ┌──────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  Client   │ ═══════════════════▶ │  Ingress Controller  │    │
  │  │ (Browser) │    TLS encrypted     │  (NGINX pod)         │    │
  │  └──────────┘                       │                      │    │
  │                                     │  1. TLS handshake    │    │
  │                                     │  2. Reads TLS cert   │    │
  │                                     │     from Secret      │    │
  │                                     │     "anihpj-tls"     │    │
  │                                     │  3. Decrypts HTTPS   │    │
  │                                     │  4. Reads path /api  │    │
  │                                     │  5. Routes to        │    │
  │                                     │     anihpj-svc:80    │    │
  │                                     └──────────┬───────────┘    │
  │                                                │                 │
  │                                     HTTP (80) — unencrypted     │
  │                                                │                 │
  │                                     ┌──────────▼───────────┐    │
  │                                     │  anihpj pod          │    │
  │                                     │  :8000 (Django)      │    │
  │                                     └──────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                  │
  │  ⚡ TLS terminated at Ingress — backend pods use plain HTTP    │
  │  🔐 Certificate stored as K8s Secret (type: kubernetes.io/tls) │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Default Backend & 404 Handling

If no Ingress rule matches the request path, the controller returns a 404. You can configure a default backend in the Ingress spec to serve a custom error page: spec.defaultBackend.service.name: default-http-backend.

20.2 Ingress Controller Setup

An Ingress resource is just configuration — it does nothing without an Ingress Controller. The controller is the actual reverse proxy (NGINX, Traefik, HAProxy) that reads Ingress rules and routes traffic.

📦
Ingress Resource

The rules — path routing, TLS config, host rules. Just a YAML API object. Does nothing by itself.

⚙️
Ingress Controller

The implementation — NGINX/Traefik pod that reads rules and configures the reverse proxy. Must be installed separately.

Ingress Controller Provider Features Best For
NGINX Ingress Kubernetes community Path/host routing, TLS, rate limiting, auth CKA exam, most clusters
Traefik Traefik Labs Auto-discovery, Let's Encrypt, middleware Dynamic environments
HAProxy HAProxy Technologies High performance, TCP/HTTP load balancing High-traffic production
AWS ALB AWS AWS Application Load Balancer integration EKS clusters
Installing NGINX Ingress Controller 
# Install NGINX Ingress Controller (bare metal / kubeadm)
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/controller-v1.10.0/deploy/static/provider/baremetal/deploy.yaml

# Verify the controller is running
kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
# NAME                                       READY   STATUS
# ingress-nginx-controller-abc12-xyz         1/1     Running

# Check the IngressClass
kubectl get ingressclass
# NAME    CONTROLLER                           PARAMETERS   AGE
# nginx   k8s.io/ingress-nginx                 <none>       30s

# Verify the controller Service
kubectl get svc -n ingress-nginx
# NAME                        TYPE       EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)
# ingress-nginx-controller    NodePort   <none>        80:31080/TCP,443:31443/TCP
⚠️ CKA Exam: Ingress Controller Must Be Running

If the exam asks you to create an Ingress resource and it's not working, first check if an Ingress Controller is installed: kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx. An Ingress resource without a controller does nothing — the ADDRESS field will be empty.

20.3 Network Policies — Firewall for Pods

By default, Kubernetes allows all pods to communicate with all other pods — no restrictions. Network Policies let you define fine-grained rules controlling which pods can send traffic to which other pods, implementing a zero-trust network.

Default Behavior vs Network Policy Applied
  ┌──────────────────────── DEFAULT (No NetworkPolicy) ──────────────┐
  │                                                                   │
  │  anihpj ─────────→ postgres  ✅                                  │
  │  anihpj ─────────→ redis     ✅                                  │
  │  random-pod ─────→ postgres  ✅  ← anyone can access DB!        │
  │  random-pod ─────→ anihpj   ✅  ← no isolation at all          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  EVERYTHING is allowed. Any pod talks to any pod.                │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌──────────────── WITH NetworkPolicy on postgres ──────────────────┐
  │                                                                   │
  │  NetworkPolicy: "only pods with app=anihpj can reach postgres"  │
  │                                                                   │
  │  anihpj ─────────→ postgres  ✅  (label matches)                │
  │  random-pod ─────→ postgres  ❌  BLOCKED                        │
  │  redis ──────────→ postgres  ❌  BLOCKED                        │
  │                                                                   │
  │  anihpj ─────────→ redis     ✅  (no policy on redis)           │
  │  random-pod ─────→ anihpj   ✅  (no policy on anihpj)          │
  │                                                                   │
  │  ⚠️ Policy only affects the TARGET pod (podSelector)            │
  │  ⚠️ Pods with NO policy → still fully open                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🛡️
Key Concepts
  • Policies are additive — multiple policies are OR'd together
  • Applying ANY policy to a pod makes it deny by default
  • No policy = all traffic allowed (default open)
  • Requires CNI with NetworkPolicy support (Calico, Cilium)
⚠️
Critical Requirement

Flannel does NOT support NetworkPolicies. If you apply a NetworkPolicy with Flannel, it's silently ignored. The CKA exam uses Calico or Cilium — both support NetworkPolicy.

20.4 Network Policy Types

Network Policies have two directions: Ingress (incoming traffic TO the pods) and Egress (outgoing traffic FROM the pods). You can control both independently.

Policy Type Direction Controls Example
Ingress Incoming → to selected pods Who can SEND traffic to these pods Only anihpj pods can reach postgres on port 5432
Egress Outgoing ← from selected pods Where these pods can SEND traffic anihpj pods can only reach postgres and DNS
Both Bidirectional Controls both directions Fully locked-down pods — explicit allow required

Network Policy Selectors — Who Can Talk?

📦 podSelector
Match pods by labels in the same namespace. matchLabels: { app: anihpj }
📁 namespaceSelector
Match all pods in namespaces with specific labels. matchLabels: { env: prod }
🌐 ipBlock
Match traffic from/to specific IP ranges. cidr: 10.0.0.0/8, except: [10.0.1.0/24]

20.5 Network Policy YAML Patterns

Here are the most common Network Policy patterns you'll encounter on the CKA exam, using our anihpj stack as context.

Pattern 1: Deny All Ingress (Default Deny)

deny-all-ingress.yaml 
# Deny ALL incoming traffic to ALL pods in "default" namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-ingress
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}          # Empty = selects ALL pods in namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress              # Only control incoming traffic
  # ingress: []            # Omitted = no traffic allowed in
                           # (there are no "allow" rules)

Pattern 2: Allow Specific Pod Access

allow-anihpj-to-postgres.yaml 
# Only anihpj pods can reach postgres on port 5432
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-anihpj-to-postgres
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:              # Target: postgres pods
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:                  # Allow FROM:
        - podSelector:       # Pods with these labels
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj
      ports:                 # On these ports only
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432

Pattern 3: Allow from Specific Namespace

allow-from-monitoring-ns.yaml 
# Allow monitoring namespace to scrape all pods in default namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-monitoring
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}           # All pods in default
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: monitoring    # Namespace must have this label!

Pattern 4: Egress — Restrict Outgoing Traffic

anihpj-egress-policy.yaml 
# anihpj pods can only reach postgres and DNS
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: anihpj-egress
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:                    # Allow TO postgres
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
    - to: []                 # Allow DNS (required!)
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53

Pattern 5: Deny All Ingress + Egress (Total Lockdown)

deny-all-traffic.yaml 
# Complete isolation — no traffic in or out
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}           # All pods
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  # No ingress or egress rules = deny everything
⚠️ CKA Gotcha: AND vs OR Logic in Network Policies

Within a single from block, multiple selectors are ANDed. Separate - from: entries are ORed.

AND vs OR — Critical Difference 
# AND — pod must match BOTH selectors
ingress:
  - from:
      - namespaceSelector:          # AND
          matchLabels: { env: prod }
        podSelector:                # AND (same list item!)
          matchLabels: { app: anihpj }

# OR — pod matches EITHER selector
ingress:
  - from:
      - namespaceSelector:          # OR
          matchLabels: { env: prod }
      - podSelector:                # OR (separate list item!)
          matchLabels: { app: anihpj }

Pattern 6: Allow from Specific IP Range (ipBlock)

allow-from-office-ips.yaml 
# Allow only office network to reach the anihpj admin panel
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-office-only
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - ipBlock:
            cidr: 10.0.0.0/8        # Allow entire 10.x.x.x range
            except:
              - 10.0.99.0/24         # Except the guest WiFi subnet
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: nginx-ingress     # Also allow Ingress controller
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000

Pattern 7: Full Production Lockdown (Ingress + Egress)

anihpj-full-lockdown.yaml 
# anihpj pods: accept from Ingress only, talk only to postgres + DNS
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: anihpj-lockdown
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    - from:                         # Only accept from Ingress controller
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: ingress-nginx
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app.kubernetes.io/name: ingress-nginx
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000
  egress:
    - to:                           # Can reach postgres
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
    - to: []                        # Can reach DNS (required!)
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53

20.6 Testing Network Policies

Always verify your Network Policies work as expected. Here's a systematic testing approach using our anihpj stack.

Testing Network Policy Enforcement 
# Step 1: Apply the "allow-anihpj-to-postgres" policy
kubectl apply -f allow-anihpj-to-postgres.yaml

# Step 2: Verify policy is created
kubectl get networkpolicies
# NAME                        POD-SELECTOR     AGE
# allow-anihpj-to-postgres   app=postgres     5s

# Step 3: Describe the policy for details
kubectl describe networkpolicy allow-anihpj-to-postgres
# Allowing ingress traffic:
#   To Port: 5432/TCP
#   From:
#     PodSelector: app=anihpj

# Step 4: Test ALLOWED traffic (anihpj → postgres)
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- nc -zv postgres-svc 5432
# postgres-svc (10.96.88.5:5432) open   ✅ ALLOWED

# Step 5: Test BLOCKED traffic (random pod → postgres)
kubectl run test-blocked --image=busybox --rm -it -- nc -zv postgres-svc 5432
# → hangs/timeout  ❌ BLOCKED!   (This pod has no "app: anihpj" label)

# Step 6: Test that non-policy pods are still open
kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- curl -s http://redis-svc:6379
# ✅ Works — redis has no NetworkPolicy, so it's still fully open
1
List Policies

kubectl get networkpolicies -A

2
Check Target Pods

kubectl describe netpol POLICY — verify podSelector matches

3
Test Allowed Path

kubectl exec ALLOWED-POD -- nc -zv TARGET PORT

4
Test Blocked Path

kubectl run test -- nc -zv TARGET PORT — should timeout

5
Verify CNI Support

If nothing is blocked — CNI may not support policies (Flannel)

🔑 Chapter 20 Quick Reference

Action Command / YAML
List Ingress resourceskubectl get ingress -A
Describe Ingress routingkubectl describe ingress NAME
Check Ingress Controllerkubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
Create TLS Secretkubectl create secret tls NAME --cert=FILE --key=FILE
List NetworkPolicieskubectl get networkpolicies -A
Describe policy detailskubectl describe netpol NAME
Deny all ingresspodSelector: {} / policyTypes: [Ingress]
Allow specific podfrom: [podSelector: {matchLabels: {app: X}}]
Allow DNS in egressto: [] / ports: [{port: 53, protocol: UDP}]
Test connectivitykubectl exec POD -- nc -zv TARGET PORT
🧪 CKA Practice Scenario: Network Policy Challenge

Task: In the anihpj-prod namespace, create a NetworkPolicy so that only the anihpj web pods can access the postgres pods on port 5432. All other ingress traffic to postgres must be denied. Pods must still be able to resolve DNS.

Solution 
# Step 1: Create the NetworkPolicy YAML
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: postgres-allow-anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-prod
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres           # Target: postgres pods
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress                 # Control incoming traffic
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj     # Only anihpj pods can connect
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432          # Only on postgres port
EOF

# Step 2: Verify the policy
kubectl get netpol -n anihpj-prod
# NAME                    POD-SELECTOR    AGE
# postgres-allow-anihpj  app=postgres    5s

# Step 3: Test — anihpj CAN reach postgres 
kubectl exec -n anihpj-prod anihpj-pod -- nc -zv postgres-svc 5432
# open  ✅

# Step 4: Test — random pod CANNOT reach postgres
kubectl run -n anihpj-prod test --image=busybox --rm -it -- \
  nc -w 3 -zv postgres-svc 5432
# timeout  ❌ BLOCKED — policy works!
Part 5 Complete Network Architecture — anihpj Stack
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    INTERNET                                        │
  │                       │                                             │
  │              ┌────────▼────────┐                                   │
  │              │  Cloud LB /     │  TLS Termination                  │
  │              │  Ingress (443)  │  anihpj.example.com               │
  │              └────────┬────────┘                                   │
  │                  Ingress Rules:                                    │
  │                  /api  → anihpj-svc:80                            │
  │                  /admin → anihpj-svc:80                           │
  │                       │                                             │
  │   ┌───────────────────▼──────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │   │              anihpj-svc (ClusterIP: 10.96.45.2:80)      │    │
  │   │              selector: app=anihpj                         │    │
  │   └────────┬────────────────────┬────────────────┬───────────┘    │
  │            │                    │                │                  │
  │   ┌────────▼──────┐  ┌─────────▼─────┐  ┌──────▼─────────┐      │
  │   │ anihpj-abc    │  │ anihpj-def    │  │ anihpj-ghi    │      │
  │   │ 10.244.1.5    │  │ 10.244.2.3    │  │ 10.244.3.7    │      │
  │   │ :8000 Django  │  │ :8000 Django  │  │ :8000 Django  │      │
  │   └───────┬───────┘  └───────┬───────┘  └──────┬────────┘      │
  │           │ NetworkPolicy     │                  │                  │
  │           │ allows :5432      │                  │                  │
  │   ┌───────▼──────────────────────────────────────▼──────────┐    │
  │   │        postgres-headless (clusterIP: None)              │    │
  │   │        selector: app=postgres                            │    │
  │   └────────┬────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘    │
  │   ┌────────▼──────┐   ┌────────▼──────┐                         │
  │   │ postgres-0    │   │ postgres-1    │   StatefulSet           │
  │   │ 10.244.1.15   │   │ 10.244.2.18   │   + PVC per replica    │
  │   │ PRIMARY       │   │ REPLICA       │                         │
  │   └───────────────┘   └───────────────┘                         │
  │                                                                    │
  │   DNS: anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local → 10.96.45.2        │
  │   DNS: postgres-0.postgres-headless.default... → 10.244.1.15     │
  │   CNI: Calico (VXLAN) │ kube-proxy: iptables                     │
  │   CoreDNS: 10.96.0.10 (kube-dns Service)                        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🟡 Part 6: Storage Chapters 21-23

Chapter 21: Volumes and Persistent Storage

Containers are ephemeral by design — when a container dies, everything inside it vanishes. For our anihpj/jobpost Django app, that means uploaded resumes, profile photos, and the entire SQLite database would be wiped on every restart. Volumes solve this problem by providing storage that outlives containers. In this chapter you'll understand why volumes exist, explore the different volume types Kubernetes offers, and master emptyDir and hostPath — the two simplest volume types that form the foundation for everything in Chapters 22 and 23.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1 hour 💾 Volumes 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why container storage is ephemeral and the real-world problems this causes
  • ✅ The 7 most important volume types in Kubernetes and when to use each
  • ✅ How emptyDir enables sidecar container patterns with shared temp storage
  • ✅ How hostPath maps node directories into pods — and why it's dangerous in production
  • ✅ Volume lifecycle — what survives container restarts vs pod deletions
🎓 CKA Relevance
Storage 10% Workloads & Scheduling 15%
Storage Is 10% of the CKA Exam

CKA storage questions typically ask you to create PVs, PVCs, and mount them into pods. But understanding basic volume types is the prerequisite. Key skills for this chapter:

  • Know the difference between emptyDir (pod-scoped) and hostPath (node-scoped)
  • Understand volume lifecycle: emptyDir survives container restarts but NOT pod deletions
  • Be able to write a pod spec with volumes: and volumeMounts: from scratch
  • Know why hostPath is NOT recommended in production (node affinity, security)

Volume Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Create pod with emptyDir (dry-run)kubectl run mypod --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml then add volumes manually
Inspect pod volumeskubectl describe pod <name> | grep -A10 Volumes
Check files in mounted volumekubectl exec <pod> -- ls -la /path/to/mount
Check disk usagekubectl exec <pod> -- df -h /path/to/mount
View mount events/errorskubectl describe pod <name> | grep -A5 Events
Watch pod statuskubectl get pod <name> -w

Pod with Volume — Exam Skeleton

Memorize this structure — you'll write it from scratch on the CKA exam.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:                    # ◀── WHERE to mount
    - name: my-vol
      mountPath: /data               # ◀── path inside container
      readOnly: false                 # ◀── optional
  volumes:                           # ◀── WHAT to mount
  - name: my-vol                     # ◀── must match volumeMounts.name
    emptyDir: {}                     # ◀── volume type
    # OR: hostPath: { path: /host/path, type: Directory }
    # OR: persistentVolumeClaim: { claimName: my-pvc }
    # OR: configMap: { name: my-cm }
    # OR: secret: { secretName: my-secret }

Volume Gotchas

  • ❌ emptyDir persists across pod restarts — it persists across container restarts, NOT pod deletions
  • ✅ emptyDir survives container crashes but dies with the pod
  • ❌ hostPath works the same on any node — it reads that specific node's filesystem. Different node = different data
  • ✅ hostPath is node-specific — pod rescheduling changes the data
  • volumes[] and volumeMounts[] names must match exactly — typo = mount failure
  • emptyDir: {medium: Memory} counts against the container's memory limit, not disk
  • Forgetting readOnly: true on hostPath mounts is a security risk

Find Volume Fields Fast

kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes              # all volume types
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes.emptyDir      # emptyDir options
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes.hostPath      # hostPath options
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.volumeMounts  # mount options
  • pod.spec.volumes[].emptyDir.medium — "" (disk) or "Memory" (tmpfs)
  • pod.spec.volumes[].emptyDir.sizeLimit — max size before eviction
  • pod.spec.volumes[].hostPath.path — required, path on node
  • pod.spec.volumes[].hostPath.type — validation type (Directory, File, etc.)
  • pod.spec.containers[].volumeMounts[].subPath — mount single file without hiding dir
7 Key Volume Types
2 CKA Must-Know (emptyDir, hostPath)
Pod emptyDir Lifecycle
⚠️ hostPath — Security Risk

🗺️ Volume Types — When to Use What

Pod Storage Need
📂 emptyDir Temp scratch space, sidecar sharing
🖥️ hostPath Node logs, etcd certs, dev/testing
💾 PVC Databases, uploads, persistent data
⚙️ configMap Config files mounted into containers
🔐 secret TLS certs, passwords as files

21.1 The Problem: Container Storage is Ephemeral

Every container starts with a fresh, writable filesystem layer provided by its image. When the container stops or crashes, that layer is destroyed. Any files created, modified, or downloaded during runtime are gone forever. This is by design — containers are disposable — but it creates a massive problem for stateful applications.

The Data Loss Problem — Without Volumes
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  WITHOUT VOLUMES — DATA IS LOST ON EVERY POD RESTART                    │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Timeline:  t=0              t=5min             t=5min+1sec              │
  │             Pod starts       Pod crashes         New pod starts          │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐         │
  │  │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │    │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │    │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │         │
  │  │                │    │                │    │   (new)         │         │
  │  │ 📄 db.sqlite3  │    │ 📄 db.sqlite3  │    │                │         │
  │  │ 📄 resume.pdf  │    │ 📄 resume.pdf  │    │ 📄 (empty!)    │  💀     │
  │  │ 📄 photo.jpg   │    │ 📄 photo.jpg   │    │                │         │
  │  │                │    │    💥 CRASH!    │    │ ALL DATA LOST! │         │
  │  └────────────────┘    └────────────────┘    └────────────────┘         │
  │                                                                          │
  │  50 job posts created    Everything inside     New container starts      │
  │  3 resumes uploaded      the container dies    with BLANK filesystem     │
  │  User profiles saved     with the container    Users see: "No jobs"     │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════      │
  │                                                                          │
  │  WITH VOLUMES — DATA SURVIVES POD RESTARTS                              │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐         │
  │  │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │    │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │    │ 📦 anihpj Pod  │         │
  │  │                │    │                │    │   (new)         │         │
  │  │ 📄 db.sqlite3 ─┼─┐  │    💥 CRASH!   │    │ 📄 db.sqlite3 ─┼─┐      │
  │  │ 📄 resume.pdf ─┼─┤  │                │    │ 📄 resume.pdf ─┼─┤      │
  │  └────────────────┘ │  └────────────────┘    └────────────────┘ │      │
  │                     │                                            │      │
  │                     │    ┌──────────────────┐                    │      │
  │                     └───▶│    💾 VOLUME      │◀───────────────────┘      │
  │                          │  db.sqlite3  ✅  │                           │
  │                          │  resume.pdf  ✅  │  Data survives!           │
  │                          │  photo.jpg   ✅  │  New pod reads same files │
  │                          └──────────────────┘                           │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ This Happens More Often Than You Think

Pods restart frequently in Kubernetes: OOMKilled (out of memory), node failures, rolling updates, scaling down. If your application stores ANY data inside the container filesystem, you will lose it. This includes:

  • Database files (SQLite, PostgreSQL data dir)
  • User uploads (resumes, images, documents)
  • Application logs written to local files
  • Cache files and session data
📦
Container Writable Layer

Each container gets a thin writable layer on top of its read-only image layers. Writes go here. When the container stops, this layer is deleted. It's fast but temporary — perfect for stateless apps, fatal for stateful ones.

💾
Kubernetes Volumes

A Volume is storage that lives outside the container but is mounted inside it at a specific path. The container reads and writes normally, but the data is stored externally. When the container restarts, the Volume is re-mounted with all data intact.

🔑 Key Concept: volumes vs volumeMounts

Every pod spec has two related fields. spec.volumes[] defines the storage (what type, where it comes from). spec.containers[].volumeMounts[] mounts that storage into a specific path inside the container. You always need both — a volume definition AND a mount point.

volumes-vs-volumemounts.yaml 
# The two parts of Kubernetes volume configuration
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:latest
    volumeMounts:                    # ◀── WHERE to mount (inside container)
    - name: app-data                 #     references volumes[] by name
      mountPath: /app/data           #     path inside the container
    - name: static-files
      mountPath: /app/static
      readOnly: true                 #     container can only READ
  volumes:                           # ◀── WHAT to mount (storage source)
  - name: app-data                   #     name must match volumeMounts
    emptyDir: {}                     #     type: temporary pod-scoped storage
  - name: static-files
    configMap:                       #     type: files from a ConfigMap
      name: anihpj-static-config
📖 Volume Lifecycle Rule

A volume's lifetime depends on its type. An emptyDir lives as long as the pod — it survives container crashes but is deleted when the pod is removed. A PersistentVolume lives as long as the cluster — it survives pod deletions entirely. Understanding this lifecycle is critical for choosing the right volume type.

21.2 Volume Types Overview

Kubernetes supports over 20 volume types, but for the CKA exam and daily work, you need to know 7 key types. Each serves a different purpose, has a different lifecycle, and is suited to specific use cases.

Volume Type Persists? Scope Use Case CKA Exam?
emptyDir ❌ Pod lifetime only Pod Temp files, cache, sidecar sharing ✅ Yes
hostPath ⚠️ Node lifetime Node Dev/testing, node logs access ✅ Yes
persistentVolumeClaim ✅ Cluster lifetime Cluster Databases, user uploads, any persistent data ✅ Heavily tested
configMap N/A (config data) Cluster Config files mounted as volumes ✅ Yes
secret N/A (sensitive data) Cluster TLS certs, passwords as files ✅ Yes
nfs ✅ Network storage Network Shared storage across pods/nodes ⚠️ Sometimes
csi ✅ Provider-managed Provider Cloud storage (AWS EBS, GCE PD, Azure Disk) ⚠️ Conceptual
Volume Types by Lifecycle and Scope
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  VOLUME LIFECYCLE SPECTRUM                                               │
  │                                                                          │
  │  Shortest ◀──────────────────────────────────────────▶ Longest          │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ┌─────────────┐   ┌─────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  emptyDir    │   │  hostPath   │   │  persistentVolumeClaim (PVC) │  │
  │  │             │   │             │   │                              │  │
  │  │  Lives with  │   │  Lives on   │   │  Lives in cluster           │  │
  │  │  the POD     │   │  the NODE   │   │  Independent of pods        │  │
  │  │             │   │             │   │                              │  │
  │  │  Pod dies →  │   │  Node dies →│   │  Pod dies → data SAFE ✅   │  │
  │  │  data GONE 💀│   │  data GONE 💀│   │  Node dies → data SAFE ✅  │  │
  │  └─────────────┘   └─────────────┘   └──────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  configMap / secret          │  nfs / csi                       │    │
  │  │                              │                                   │    │
  │  │  NOT storage — these inject  │  Network/cloud storage            │    │
  │  │  configuration and secrets   │  Fully independent of cluster     │    │
  │  │  as files into containers    │  Survives cluster deletion        │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯
CKA Exam Focus

The exam heavily tests persistentVolumeClaim (creating PVs, PVCs, binding). You'll also see emptyDir in multi-container pod questions and hostPath for etcd backup paths. configMap and secret volumes appear in configuration questions.

🏗️
anihpj Storage Plan

Our Django app needs: PVC for PostgreSQL data (10Gi), PVC for user uploads (resumes, photos), ConfigMap volume for Django settings, Secret volume for database credentials, emptyDir for nginx ↔ Django static file sharing.

💡 Quick Decision Guide

Need temp scratch space shared between containers? → emptyDir. Need data to survive pod deletion? → PVC. Need to inject config files? → configMap. Need to inject secrets as files? → secret. Need shared storage across multiple nodes? → nfs or CSI with ReadWriteMany.

21.3 emptyDir — Temporary Shared Storage

An emptyDir volume is the simplest volume type. It's created as an empty directory when a pod starts and is deleted when the pod is removed. It's perfect for sharing data between containers in the same pod — the classic sidecar pattern.

✅
When to Use emptyDir

Sidecar containers sharing data (nginx + Django), temporary scratch space for computation, cache that can be regenerated, checkpoint data during multi-step processing.

❌
When NOT to Use emptyDir

Database storage, user uploads, anything that must survive a pod restart. If the data matters, use a PersistentVolumeClaim instead.

emptyDir — Sidecar Pattern: Django + Nginx Sharing Static Files
  ┌──────────────────────── Pod: anihpj-web ─────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────┐         │
  │  │  Container 1:      │         │  Container 2:        │         │
  │  │  django            │         │  nginx               │         │
  │  │                    │         │                      │         │
  │  │  1. collectstatic  │         │  3. Serves static    │         │
  │  │     writes CSS/JS  │         │     files from       │         │
  │  │     to /static/    │         │     /usr/share/nginx │         │
  │  │                    │         │                      │         │
  │  │  volumeMount:      │         │  volumeMount:        │         │
  │  │    /app/static     │         │    /usr/share/nginx  │         │
  │  └─────────┬──────────┘         └──────────┬───────────┘         │
  │            │                               │                      │
  │            │   2. SHARED emptyDir volume    │                      │
  │            └──────────┐  ┌─────────────────┘                      │
  │                       ▼  ▼                                         │
  │              ┌──────────────────┐                                  │
  │              │  emptyDir: {}    │                                  │
  │              │  /static-files   │                                  │
  │              │                  │                                  │
  │              │  style.css  ✅   │                                  │
  │              │  app.js     ✅   │                                  │
  │              │  logo.png   ✅   │                                  │
  │              └──────────────────┘                                  │
  │                                                                    │
  │  Both containers see the SAME files — writes by Django are        │
  │  immediately visible to Nginx. No network calls needed!           │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-emptydir-sidecar.yaml 
# anihpj Django + Nginx sidecar sharing static files via emptyDir
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: web
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: collect-static
    image: anihpj:latest
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "collectstatic", "--noinput"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: static-files              # ◀── writes static files here
      mountPath: /app/staticfiles
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
    command: ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: static-files              # ◀── Django can still access them
      mountPath: /app/staticfiles
    - name: tmp-scratch               # ◀── scratch space for processing
      mountPath: /tmp/processing
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
    volumeMounts:
    - name: static-files              # ◀── Nginx serves the same files
      mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html/static
      readOnly: true                  #     Nginx only READS, never writes
  volumes:
  - name: static-files               # ◀── Shared between all containers
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: tmp-scratch                 # ◀── Only used by Django
    emptyDir:
      sizeLimit: 100Mi               #     Optional: limit disk usage
📖 emptyDir Medium Options

By default, emptyDir uses the node's disk. You can also use RAM-backed storage with emptyDir: { medium: Memory }. This is faster but counts against the container's memory limit. Useful for high-speed temp files, but remember: RAM is more expensive than disk and the data is still lost when the pod dies.

emptydir-memory-backed.yaml 
# RAM-backed emptyDir — fast but size-limited
volumes:
- name: cache-volume
  emptyDir:
    medium: Memory          # ◀── Uses RAM instead of disk (tmpfs)
    sizeLimit: 256Mi        #     Pod evicted if exceeds this limit

🔬 emptyDir Lifecycle — What Survives What?

🔄 Container restart
Data SURVIVES ✅ — emptyDir lives at the pod level, not the container level. If Django crashes and kubelet restarts it, the static files are still there.
💀 Pod deletion
Data LOST ❌ — When the pod is deleted (scaling down, rescheduling, node failure), the emptyDir is permanently deleted. Cannot be recovered.
🔄 Rolling update
Data LOST ❌ — A rolling update creates a NEW pod. The old pod (and its emptyDir) is deleted. The new pod gets a fresh empty emptyDir.
📍 Node failure
Data LOST ❌ — Pod is rescheduled to a different node. The emptyDir was on the old node's disk. New pod starts fresh.

21.4 hostPath — Node-Level Storage

A hostPath volume mounts a file or directory from the node's filesystem directly into the pod. Unlike emptyDir, the data exists on the node's disk and persists even after the pod is deleted — but only on that specific node.

⚠️ hostPath Is Dangerous in Production

hostPath gives the container direct access to the node's filesystem. This is a security risk (containers can read/write sensitive host files) and a portability problem (pod must always run on the same node). Use it only for development, testing, or accessing node-level system files like logs.

hostPath — Pod Reads Node's Log Directory
  ┌──────── Node: worker-1 ──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                    │
  │  Node Filesystem:                                                  │
  │  /var/log/                                                         │
  │  ├── syslog                                                        │
  │  ├── kern.log                                                      │
  │  ├── containers/                                                   │
  │  │   ├── anihpj-abc.log                                            │
  │  │   └── postgres-0.log                                            │
  │  └── pods/                                                         │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌────────────────────── Pod: log-collector ──────────────────┐   │
  │  │                                                              │   │
  │  │  Container: fluentd                                         │   │
  │  │  mountPath: /var/log/host  ◀──── hostPath: /var/log/        │   │
  │  │                                                              │   │
  │  │  $ ls /var/log/host/                                        │   │
  │  │  syslog  kern.log  containers/  pods/                       │   │
  │  │                                                              │   │
  │  │  ✅ Can read all node logs!                                 │   │
  │  │  ⚠️ Only works on THIS node                                │   │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                    │
  │  If pod moves to worker-2 → different /var/log/ directory!        │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
hostpath-log-collector.yaml 
# Log collector pod using hostPath to access node logs
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: log-collector
spec:
  containers:
  - name: fluentd
    image: fluent/fluentd:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: node-logs
      mountPath: /var/log/host        # ◀── appears here inside container
      readOnly: true                  #     ALWAYS use readOnly for safety
    - name: container-logs
      mountPath: /var/lib/docker/containers
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: node-logs
    hostPath:
      path: /var/log                  # ◀── actual path on the NODE
      type: Directory                 #     must exist, must be a directory
  - name: container-logs
    hostPath:
      path: /var/lib/docker/containers
      type: DirectoryOrCreate        #     creates if doesn't exist
hostPath Type Behavior Fails If...
"" (empty) No checks before mounting Never (unsafe)
DirectoryOrCreate Creates directory if missing Never (auto-creates)
Directory Must exist as a directory Path doesn't exist or isn't a dir
FileOrCreate Creates file if missing Never (auto-creates)
File Must exist as a file Path doesn't exist or isn't a file
Socket Must exist as a UNIX socket Not a socket file
✅
Legitimate hostPath Uses

Accessing node logs (/var/log), Docker socket (/var/run/docker.sock), etcd data dir for backups, /etc/kubernetes for kubelet config. Always with readOnly: true when possible.

⛔
Never Use hostPath For

Application databases, user uploads, any data that needs to move between nodes. If the pod gets rescheduled to a different node, it will see a different (or empty) directory. Use PVC instead.

CKA Exam: etcd backup using hostPath 
# CKA Exam Classic: etcd backup pod needs hostPath for certificates
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: etcd-backup
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  containers:
  - name: etcd-backup
    image: registry.k8s.io/etcd:3.5.12-0
    command:
    - /bin/sh
    - -c
    - |
      etcdctl snapshot save /backup/etcd-snapshot.db \
        --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
        --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
        --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
        --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
    volumeMounts:
    - name: etcd-certs                # ◀── CKA: etcd certs on control plane
      mountPath: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd
      readOnly: true
    - name: backup-dir                # ◀── where snapshot is saved
      mountPath: /backup
  volumes:
  - name: etcd-certs
    hostPath:
      path: /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd  # ◀── actual certs on the node
      type: Directory
  - name: backup-dir
    hostPath:
      path: /opt/etcd-backup          # ◀── backup saved on node filesystem
      type: DirectoryOrCreate
  hostNetwork: true
  nodeName: controlplane              # ◀── must run on control plane node
🏋️ CKA Practice Scenario: Volume Troubleshooting

Scenario: A developer reports that their anihpj pod keeps losing uploaded resumes every time it restarts. They've configured the pod as shown:

broken-pod.yaml (spot the problem) 
# What's wrong with this pod?
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: uploads
      mountPath: /app/media/uploads
  volumes:
  - name: uploads
    emptyDir: {}        # ◀── 🤔 Is this the right volume type?

Diagnosis: emptyDir data is deleted when the pod is deleted. Every restart creates a new, empty directory. For data that must persist, they need a PersistentVolumeClaim.

Fix: Replace emptyDir: {} with a PVC reference:

fixed-pod.yaml 
# Fixed: Use PVC instead of emptyDir for persistent data
  volumes:
  - name: uploads
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: anihpj-uploads-pvc   # ◀── data survives pod restarts!

🔧 Volume Troubleshooting Quick Reference

Symptom Likely Cause Fix
Pod won't start — MountVolume.SetUp failed hostPath directory doesn't exist on node Use type: DirectoryOrCreate or pre-create the directory
Data disappears after pod restart Using emptyDir for persistent data Switch to persistentVolumeClaim
Container can't write to volume readOnly: true set on volumeMount Remove readOnly or set to false
Permission denied inside container Container runs as non-root but volume owned by root Add fsGroup in securityContext to change group ownership
emptyDir exceeds size — pod evicted sizeLimit exceeded (or node disk pressure) Increase sizeLimit or clean up data inside the volume
Volume shows different data after reschedule hostPath is node-specific — different node = different data Use PVC with network storage (NFS, cloud) for multi-node access

⌨️ Chapter 21 — Essential kubectl Commands

🔍 Inspect pod volumes
kubectl describe pod <name> | grep -A10 "Volumes:"
📂 List files in mounted volume
kubectl exec <pod> -- ls -la /path/to/mount
💾 Check disk usage in volume
kubectl exec <pod> -- df -h /path/to/mount
📋 View pod events (mount errors)
kubectl describe pod <name> | grep -A5 "Events:"
🔄 Watch pod status during restart
kubectl get pod <name> -w

Chapter 22: PersistentVolumes, PVCs, and StorageClasses

This is the most heavily tested storage topic on the CKA exam. The PV/PVC model separates storage provisioning (admin responsibility) from storage consumption (developer responsibility). You'll create PersistentVolumes, bind them with PersistentVolumeClaims, mount them into pods, and set up StorageClasses for automatic dynamic provisioning — all demonstrated with our anihpj/jobpost PostgreSQL database setup.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 💾 PV/PVC 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The PV/PVC model — how admin provisioning and developer consumption are separated
  • ✅ How to create PersistentVolumes with correct access modes and reclaim policies
  • ✅ How to create PersistentVolumeClaims and understand binding rules
  • ✅ How to mount PVCs into Pods and Deployments for persistent storage
  • ✅ StorageClasses for dynamic provisioning — eliminate manual PV creation
  • ✅ Complete PostgreSQL + anihpj storage setup using volumeClaimTemplates
🎓 CKA Relevance
Storage 10%
PV/PVC Is the #1 Storage Topic on the CKA

Expect at least 1-2 questions on PV/PVC creation and binding. Master these commands:

  • kubectl get pv,pvc — check PV and PVC status
  • kubectl describe pvc <name> — debug why a PVC is Pending
  • Know all three access modes: ReadWriteOnce, ReadOnlyMany, ReadWriteMany
  • Know reclaim policies: Retain (keep data), Delete (destroy data)
  • Write PV + PVC YAML from scratch — the exam doesn't always have templates

PV/PVC Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
List PVs (cluster-wide)kubectl get pv
List PVCs in namespacekubectl get pvc -n <namespace>
List PVCs all namespaceskubectl get pvc -A
Debug Pending PVCkubectl describe pvc <name> -n <ns>
List StorageClasseskubectl get storageclass (or kubectl get sc)
Check PV detailskubectl describe pv <name>
Change reclaim policykubectl patch pv <name> -p '{"spec":{"persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy":"Retain"}}'
Delete PVkubectl delete pv <name>

PV + PVC + Pod — Complete Exam Skeleton

This 3-part pattern is the most tested storage question on the CKA. Write it from memory.

# 1. PersistentVolume (admin creates)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: my-pv                       # cluster-scoped, no namespace
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Gi                    # ◀── total capacity
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce                    # ◀── RWO / ROX / RWX
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain  # ◀── Retain / Delete
  storageClassName: manual           # ◀── must match PVC
  hostPath:
    path: /mnt/data                  # ◀── node path (exam default)
---
# 2. PersistentVolumeClaim (developer creates)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: my-pvc                      # namespaced
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce                    # ◀── must match PV
  storageClassName: manual           # ◀── must match PV
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi                   # ◀── ≤ PV capacity
---
# 3. Pod using PVC
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: data-vol
      mountPath: /data
  volumes:
  - name: data-vol
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: my-pvc              # ◀── reference PVC by name

PV/PVC Exam Traps

  • ❌ PVC size must exactly match PV size
  • ✅ PVC request must be ≤ PV capacity — K8s picks the smallest matching PV
  • ❌ ReadWriteOnce means only one pod can mount it
  • ✅ RWO means one NODE. Multiple pods on same node can all mount it
  • storageClassName must match EXACTLY between PV and PVC — empty string ≠ omitted
  • PV is cluster-scoped but PVC is namespaced — pod and PVC must be in same namespace
  • Recycle reclaim policy is deprecated — use Retain or Delete
  • After PVC deletion with Retain policy, PV stays Released — must delete and recreate PV to reuse

Find PV/PVC YAML Fields Fast

kubectl explain pv.spec                        # PV spec fields
kubectl explain pv.spec.capacity               # storage size
kubectl explain pv.spec.accessModes            # RWO/ROX/RWX
kubectl explain pv.spec.persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy  # reclaim policy
kubectl explain pvc.spec                       # PVC spec fields
kubectl explain pvc.spec.resources.requests    # request size
🔧Admin creates PV
→
👨‍💻Dev creates PVC
→
🔗K8s binds PVC→PV
→
📦Pod mounts PVC
→
✅Data persists!
3 Access Modes (RWO/ROX/RWX)
2 Reclaim Policies (Retain/Delete)
4 PV Statuses
1:1 PV↔PVC Binding

22.1 The PV/PVC Model — Separation of Concerns

Kubernetes storage uses a two-layer model to separate the "how" from the "what". The cluster admin provisions storage (PersistentVolume), and the developer requests storage (PersistentVolumeClaim). Kubernetes automatically binds matching PVs to PVCs based on size, access mode, and StorageClass.

The PV/PVC Model — Admin vs Developer Responsibilities
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  THE PV / PVC MODEL — SEPARATION OF CONCERNS                            │
  │                                                                          │
  │  🔧 CLUSTER ADMIN                    👨‍💻 DEVELOPER                       │
  │  "I provision storage"               "I request storage"                 │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ┌──────────────────────┐           ┌──────────────────────┐            │
  │  │  PersistentVolume    │           │  PersistentVolume    │            │
  │  │  (PV)                │           │  Claim (PVC)         │            │
  │  │                      │           │                      │            │
  │  │  capacity: 10Gi      │           │  request: 5Gi        │            │
  │  │  accessModes:        │           │  accessModes:        │            │
  │  │    - ReadWriteOnce   │◀─── BIND ─▶│    - ReadWriteOnce   │            │
  │  │  storageClass: fast  │           │  storageClass: fast  │            │
  │  │  hostPath:           │           │                      │            │
  │  │    /data/postgres    │           │                      │            │
  │  └──────────────────────┘           └──────────┬───────────┘            │
  │                                                │                        │
  │  Admin doesn't need to know                    │ Developer doesn't      │
  │  WHICH pod will use it                         │ need to know WHERE     │
  │                                                │ the storage is         │
  │                                                ▼                        │
  │                                     ┌──────────────────────┐            │
  │                                     │  Pod / Deployment    │            │
  │                                     │                      │            │
  │                                     │  volumes:            │            │
  │                                     │  - persistentVolume  │            │
  │                                     │    Claim:            │            │
  │                                     │    claimName: my-pvc │            │
  │                                     │                      │            │
  │                                     │  volumeMounts:       │            │
  │                                     │  - mountPath: /data  │            │
  │                                     └──────────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
  │  BINDING RULES:                                                          │
  │  ✅ PVC.request ≤ PV.capacity        (size must fit)                    │
  │  ✅ PVC.accessModes ⊆ PV.accessModes (modes must match)                │
  │  ✅ PVC.storageClass = PV.storageClass (class must match)               │
  │  ❌ No match? → PVC stays "Pending" until a matching PV appears        │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
1
Admin Creates PV

Defines capacity, access modes, reclaim policy, and points to actual storage (hostPath, NFS, cloud disk)

2
Developer Creates PVC

Requests storage size and access mode. Doesn't need to know where storage lives.

3
Kubernetes Binds

Finds a PV that matches the PVC's requirements. PVC status changes from Pending → Bound.

4
Pod Mounts PVC

Pod references the PVC by name. The bound PV's storage appears at the specified mountPath.

5
Data Persists ✅

Pod dies, restarts, gets rescheduled — PVC binding survives. New pod mounts same data.

🔬 PV/PVC Status Lifecycle

📋 Available
PV exists but no PVC has claimed it yet. It's ready to be bound.
🔗 Bound
PV is bound to a PVC. One-to-one relationship. The PV is now in use.
🔓 Released
PVC was deleted but PV still has data from the old claim. Admin must manually reclaim it (if Retain policy).
❌ Failed
Automatic reclamation failed. PV needs manual intervention.

22.2 Creating PersistentVolumes

A PersistentVolume is a cluster-level resource — it doesn't belong to any namespace. The admin creates it with a specific capacity, access mode, reclaim policy, and storage backend.

pv-hostpath.yaml 
# PersistentVolume with hostPath (for local testing / CKA exam)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: anihpj-data-pv           # ◀── cluster-scoped, no namespace
  labels:
    type: local
    app: anihpj
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Gi                 # ◀── total storage this PV provides
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce                 # ◀── can be mounted read-write by ONE node
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain    # ◀── keep data when PVC deleted
  storageClassName: manual        # ◀── must match PVC's storageClassName
  hostPath:
    path: /data/anihpj-postgres   # ◀── directory on the node
    type: DirectoryOrCreate
Access Mode Abbreviation Meaning Typical Use
ReadWriteOnce RWO Read-write by a single node Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL)
ReadOnlyMany ROX Read-only by multiple nodes Shared config, static assets
ReadWriteMany RWX Read-write by multiple nodes Shared uploads, NFS-backed
ReadWriteOncePod RWOP Read-write by a single pod Exclusive access (K8s 1.29+)
⚠️ Access Modes Are About NODES, Not Pods

ReadWriteOnce means only one node can mount the volume for writing. Multiple pods on that same node CAN all mount it. This is a common CKA misconception. For true single-pod exclusivity, use ReadWriteOncePod (v1.29+).

Reclaim Policy What Happens When PVC Deleted Use When
Retain PV keeps data, status → Released. Admin must manually clean up and re-provision. Production databases, critical data you can't lose
Delete PV AND underlying storage deleted automatically. Data is gone. Dynamic provisioning, temporary/recreatable data
Recycle Runs rm -rf on the volume and makes it Available again. Deprecated. Don't use — deprecated since K8s 1.15
pv-nfs.yaml 
# PersistentVolume backed by NFS (multi-node shared storage)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: anihpj-uploads-pv
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 20Gi
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteMany                 # ◀── NFS supports multi-node read-write
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain
  storageClassName: nfs
  nfs:
    server: 192.168.1.100         # ◀── NFS server IP
    path: /exports/anihpj-uploads # ◀── shared directory on NFS server

22.3 Creating PersistentVolumeClaims

A PVC is the developer's side of the equation — a request for storage. Unlike PVs (cluster-scoped), PVCs are namespaced. When you create a PVC, Kubernetes searches for a PV that meets the requirements. If found, the PVC binds to it. If not, the PVC stays in Pending state.

pvc-postgres.yaml 
# PVC requesting storage for anihpj PostgreSQL database
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: anihpj-postgres-pvc      # ◀── namespaced resource
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce                 # ◀── must match PV's access modes
  storageClassName: manual        # ◀── must match PV's storageClassName
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi                # ◀── must be ≤ PV's capacity (10Gi)
# Binding: K8s finds anihpj-data-pv (10Gi, RWO, manual) → MATCH → Bound!
PVC Binding Logic — How Kubernetes Matches PVCs to PVs
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PVC BINDING DECISION TREE                                               │
  │                                                                          │
  │  New PVC created: requests 5Gi, RWO, storageClass: "manual"             │
  │       │                                                                  │
  │       ▼                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐                           │
  │  │  Step 1: Filter by storageClassName       │                           │
  │  │  Looking for PVs with class = "manual"    │                           │
  │  └────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘                           │
  │                   │                                                      │
  │       ┌───────────┴───────────┐                                         │
  │       ▼                       ▼                                         │
  │  ┌─────────┐            ┌─────────┐                                    │
  │  │ PV-A    │            │ PV-B    │                                    │
  │  │ 10Gi    │            │ 5Gi     │                                    │
  │  │ manual  │ ✅          │ fast    │ ❌ wrong class                     │
  │  └────┬────┘            └─────────┘                                    │
  │       │                                                                  │
  │       ▼                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐                           │
  │  │  Step 2: Filter by accessModes            │                           │
  │  │  PVC needs RWO → PV-A has RWO?           │                           │
  │  │  YES ✅                                   │                           │
  │  └────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘                           │
  │                   │                                                      │
  │       ▼                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐                           │
  │  │  Step 3: Check capacity                   │                           │
  │  │  PVC requests 5Gi ≤ PV-A has 10Gi?       │                           │
  │  │  YES ✅                                   │                           │
  │  └────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘                           │
  │                   │                                                      │
  │       ▼                                                                  │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐                           │
  │  │  ✅ BOUND!                                │                           │
  │  │  PVC → PV-A                               │                           │
  │  │  PVC gets full 10Gi (even though it       │                           │
  │  │  only asked for 5Gi — smallest match)     │                           │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘                           │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Debugging: PVC Stuck in Pending

If a PVC shows Pending status, run kubectl describe pvc <name> and check the Events section. Common causes:

  • No PV with matching storageClassName
  • No PV with sufficient capacity
  • No PV with matching access modes
  • All matching PVs are already Bound to other PVCs
  • If using dynamic provisioning: the StorageClass provisioner is not installed
Verify PV/PVC binding 
# Check PV and PVC status
kubectl get pv
# NAME              CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES   RECLAIM POLICY   STATUS   CLAIM
# anihpj-data-pv    10Gi       RWO            Retain           Bound    anihpj-production/anihpj-postgres-pvc

kubectl get pvc -n anihpj-production
# NAME                   STATUS   VOLUME            CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES
# anihpj-postgres-pvc    Bound    anihpj-data-pv    10Gi       RWO

# Debug a Pending PVC
kubectl describe pvc anihpj-postgres-pvc -n anihpj-production
# Events:
#   Warning  ProvisioningFailed  no persistent volumes available for this claim

22.4 Using PVCs in Pods

Once a PVC is Bound, you reference it in your Pod spec exactly like any other volume. The pod doesn't know or care whether the PVC is backed by a local disk, NFS, or cloud storage — it just mounts a directory.

deployment-with-pvc.yaml 
# anihpj Deployment using PVC for PostgreSQL data
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: anihpj:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        volumeMounts:
        - name: user-uploads          # ◀── mount name (matches volumes[])
          mountPath: /app/media       # ◀── path INSIDE the container
        - name: static-files
          mountPath: /app/staticfiles
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: user-uploads            # ◀── references the PVC
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: anihpj-uploads-pvc   # ◀── name of the PVC
      - name: static-files
        configMap:
          name: anihpj-static-config
Complete Flow: PV → PVC → Pod → Container Mount
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  COMPLETE MOUNT FLOW                                                     │
  │                                                                          │
  │  PersistentVolume          PVC                   Pod                     │
  │  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐       │
  │  │ anihpj-pv    │    │ anihpj-pvc   │    │ anihpj-web-abc       │       │
  │  │              │    │              │    │                      │       │
  │  │ /data/uploads│◀──▶│ BOUND        │◀───│ volumes:             │       │
  │  │ on node disk │    │              │    │ - pvc: anihpj-pvc   │       │
  │  │              │    │              │    │                      │       │
  │  │ 10Gi, RWO    │    │ 5Gi request  │    │ containers:          │       │
  │  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    │ - volumeMounts:      │       │
  │                                          │   - /app/media ◀─┐  │       │
  │                                          └──────────────────┼──┘       │
  │                                                             │           │
  │  What the container sees:                                   │           │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                       │           │
  │  │  / (container root)              │                       │           │
  │  │  ├── app/                        │                       │           │
  │  │  │   ├── manage.py               │                       │           │
  │  │  │   ├── anihpj/                 │                       │           │
  │  │  │   └── media/ ◀───────────────────── PVC mounted here  │           │
  │  │  │       ├── resumes/            │                       │           │
  │  │  │       │   └── john_doe.pdf    │   Data on external    │           │
  │  │  │       └── photos/             │   storage, not in     │           │
  │  │  │           └── profile.jpg     │   the container!      │           │
  │  │  └── ...                         │                       │           │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────┘                       │           │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📖 PVC Must Be in the Same Namespace as the Pod

PVCs are namespaced resources. A pod in anihpj-production can only mount PVCs that also exist in anihpj-production. PVs themselves are cluster-scoped, but the PVC (the claim) must be co-located with the pod.

22.5 StorageClasses — Dynamic Provisioning

StorageClasses eliminate the need for an admin to pre-create PVs. When a developer creates a PVC with a storageClassName, and that class has a provisioner, Kubernetes automatically creates a matching PV on the fly. This is how most production clusters work.

Static vs Dynamic Provisioning
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  STATIC PROVISIONING (manual)        DYNAMIC PROVISIONING (automatic)    │
  │                                                                          │
  │  ┌─────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────┐            │
  │  │ 1. Admin creates PV │            │ 1. Admin creates     │            │
  │  │    (10Gi, RWO, host)│            │    StorageClass      │            │
  │  └─────────┬───────────┘            │    (provisioner,     │            │
  │            │                        │     params)          │            │
  │            ▼                        └──────────┬───────────┘            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────┐                       │  (one-time setup)      │
  │  │ 2. Dev creates PVC  │                       │                        │
  │  │    (5Gi, RWO)       │                       ▼                        │
  │  └─────────┬───────────┘            ┌──────────────────────┐            │
  │            │                        │ 2. Dev creates PVC   │            │
  │            ▼                        │    with storageClass │            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────┐            └──────────┬───────────┘            │
  │  │ 3. K8s binds        │                       │                        │
  │  │    PVC → PV         │                       ▼                        │
  │  └─────────┬───────────┘            ┌──────────────────────┐            │
  │            │                        │ 3. StorageClass auto │            │
  │            ▼                        │    creates PV! 🎉    │            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────┐            └──────────┬───────────┘            │
  │  │ 4. Pod mounts PVC   │                       │                        │
  │  └─────────────────────┘                       ▼                        │
  │                                     ┌──────────────────────┐            │
  │  Every new PVC = admin must         │ 4. Auto-bind + mount │            │
  │  manually create a PV first 😰     └──────────────────────┘            │
  │                                                                          │
  │                                     Admin sets up ONCE.                  │
  │                                     Every new PVC = auto PV! 🎉        │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
storageclass.yaml 
# StorageClass for dynamic provisioning
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-ssd                      # ◀── the name PVCs reference
provisioner: kubernetes.io/no-provisioner  # ◀── for local testing
#provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com         # ◀── AWS EBS
#provisioner: pd.csi.storage.gke.io   # ◀── GCE Persistent Disk
#provisioner: disk.csi.azure.com      # ◀── Azure Disk
reclaimPolicy: Delete                  # ◀── auto-delete PV when PVC deleted
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer  # ◀── delay binding until pod scheduled
allowVolumeExpansion: true             # ◀── allow PVC resize
pvc-dynamic.yaml 
# PVC using dynamic provisioning — no PV needed, auto-created!
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: anihpj-dynamic-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: fast-ssd          # ◀── references StorageClass above
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
# Result: StorageClass provisioner creates a 10Gi PV automatically
# PVC binds to the new PV → Ready to use in pods!
Feature Static Provisioning Dynamic Provisioning
PV creation Manual by admin Automatic by StorageClass
Admin workload High — create PV per request Low — one-time StorageClass setup
Storage waste PVs may sit unused PV created on-demand, no waste
CKA exam ✅ Tested (manual PV+PVC) ✅ Tested (StorageClass config)
Production use Rare (legacy/special cases) Standard practice in most clusters
💡 Default StorageClass

Most clusters have a default StorageClass. If you create a PVC without specifying storageClassName, the default class is used automatically. Check with kubectl get storageclass — the default has (default) annotation. On the CKA exam, there's usually a default class already configured.

22.6 PostgreSQL Storage for anihpj

Let's put everything together: a complete production storage setup for PostgreSQL backing our anihpj/jobpost Django application. This uses a StorageClass, PVC via volumeClaimTemplates in a StatefulSet, and proper data directory mounting.

Complete anihpj PostgreSQL Storage Architecture
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  anihpj POSTGRESQL STORAGE — COMPLETE SETUP                              │
  │                                                                          │
  │  StorageClass: "postgres-storage"                                        │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────┐                                      │
  │  │  provisioner: local/hostPath   │                                      │
  │  │  reclaimPolicy: Retain         │ ◀── never delete DB data            │
  │  │  volumeBindingMode: WFFC       │                                      │
  │  └──────────────┬─────────────────┘                                      │
  │                 │ creates PV automatically                               │
  │                 ▼                                                         │
  │  PersistentVolumeClaim: "postgres-data-postgres-0"                       │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────┐                                      │
  │  │  10Gi, ReadWriteOnce           │                                      │
  │  │  storageClass: postgres-storage│                                      │
  │  │  STATUS: Bound ✅              │                                      │
  │  └──────────────┬─────────────────┘                                      │
  │                 │ mounted into                                           │
  │                 ▼                                                         │
  │  StatefulSet: "postgres"                                                 │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
  │  │  Pod: postgres-0                                       │             │
  │  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │             │
  │  │  │  Container: postgres                              │  │             │
  │  │  │  image: postgres:15                               │  │             │
  │  │  │                                                    │  │             │
  │  │  │  /var/lib/postgresql/data  ◀── PVC mounted here   │  │             │
  │  │  │  ├── base/        (databases)                     │  │             │
  │  │  │  ├── global/      (cluster-wide tables)           │  │             │
  │  │  │  ├── pg_wal/      (write-ahead logs)              │  │             │
  │  │  │  └── postgresql.conf                              │  │             │
  │  │  │                                                    │  │             │
  │  │  │  Data survives:                                    │  │             │
  │  │  │  ✅ Container restart                              │  │             │
  │  │  │  ✅ Pod deletion                                   │  │             │
  │  │  │  ✅ StatefulSet scaling                            │  │             │
  │  │  │  ✅ Rolling updates                                │  │             │
  │  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │             │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-postgres-complete.yaml 
# ── 1. StorageClass ──
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: postgres-storage
provisioner: kubernetes.io/no-provisioner
reclaimPolicy: Retain                 # ◀── NEVER auto-delete DB data
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
---
# ── 2. PersistentVolume (for local/exam clusters) ──
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: postgres-pv
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Gi
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain
  storageClassName: postgres-storage
  hostPath:
    path: /data/postgres
    type: DirectoryOrCreate
---
# ── 3. StatefulSet with volumeClaimTemplates ──
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  serviceName: postgres               # ◀── headless service name
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
        env:
        - name: POSTGRES_DB
          value: anihpj_db
        - name: POSTGRES_USER
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: postgres-credentials
              key: username
        - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: postgres-credentials
              key: password
        - name: PGDATA                 # ◀── PostgreSQL data subdirectory
          value: /var/lib/postgresql/data/pgdata
        volumeMounts:
        - name: postgres-data          # ◀── matches volumeClaimTemplates
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 250m
            memory: 256Mi
          limits:
            cpu: "1"
            memory: 1Gi
  volumeClaimTemplates:                # ◀── auto-creates PVC per replica
  - metadata:
      name: postgres-data              # ◀── PVC name = postgres-data-postgres-0
    spec:
      accessModes:
      - ReadWriteOnce
      storageClassName: postgres-storage
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi
---
# ── 4. Headless Service for StatefulSet ──
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-production
spec:
  clusterIP: None                      # ◀── headless = direct pod DNS
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432
# DNS: postgres-0.postgres.anihpj-production.svc.cluster.local
Verify the complete setup 
# Apply the complete storage stack
kubectl apply -f anihpj-postgres-complete.yaml

# Verify StorageClass
kubectl get storageclass postgres-storage
# NAME               PROVISIONER                    RECLAIMPOLICY
# postgres-storage   kubernetes.io/no-provisioner   Retain

# Verify PV is bound
kubectl get pv postgres-pv
# NAME          CAPACITY   ACCESS   STATUS   CLAIM
# postgres-pv   10Gi       RWO      Bound    anihpj-production/postgres-data-postgres-0

# Verify PVC is bound
kubectl get pvc -n anihpj-production
# NAME                       STATUS   VOLUME        CAPACITY
# postgres-data-postgres-0   Bound    postgres-pv   10Gi

# Verify pod is running with mounted volume
kubectl describe pod postgres-0 -n anihpj-production | grep -A5 "Mounts:"
#     Mounts:
#       /var/lib/postgresql/data from postgres-data (rw)

# Test data persistence: kill the pod, check data survives
kubectl delete pod postgres-0 -n anihpj-production
# StatefulSet recreates postgres-0 → PVC re-mounts → data intact! ✅
🏋️ CKA Practice Scenario: Create PV and PVC from Scratch

Task: Create a PersistentVolume named exam-pv with 2Gi capacity, ReadWriteOnce, storageClass manual, backed by hostPath /mnt/data. Then create a PVC named exam-pvc requesting 1Gi from that PV. Verify binding.

CKA Solution 
# Step 1: Create PV
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: exam-pv
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 2Gi
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: manual
  hostPath:
    path: /mnt/data
EOF

# Step 2: Create PVC
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: exam-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: manual
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
EOF

# Step 3: Verify binding
kubectl get pv exam-pv
# STATUS: Bound   CLAIM: default/exam-pvc ✅

kubectl get pvc exam-pvc
# STATUS: Bound   VOLUME: exam-pv ✅

🔄 PersistentVolume State Machine — Complete Lifecycle
