│  │ $ apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y                            │   │
│  │ $ reboot     (if kernel patch requires reboot)                    │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  Step 3: VERIFY the node is healthy                                      │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Wait for node to come back online after reboot                    │   │
│  │ $ systemctl status kubelet       (is kubelet running?)            │   │
│  │ $ kubectl get nodes              (is node Ready?)                 │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                          │
│                               ▼                                          │
│  Step 4: UNCORDON the node                                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ $ kubectl uncordon worker01                                       │   │
│  │ Node is now schedulable — new pods can be placed here             │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                          │
│  ⏱️ pod-eviction-timeout = 5 minutes (default)                          │
│  If a node goes down WITHOUT drain, the controller-manager waits 5      │
│  minutes before marking pods as "Unknown" and rescheduling them.        │
│  Always DRAIN first to avoid this delay!                                 │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
os-upgrade-workflow.sh 
# === COMPLETE OS UPGRADE WORKFLOW ===

# FROM CONTROL PLANE:
# 1. Drain the node
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# 2. SSH to worker and perform upgrade
ssh worker01

# ON WORKER NODE:
apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y
# If kernel upgrade:
reboot

# Wait for node to come back...

# FROM CONTROL PLANE:
# 3. Verify node is healthy
kubectl get nodes
# worker01 should show Ready,SchedulingDisabled

# 4. Verify kubelet is running
ssh worker01 systemctl status kubelet

# 5. Make node schedulable again
kubectl uncordon worker01

# 6. Final verification
kubectl get nodes
# All nodes should show Ready (no SchedulingDisabled)

📊 Planned Drain vs Unplanned Node Failure

Scenario Pod Rescheduling Downtime
Planned: kubectl drain first Immediate — pods evicted and rescheduled instantly Minimal (graceful shutdown period only)
Unplanned: Node crashes After pod-eviction-timeout (default 5 minutes) 5+ minutes — pods stuck in Unknown state
Unplanned: Node power off After node becomes NotReady + eviction timeout 5+ minutes — no graceful shutdown
💡 Key Takeaway

Always kubectl drain before maintenance. It ensures pods are gracefully rescheduled immediately instead of waiting 5 minutes for the eviction timeout. Combined with a PDB, your application maintains availability throughout the process.

⏱️ Node Controller Timeline — Unplanned Node Failure
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   WHAT HAPPENS WHEN A NODE GOES DOWN                     │
│                   (without kubectl drain)                                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  TIME ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────►     │
│                                                                          │
│  t=0s        Node stops sending heartbeats to API server                │
│              kubelet stops updating node lease object                    │
│              Status: Node still shows "Ready"                            │
│                                                                          │
│  t=40s       node-monitor-grace-period expires (default: 40s)           │
│              ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│              │ Node Controller marks node as "NotReady"     │            │
│              │ kubectl get nodes → STATUS: NotReady         │            │
│              │ Pods still show "Running" (not evicted yet)  │            │
│              └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                          │
│  t=5m 40s    pod-eviction-timeout expires (default: 5 minutes)          │
│              ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│              │ Node Controller adds NoExecute taint         │            │
│              │ Taint-based eviction kicks in                │            │
│              │ Pods get tolerationSeconds (default: 300s)   │            │
│              │ Pod status → "Unknown"                       │            │
│              └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                          │
│  t=10m 40s   tolerationSeconds expires for pods                         │
│              ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│              │ Pods finally evicted and rescheduled         │            │
│              │ Deployment controller creates replacements   │            │
│              │ ⚠️ Total downtime: ~10+ minutes!            │            │
│              └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                          │
│  Compare with kubectl drain: pods rescheduled in ~15-30 seconds! ✅     │
│                                                                          │
│  KEY PARAMETERS (kube-controller-manager):                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ --node-monitor-grace-period=40s    Time before marking NotReady  │   │
│  │ --pod-eviction-timeout=5m0s        Time before evicting pods     │   │
│  │ --node-monitor-period=5s           How often node status checked │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔄 Multi-Node Rolling Maintenance Strategy (anihpj Production)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          ROLLING MAINTENANCE — 3 Worker Nodes, Zero Downtime             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  BEFORE: anihpj (3 replicas), PDB: minAvailable=2                       │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01   │  │  worker02   │  │  worker03   │                      │
│  │  anihpj-1 ✅│  │  anihpj-2 ✅│  │  anihpj-3 ✅│                     │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│                                                                          │
│  ROUND 1: Maintain worker01                                              │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01   │  │  worker02   │  │  worker03   │                      │
│  │  🔧 DRAIN  │  │  anihpj-2 ✅│  │  anihpj-3 ✅│                     │
│  │  upgrading  │  │  anihpj-1'✅│  │             │                      │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│  ✅ 3 pods running across 2 nodes — PDB satisfied                       │
│                                                                          │
│  ROUND 2: Uncordon worker01, drain worker02                              │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01   │  │  worker02   │  │  worker03   │                      │
│  │  anihpj-2'✅│  │  🔧 DRAIN  │  │  anihpj-3 ✅│                     │
│  │             │  │  upgrading  │  │  anihpj-1'✅│                      │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│  ✅ 3 pods running across 2 nodes — PDB satisfied                       │
│                                                                          │
│  ROUND 3: Uncordon worker02, drain worker03                              │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01   │  │  worker02   │  │  worker03   │                      │
│  │  anihpj-1 ✅│  │  anihpj-2 ✅│  │  🔧 DRAIN  │                     │
│  │             │  │  anihpj-3'✅│  │  upgrading  │                      │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│  ✅ 3 pods running across 2 nodes — PDB satisfied                       │
│                                                                          │
│  AFTER: All nodes upgraded, zero downtime achieved! 🎉                   │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                        │
│  │  worker01 ✅│  │  worker02 ✅│  │  worker03 ✅│                      │
│  │  upgraded   │  │  upgraded   │  │  upgraded   │                      │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                        │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
rolling-maintenance.sh 
# === MULTI-NODE ROLLING MAINTENANCE ===
# Ensure PDB is in place BEFORE starting

# Verify PDB
kubectl get pdb
# ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS should be ≥ 1

# Node list
NODES="worker01 worker02 worker03"

for NODE in $NODES; do
  echo "=== Maintaining $NODE ==="

  # 1. Drain
  kubectl drain $NODE --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
  echo "  Drained $NODE — pods rescheduled"

  # 2. SSH and upgrade (customize per environment)
  ssh $NODE "apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y && reboot"

  # 3. Wait for node to come back
  echo "  Waiting for $NODE to rejoin cluster..."
  until kubectl get node $NODE | grep -q " Ready"; do
    sleep 10
  done

  # 4. Uncordon
  kubectl uncordon $NODE
  echo "  $NODE back in service ✅"

  # 5. Brief pause for pods to stabilize
  sleep 30
done

echo "=== All nodes upgraded! ==="
kubectl get nodes

🚨 CKA Exam Gotchas — Node Maintenance

TrapWhat HappensCorrect Approach
Forgot to uncordon after maintenance Node stays SchedulingDisabled — new pods won't land on it Always kubectl uncordon <node> as your LAST step
Using kubectl delete node instead of drain Node removed from cluster — must rejoin with kubeadm Use kubectl drain for maintenance, never delete
Confused cordon vs drain Cordon only prevents NEW pods — existing pods stay running Drain = cordon + evict all pods. Use drain for maintenance
Draining control plane without thought If single control plane, you lose API server access! Control plane static pods can't be drained anyway — only user workloads

🔬 Practice Drill 30.1 — Drain and Uncordon a Node

⏱️ Target Time: 3 minutes
Scenario

Node worker02 needs emergency maintenance. Safely remove all workloads from the node, then after the maintenance is complete, make it schedulable again.

  1. Drain worker02, ignoring DaemonSet-managed pods
  2. Verify no user pods are running on worker02
  3. After maintenance, uncordon the node
  4. Verify the node is Ready and schedulable
Hint: Use --ignore-daemonsets with drain. DaemonSet pods (like kube-proxy) cannot be evicted — the flag tells drain to skip them. If pods use emptyDir, add --delete-emptydir-data.
View Solution 
# 1. Drain the node
kubectl drain worker02 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# 2. Verify no user pods on worker02
kubectl get pods -A -o wide | grep worker02
# Only DaemonSet pods should remain (kube-proxy, etc.)

# 3. Perform maintenance (OS upgrade, kernel patch, etc.)
# ...

# 4. Uncordon the node
kubectl uncordon worker02

# 5. Verify node is Ready and schedulable
kubectl get nodes
# worker02 should show Ready (no SchedulingDisabled)

🔬 Practice Drill 30.2 — Create a PodDisruptionBudget

⏱️ Target Time: 4 minutes
Scenario

Your anihpj deployment has 3 replicas with label app: anihpj. Create a PodDisruptionBudget named anihpj-pdb that ensures at least 2 pods are always available during voluntary disruptions.

  1. Create a PDB YAML with minAvailable: 2
  2. Apply the PDB to the cluster
  3. Verify the PDB shows correct allowed disruptions
  4. Test by draining a node — observe the PDB in action
Hint: The PDB's selector.matchLabels must match the pod labels from your Deployment. Use apiVersion: policy/v1 and choose either minAvailable or maxUnavailable — not both.
View Solution 
# anihpj-pdb.yaml
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pdb
  namespace: default
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
# Apply
kubectl apply -f anihpj-pdb.yaml

# Verify
kubectl get pdb
# NAME         MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
# anihpj-pdb   2               N/A               1                     10s

# Test — drain will respect PDB
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets
# Drain will evict pods ONE AT A TIME, waiting for each to reschedule
# before evicting the next, ensuring at least 2 are always running

🔬 Practice Drill 30.3 — Complete OS Upgrade Workflow

⏱️ Target Time: 5 minutes
Scenario

Worker node worker01 needs a kernel upgrade. Perform the complete maintenance workflow: drain, upgrade, verify, and restore service.

  1. Drain worker01
  2. SSH to worker01 and simulate an OS upgrade
  3. Verify kubelet is running and the node is healthy
  4. Uncordon and verify pods can be scheduled again
Hint: After a reboot, kubelet should start automatically (it's a systemd service). If it doesn't, check with systemctl status kubelet and start it with systemctl start kubelet.
View Solution 
# From control plane:
kubectl drain worker01 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# SSH to worker:
ssh worker01
apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y
# If kernel upgrade: reboot
# Wait for node to come back...

# From control plane — verify:
kubectl get nodes
# worker01 should show Ready,SchedulingDisabled

# Check kubelet
ssh worker01 systemctl status kubelet

# Uncordon
kubectl uncordon worker01

# Verify
kubectl get nodes
# All nodes Ready, no SchedulingDisabled

✅ Chapter 30 — Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get nodes — All nodes Ready, no SchedulingDisabled after maintenance
  • kubectl get pods -A -o wide — Pods distributed across available nodes
  • kubectl get pdb — PDBs show correct ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS count
  • kubectl describe pdb <name> — PDB selector matches target pods
  • kubectl drain <node> --dry-run=client — Preview what drain would do
  • systemctl status kubelet — kubelet running on nodes after reboot

🔴 Part 9: Troubleshooting Chapters 31-34

Chapter 31: Debugging Pods and Containers

Pod lifecycle, status codes, debugging toolkit, essential debug commands, and common troubleshooting scenarios. This is the heaviest CKA exam domain at 30% — master these skills.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔍 Debug 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Pod lifecycle phases — Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, Unknown
  • ✅ Container status codes and what each exit code means
  • ✅ The systematic debugging toolkit — describe, logs, exec, events
  • ✅ Ephemeral debug containers for distroless images
  • ✅ Real-world troubleshooting scenarios with anihpj Django app
🎓 CKA Relevance
Troubleshooting 30%
🎯 Troubleshooting is 30% of CKA — The Heaviest Domain

Every CKA exam includes multiple debugging tasks. You must have these commands in muscle memory:

  • kubectl describe pod <name> — always check Events at the bottom
  • kubectl logs <pod> --previous — see why a crashed container failed
  • kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh — shell into a running container
  • kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' — cluster-wide event timeline

31.1 Pod Lifecycle and Status Codes

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Understanding pod phases and container states is the foundation of all Kubernetes troubleshooting. When something breaks, the status tells you where to look.

Pod Lifecycle — From Creation to Termination

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        POD LIFECYCLE FLOW                                     │
│                                                                              │
│  kubectl apply ─▶ API Server ─▶ Scheduler ─▶ kubelet ─▶ Container Runtime   │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌───────────┐             │
│  │ Pending │────▶│ Running │────▶│Succeeded│     │  Failed   │             │
│  │         │     │         │──┐  │ (exit 0)│     │(exit ≠ 0) │             │
│  └─────────┘     └─────────┘  │  └─────────┘     └───────────┘             │
│       │               │      │                        ▲                     │
│       │               │      └────────────────────────┘                     │
│       ▼               ▼                                                     │
│  ┌─────────┐     ┌──────────────┐                                          │
│  │ Unknown │     │CrashLoopBack │  ← Container keeps crashing              │
│  │(node    │     │    Off       │     and Kubernetes keeps restarting       │
│  │ lost)   │     │              │     it with exponential backoff           │
│  └─────────┘     └──────────────┘     (10s, 20s, 40s, ... 5min max)        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Pod Phases

Phase Meaning What To Do
Pending Accepted by API server but not yet running — scheduling, image pull, or resource wait kubectl describe pod → check Events
Running At least one container is running, starting, or restarting Check if pod is Ready (readiness probe passing)
Succeeded All containers terminated with exit code 0 Normal for Jobs and CronJobs
Failed All containers terminated, at least one with non-zero exit kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Unknown Cannot determine pod state — node communication lost Check node status: kubectl get nodes

Container States — What Each Status Means

Status Common Cause Debug Command
Waiting: ContainerCreating Image being pulled or volume mounting kubectl describe pod → Events
Waiting: ErrImagePull Wrong image name, tag doesn't exist, or registry auth failed kubectl describe pod → check image field
Waiting: ImagePullBackOff Same as ErrImagePull but after repeated failures (with backoff) Fix image reference or add imagePullSecrets
Waiting: CrashLoopBackOff Container starts then crashes repeatedly kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Waiting: CreateContainerError SecurityContext issue, ConfigMap/Secret missing kubectl describe pod → Events
Running but not Ready Readiness probe failing — app not serving traffic kubectl describe pod → Conditions section
Terminated: OOMKilled Container exceeded memory limit (exit code 137) Increase resources.limits.memory
Terminated: Error Container exited with non-zero code (app crash) kubectl logs <pod> --previous
💡 Exit Code Quick Reference

Exit 0 = Success (normal completion). Exit 1 = Application error. Exit 137 = OOM Killed (128 + signal 9). Exit 139 = Segmentation fault. Exit 143 = Graceful termination (128 + signal 15 SIGTERM).

Restart Policies — How Kubernetes Handles Failures

Restart Policy Behavior Used By
Always (default) Restart container regardless of exit code — with exponential backoff (10s, 20s, 40s, ... 5min max) Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets
OnFailure Restart only if exit code ≠ 0. Exit 0 = done. Jobs (retry on failure)
Never Never restart, regardless of exit code Jobs (one-shot), debug pods

Restart Backoff Timeline — CrashLoopBackOff Explained

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  restartPolicy: Always — Exponential Backoff                                 │
│                                                                              │
│  Time:  0s     10s     30s      70s       150s        310s       600s       │
│         │       │       │        │          │           │          │         │
│  Run:   ▓▓▓X   ▓▓X    ▓▓▓X    ▓▓X       ▓▓▓X       ▓▓X        ▓▓▓        │
│         crash   crash  crash   crash     crash      crash      success?    │
│                                                                              │
│  Wait:  ──10s── ─20s── ──40s── ───80s─── ──160s──── ──300s────             │
│                                                                              │
│  Status:  CrashLoopBackOff ────────────────────────────▶  Running           │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 Max backoff = 5 minutes (300s). Resets after pod runs for 10+ minutes.  │
│  💡 The STATUS alternates between "CrashLoopBackOff" and "Error".           │
│  💡 Restart count = shown in kubectl get pods RESTARTS column.              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

31.2 The Debugging Toolkit

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

When a pod isn't working, follow this decision tree systematically. Never guess — always gather evidence first.

🔍 Pod Debugging Decision Tree

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 POD NOT WORKING? Follow this tree:                         │
│                                                                            │
│  $ kubectl get pods -o wide                                                │
│       │                                                                    │
│       ├── STATUS: Pending?                                                 │
│       │    └── $ kubectl describe pod <name>                               │
│       │         ├── "Insufficient cpu/memory"                              │
│       │         │    → Scale cluster or adjust resource requests           │
│       │         ├── "no nodes match pod's node selector"                   │
│       │         │    → Check nodeSelector/nodeAffinity labels              │
│       │         ├── "node(s) had taint... pod didn't tolerate"             │
│       │         │    → Add toleration to pod spec OR remove taint          │
│       │         └── "persistentvolumeclaim not found"                      │
│       │              → Create the missing PVC or fix PVC name              │
│       │                                                                    │
│       ├── STATUS: CrashLoopBackOff?                                        │
│       │    └── $ kubectl logs <pod> --previous                             │
│       │         ├── Application error / stack trace                        │
│       │         │    → Fix app code or configuration                       │
│       │         ├── "connection refused" / "no such host"                  │
│       │         │    → Dependent service not ready (DB, Redis)             │
│       │         ├── Missing config/secret reference                        │
│       │         │    → Check ConfigMap/Secret exists & mounted correctly   │
│       │         └── Permission denied                                      │
│       │              → Check SecurityContext & file permissions            │
│       │                                                                    │
│       ├── STATUS: ImagePullBackOff?                                        │
│       │    └── $ kubectl describe pod <name>                               │
│       │         ├── Wrong image name/tag                                   │
│       │         │    → Fix spec.containers[].image                         │
│       │         └── Private registry auth failed                           │
│       │              → Add imagePullSecrets to pod spec                    │
│       │                                                                    │
│       └── STATUS: Running but not working?                                 │
│            ├── $ kubectl logs <pod>           ← application logs           │
│            ├── $ kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh  ← shell in            │
│            ├── $ kubectl port-forward <pod> 8080:80 ← test locally        │
│            └── Check readiness/liveness probes in describe output          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ Common Debugging Mistakes

  • ✗ Wrong Immediately editing the pod YAML without checking logs first
  • ✓ Right Always run describe → logs → events before making changes
  • ✗ Wrong Using kubectl logs <pod> for a crashed container
  • ✓ Right Using kubectl logs <pod> --previous to see the last crashed container's output
  • ✗ Wrong Forgetting to check the Events section at the bottom of describe
  • ✓ Right Scroll to the very bottom — Events show scheduling/pulling/startup timeline

31.3 Essential Debug Commands

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

These are the commands you'll use on every single troubleshooting task in the CKA exam. Memorize them.

🚀 The Debug Command Sequence

Follow this order — each step narrows down the problem:

Step Command What You Learn
1. Overview kubectl get pods -o wide Pod status, restarts, node placement, IP
2. Details kubectl describe pod <name> Config, conditions, events timeline
3. App logs kubectl logs <pod> Application stdout/stderr output
4. Prev logs kubectl logs <pod> --previous Output from last crashed container
5. Shell in kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh Interactive debugging inside container
6. Events kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' Cluster-wide event timeline
7. Resources kubectl top pod <name> Current CPU/memory usage
Essential Debug Commands — Complete Reference 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. GET — Overview of pod status
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get pods                           # All pods in current namespace
kubectl get pods -o wide                   # + Node, IP columns
kubectl get pods -A                        # All namespaces
kubectl get pods --show-labels             # See pod labels
kubectl get pods -w                        # Watch for changes in real-time

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2. DESCRIBE — Detailed pod information + Events
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl describe pod <name>                # Full details + Events at bottom
# KEY SECTIONS TO CHECK:
#   - Status / Conditions     → Is it ready? Why not?
#   - Containers → State      → Waiting/Running/Terminated + reason
#   - Events (bottom)         → Scheduling, pulling, startup timeline

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. LOGS — Application output
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl logs <pod>                         # Current container stdout
kubectl logs <pod> --previous              # Previous crashed container ⭐
kubectl logs <pod> -f                      # Follow / stream live
kubectl logs <pod> --tail=100              # Last 100 lines only
kubectl logs <pod> --since=1h              # Last hour only
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container>          # Specific container (multi-container)
kubectl logs <pod> --all-containers        # All containers in the pod

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 4. EXEC — Shell into a running container
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh          # Interactive shell (Alpine/BusyBox)
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/bash        # Bash shell (Ubuntu/Debian)
kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /etc/resolv.conf # Run single command
kubectl exec <pod> -- env                  # Check environment variables
kubectl exec <pod> -- wget -qO- http://svc # Test connectivity from inside

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 5. EVENTS — Cluster-wide timeline
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get events --sort-by='.metadata.creationTimestamp'
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=Failed
kubectl get events -n kube-system          # Control plane events

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 6. RESOURCE USAGE — CPU and memory
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl top pods                           # CPU/memory per pod
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu             # Sort by CPU usage
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory          # Sort by memory usage
kubectl top nodes                          # Node-level resource usage

✅ Verify Your Debug Skills

  • Can you get previous container logs? → kubectl logs <pod> --previous
  • Can you check events sorted by time? → kubectl get events --sort-by='.metadata.creationTimestamp'
  • Can you shell into a specific container? → kubectl exec -it <pod> -c <container> -- /bin/sh

Ephemeral Debug Containers — Debugging Distroless Images

💡 When kubectl exec Won't Work

Many production images are distroless — they don't include a shell (/bin/sh), curl, or any debug tools. Ephemeral containers let you inject a debug container into a running pod without restarting it.

Ephemeral Debug Containers 
# Attach a debug container to a running pod
kubectl debug -it <pod-name> --image=busybox:1.36 --target=<container>

# Debug with a full toolkit (nicolaka/netshoot has curl, dig, tcpdump, etc.)
kubectl debug -it <pod-name> --image=nicolaka/netshoot --target=app

# Create a copy of the pod with a debug container (doesn't affect original)
kubectl debug <pod-name> -it --image=busybox --copy-to=debug-pod

# Debug a node directly (creates a pod with host namespaces)
kubectl debug node/<node-name> -it --image=busybox

Ephemeral Containers vs kubectl exec

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                              │
│  REGULAR CONTAINER (distroless)        EPHEMERAL DEBUG CONTAINER             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────────────┐           │
│  │  Your App (Go binary)   │          │  Debug Tools            │           │
│  │                         │          │  • /bin/sh              │           │
│  │  ❌ No /bin/sh          │  ◄──────▶│  • curl, wget           │           │
│  │  ❌ No curl, dig        │  shares  │  • dig, nslookup        │           │
│  │  ❌ No package manager  │  process │  • tcpdump, strace      │           │
│  │                         │  space   │  • ps, netstat          │           │
│  └─────────────────────────┘          └─────────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 The ephemeral container shares the same PID namespace, network,         │
│     and IPC namespace — you can see the app's processes, files, and         │
│     network connections without modifying the original container.            │
│                                                                              │
│  ⚠️  Ephemeral containers CANNOT be removed once added — they stay          │
│     until the pod is deleted. They are never restarted.                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

31.4 Debugging anihpj — Common Scenarios

Let's apply the debugging toolkit to real-world scenarios using our anihpj Django application. Each scenario follows the identify → gather → analyze → fix → verify flow.

Scenario 1: anihpj Pod in CrashLoopBackOff

🔴 CrashLoopBackOff — Database Connection Refused

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP-BY-STEP WALKTHROUGH                                               │
│                                                                         │
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     NAME                  READY   STATUS             RESTARTS   AGE     │
│     anihpj-web-7d4f8c5    0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5          10m     │
│     anihpj-db-0           1/1     Running            0          10m     │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check logs from the PREVIOUS crash:                        │
│     $ kubectl logs anihpj-web-7d4f8c5 -n anihpj --previous             │
│                                                                         │
│     django.db.utils.OperationalError:                                   │
│       could not connect to server: Connection refused               ◄── │
│       Is the server running on host "anihpj-db" and                     │
│       accepting TCP/IP connections on port 5432?                        │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE:                                                            │
│     → The web pod starts before the DB pod is ready                     │
│     → Django tries to connect to PostgreSQL → fails → crashes           │
│     → Kubernetes restarts it → same thing → CrashLoopBackOff            │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Add an init container that waits for DB:                      │
│     initContainers:                                                     │
│     - name: wait-for-db                                                 │
│       image: busybox                                                    │
│       command: ['sh', '-c',                                             │
│         'until nc -z anihpj-db 5432; do sleep 2; done']                 │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj -w                                     │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   0/1   Init:0/1   0   5s    ← init running        │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   1/1   Running    0   15s   ← ✅ success          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 2: anihpj Pod Stuck in Pending

🟡 Pending — Insufficient Memory

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP-BY-STEP WALKTHROUGH                                               │
│                                                                         │
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     NAME                  READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE              │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx       0/1     Pending   0          5m               │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl describe pod anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj                    │
│     ...                                                                 │
│     Events:                                                             │
│       Warning  FailedScheduling  0/2 nodes available:                   │
│         2 Insufficient memory.  ◄── The clue is always in Events!       │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE:                                                            │
│     → Pod requests 2Gi memory but no node has that much free            │
│     → Check current requests vs capacity:                               │
│       $ kubectl describe node worker01 | grep -A5 "Allocated"           │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Reduce the request to fit available resources:                │
│     resources:                                                          │
│       requests:                                                         │
│         memory: "512Mi"    ← was 2Gi, reduced to fit                    │
│         cpu: "250m"                                                     │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   1/1   Running   0   30s   ← ✅ scheduled         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 3: anihpj Running but 502 Errors

🟢 Running but Broken — Django Not Binding Correctly

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP-BY-STEP WALKTHROUGH                                               │
│                                                                         │
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   1/1   Running   0   5m   ← Looks fine!           │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get svc -n anihpj                                         │
│     anihpj-svc   ClusterIP   10.96.45.12   80/TCP   ← Service exists   │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl exec test-pod -- wget -qO- http://anihpj-svc.anihpj      │
│     → 502 Bad Gateway                                                   │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Shell into the pod:                                        │
│     $ kubectl exec -it anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj -- /bin/bash           │
│     root@anihpj:/app$ python manage.py runserver                        │
│     → Listening on http://127.0.0.1:8000/   ◄── PROBLEM!               │
│     → Django binds to localhost only!                                    │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE:                                                            │
│     → Django listens on 127.0.0.1 (localhost)                           │
│     → Service sends traffic to the container's pod IP                   │
│     → Connection refused because Django isn't listening on 0.0.0.0      │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Bind to all interfaces:                                       │
│     command: ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]       │
│     Also add to settings.py:                                            │
│       ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*']    # or specific hostnames                  │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl exec test-pod -- wget -qO- http://anihpj-svc.anihpj      │
│     → 200 OK ✅                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🏋️ Practice Drill: Debug These Pods

1
Create a pod with a wrong image name: kubectl run broken --image=nginxxx:latest

Debug: What status? What does describe show?

2
Create a pod that crashes immediately: kubectl run crasher --image=busybox -- /bin/sh -c "exit 1"

Debug: Use --previous flag. What exit code?

3
Create a pod with impossible resource request: kubectl run toobig --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml | sed 's/resources: {}/resources:\n requests:\n memory: "100Gi"/' | kubectl apply -f -

Debug: Why is it Pending? Check describe Events.

Chapter 32: Troubleshooting Nodes and Cluster Components

Node Not Ready diagnosis, kubelet troubleshooting, control plane component recovery, and networking issues. These skills are critical for the CKA exam.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔧 Troubleshoot 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Diagnosing and recovering Node NotReady conditions
  • ✅ kubelet troubleshooting — service status, logs, configuration
  • ✅ Control plane component recovery — API server, scheduler, controller-manager, etcd
  • ✅ Network troubleshooting — DNS, Services, kube-proxy, CNI plugins
🎓 CKA Relevance
Troubleshooting 30% Cluster Architecture 25%

32.1 Node Not Ready — Diagnosis

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

A node in NotReady state means the kubelet has stopped reporting to the API server. Your job is to find out why and fix it.

🔍 Node Troubleshooting Flowchart

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  $ kubectl get nodes                                                         │
│  NAME           STATUS     ROLES           AGE   VERSION                     │
│  controlplane   Ready      control-plane   30d   v1.31.0                    │
│  worker01       NotReady   <none>          30d   v1.31.0  ◄── PROBLEM      │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ▼                                                                      │
│  $ kubectl describe node worker01                                            │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ├── Conditions Section:                                                │
│       │   ┌──────────────────┬────────┬──────────────────────────────┐       │
│       │   │ Condition        │ Status │ What It Means                │       │
│       │   ├──────────────────┼────────┼──────────────────────────────┤       │
│       │   │ Ready            │ False  │ kubelet not reporting        │       │
│       │   │ MemoryPressure   │ True   │ Node running out of RAM     │       │
│       │   │ DiskPressure     │ True   │ Node running out of disk    │       │
│       │   │ PIDPressure      │ True   │ Too many processes          │       │
│       │   │ NetworkUnavail.  │ True   │ CNI plugin not working      │       │
│       │   └──────────────────┴────────┴──────────────────────────────┘       │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ▼                                                                      │
│  SSH into the node: $ ssh worker01                                           │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ├── $ systemctl status kubelet                                         │
│       │    ├── active (running) → kubelet is up, check network              │
│       │    └── inactive (dead)  → kubelet crashed or was stopped            │
│       │                                                                      │
│       ├── $ journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -50                      │
│       │    └── Look for error messages about certificates, config, etc.     │
│       │                                                                      │
│       └── Common Fixes:                                                      │
│            ├── Kubelet stopped   → systemctl start kubelet                   │
│            ├── Kubelet disabled  → systemctl enable --now kubelet            │
│            ├── Container runtime → systemctl restart containerd              │
│            ├── Swap enabled      → swapoff -a                                │
│            └── Wrong config      → check /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml       │
│                                                                              │
│  VERIFY: $ kubectl get nodes   → worker01 should show Ready                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ Node Conditions vs Node Status

Status (Ready/NotReady) is the overall health. Conditions (MemoryPressure, DiskPressure, etc.) tell you why a node is unhealthy. A node is Ready only when ALL pressure conditions are False and kubelet is communicating with the API server.

Node Diagnosis Commands 
# Step 1: Get node status overview
kubectl get nodes -o wide

# Step 2: Describe the problematic node
kubectl describe node worker01

# Key areas to check in the output:
# - Conditions     → Ready, MemoryPressure, DiskPressure
# - Capacity       → Total CPU, memory, pods
# - Allocatable    → Available for pods
# - Allocated      → Currently used by pods
# - Events         → Recent node events

# Step 3: SSH to node and check kubelet
ssh worker01
systemctl status kubelet
journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -l | tail -50

# Step 4: Check container runtime
systemctl status containerd
crictl ps -a                    # List all containers on the node

# Step 5: Check disk and memory
df -h                           # Disk usage
free -m                         # Memory usage
top                             # Process and CPU overview

32.2 Troubleshooting kubelet

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

kubelet is the only component that runs as a systemd service (not a static pod). It's the node agent — if kubelet is down, the node goes NotReady.

kubelet — The Node Agent Architecture

┌──────────────────────────── Worker Node ─────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                          │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │                    kubelet (systemd service)                 │        │
│  │                                                             │        │
│  │  📡 Reports to API Server every 10s (node heartbeat)        │        │
│  │  📋 Watches for pod assignments from Scheduler              │        │
│  │  🔄 Manages pod lifecycle via Container Runtime             │        │
│  │  💉 Runs health probes (liveness, readiness, startup)       │        │
│  │  📊 Collects metrics for Metrics Server                     │        │
│  │                                                             │        │
│  │  Config: /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml                       │        │
│  │  Certs:  /var/lib/kubelet/pki/                              │        │
│  │  Logs:   journalctl -u kubelet                              │        │
│  └────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                           │                                              │
│                           ▼                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐                       │
│  │         Container Runtime (containerd)        │                       │
│  │                                              │                       │
│  │  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐        │                       │
│  │  │Pod A│  │Pod B│  │Pod C│  │Pod D│        │                       │
│  │  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘        │                       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘                       │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Common kubelet Issues and Fixes

Problem Symptom Diagnosis Fix
kubelet stopped Node goes NotReady systemctl status kubelet → inactive systemctl start kubelet
kubelet disabled Doesn't start after reboot systemctl is-enabled kubelet → disabled systemctl enable kubelet
Wrong config kubelet crashes repeatedly journalctl -u kubelet → config errors Fix /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
Certificate expired "x509: certificate has expired" openssl x509 -in cert -noout -dates kubeadm certs renew all
Cannot reach API server "connection refused" in kubelet logs Check network, firewall, API server IP Fix network/firewall or API server address in kubelet config
Swap enabled kubelet refuses to start free -m shows swap swapoff -a and remove from /etc/fstab
kubelet Troubleshooting — Step by Step 
# ═══ On the problematic node (SSH in first) ═══

# 1. Check kubelet status
systemctl status kubelet
# Look for: active (running) vs inactive (dead) vs failed

# 2. Check kubelet logs for errors
journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager | tail -50
journalctl -u kubelet -f                    # Follow live

# 3. If kubelet is stopped, start it
systemctl start kubelet
systemctl enable kubelet                    # Persist across reboots

# 4. Check kubelet configuration
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Verify: clusterDNS, clusterDomain, authentication/authorization

# 5. Check kubelet certificate
openssl x509 -in /var/lib/kubelet/pki/kubelet-client-current.pem \
  -noout -text | grep -A2 "Validity"

# 6. Restart kubelet after any config change
systemctl daemon-reload                     # If service file changed
systemctl restart kubelet

# 7. Verify from control plane
kubectl get nodes                           # Should show Ready

⚠️ kubelet Gotchas on the CKA Exam

  • ✗ Wrong Looking for kubelet in kubectl get pods -n kube-system — kubelet is NOT a pod
  • ✓ Right Check kubelet via systemctl status kubelet on the node itself
  • ✗ Wrong Forgetting systemctl daemon-reload after editing service files
  • ✓ Right Always run daemon-reload before restart if you modified a systemd unit
  • ✗ Wrong Restarting kubelet without checking WHY it crashed
  • ✓ Right Read journalctl -u kubelet first to understand the root cause

32.3 Troubleshooting Control Plane Components

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Control plane components run as static pods managed by kubelet. Their manifests live in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/. If a component is broken, check its manifest and logs.

Control Plane Components — Where They Live

┌──────────────────────── Control Plane Node ──────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                          │
│  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/           ← kubelet watches this directory   │
│  ├── kube-apiserver.yaml              ← API Server static pod           │
│  ├── kube-controller-manager.yaml     ← Controller Manager static pod   │
│  ├── kube-scheduler.yaml              ← Scheduler static pod            │
│  └── etcd.yaml                        ← etcd static pod                 │
│                                                                          │
│  How static pods work:                                                   │
│  ┌──────────┐    watches    ┌────────────────────┐                      │
│  │ kubelet  │ ──────────▶  │ /etc/kubernetes/   │                      │
│  │          │    dir for    │    manifests/      │                      │
│  │          │    changes    │                    │                      │
│  └────┬─────┘               └────────────────────┘                      │
│       │ creates/restarts                                                 │
│       ▼                                                                  │
│  ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐                   │
│  │API Server│ │Controller│ │Scheduler │ │  etcd   │                   │
│  │   Pod    │ │Mgr Pod   │ │   Pod    │ │   Pod   │                   │
│  └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘                   │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚠️ If kubelet is down, static pods don't restart!                      │
│  ⚠️ If manifest has syntax error, static pod won't start!              │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Control Plane Troubleshooting Commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. CHECK — Are control plane pods running?
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
# Expected: kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, etcd

# 2. CHECK — Control plane pod logs
kubectl logs kube-apiserver-controlplane -n kube-system
kubectl logs kube-scheduler-controlplane -n kube-system
kubectl logs kube-controller-manager-controlplane -n kube-system
kubectl logs etcd-controlplane -n kube-system

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. IF API SERVER IS DOWN — kubectl won't work!
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Use container runtime directly:
crictl ps -a                               # List all containers
crictl ps -a | grep kube-apiserver         # Find API server container
crictl logs <container-id>                 # Read its logs

# 4. CHECK — Static pod manifests
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml

# 5. FIX — Edit manifest, kubelet auto-restarts the pod
vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml
# kubelet detects change → restarts the static pod automatically

# 6. VERIFY
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl get componentstatuses             # Quick cluster health check
💡 CKA Pro Tip: When API Server Is Down

If the API server won't start, kubectl doesn't work at all. You must use crictl (the container runtime CLI) to check container status and logs directly. This is a common CKA exam scenario — they break the API server manifest and you need to fix it.

🏋️ Practice: Break and Fix the Control Plane

1
Break the scheduler: sudo sed -i 's/--leader-elect=true/--leader-elect=BROKEN/' /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml

Watch: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -w — scheduler pod will restart/crash

2
Diagnose: kubectl logs kube-scheduler-controlplane -n kube-system

Find the error message pointing to the invalid flag

3
Fix: Edit the manifest back to --leader-elect=true

kubelet detects the change and restarts the scheduler automatically

32.4 Troubleshooting Networking

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Network problems manifest as pods that can't reach services, DNS failures, or external traffic not arriving. Systematic diagnosis is key.

Network Troubleshooting — Layer by Layer

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NETWORKING TROUBLESHOOTING LAYERS                                           │
│                                                                              │
│  Layer 1: POD-TO-POD                                                         │
│  ┌──────────┐              ┌──────────┐                                     │
│  │  Pod A   │──── CNI ────▶│  Pod B   │                                     │
│  │ 10.244.1 │   network    │ 10.244.2 │                                     │
│  └──────────┘              └──────────┘                                     │
│  Debug: kubectl exec podA -- ping <podB-IP>                                 │
│  Fix:   Check CNI plugin pods (Calico/Weave/Flannel) running in kube-system │
│                                                                              │
│  Layer 2: POD-TO-SERVICE                                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  ┌──────────┐                             │
│  │  Pod A   │─▶│  Service      │─▶│  Pod B   │                             │
│  │          │  │  (ClusterIP)  │  │(endpoint)│                             │
│  └──────────┘  └───────────────┘  └──────────┘                             │
│  Debug: kubectl get endpoints <svc>   ← Are there endpoints?               │
│  Fix:   Service selector must match pod labels                               │
│                                                                              │
│  Layer 3: DNS RESOLUTION                                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                              │
│  │  Pod A   │─▶│ CoreDNS  │─▶│ Service IP   │                              │
│  │nslookup  │  │(kube-dns)│  │  resolved    │                              │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘                              │
│  Debug: kubectl exec pod -- nslookup <svc-name>                             │
│  Fix:   Check CoreDNS pods, ConfigMap, and kube-dns Service                  │
│                                                                              │
│  Layer 4: EXTERNAL ACCESS                                                    │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐                   │
│  │ External │─▶│ NodePort │─▶│ Service  │─▶│  Pod     │                   │
│  │  Client  │  │  :30080  │  │ ClusterIP│  │(backend) │                   │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘                   │
│  Debug: curl http://<node-ip>:<nodeport>                                    │
│  Fix:   Check firewall rules, kube-proxy, service type                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Network Debugging Commands 
# ═══ SERVICE NOT RESPONDING ═══
# Step 1: Check endpoints — are pods registered?
kubectl get endpoints <service-name>
# If <none>: service selector doesn't match any pod labels!

# Step 2: Compare service selector with pod labels
kubectl get svc <service-name> -o yaml | grep -A5 selector
kubectl get pods --show-labels

# ═══ DNS NOT WORKING ═══
# Step 1: Test DNS from inside a pod
kubectl run dnstest --image=busybox --rm -it --restart=Never -- \
  nslookup kubernetes.default

# Step 2: Check CoreDNS pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Step 3: Check CoreDNS ConfigMap for typos
kubectl get cm coredns -n kube-system -o yaml

# Step 4: Check kube-dns Service ClusterIP
kubectl get svc kube-dns -n kube-system
# This IP should match /etc/resolv.conf inside pods

# ═══ CNI PLUGIN ISSUES ═══
# Check CNI plugin pods (Calico, Weave, Flannel, etc.)
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -i "calico\|weave\|flannel\|cilium"
kubectl get ds -n kube-system            # CNI typically runs as DaemonSet

# ═══ KUBE-PROXY ISSUES ═══
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep kube-proxy
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
# kube-proxy manages iptables rules for Service → Pod routing

✅ Verify Your Node & Cluster Troubleshooting Skills

  • Can you check a node's kubelet status? → ssh node; systemctl status kubelet
  • Can you view static pod manifests? → ls /etc/kubernetes/manifests/
  • Can you debug when API server is down? → crictl ps -a; crictl logs <id>
  • Can you test DNS from a pod? → kubectl exec pod -- nslookup svc-name
  • Can you check if service has endpoints? → kubectl get endpoints svc-name

kube-proxy Modes — How Service Routing Works

Mode How It Works Performance Check Command
iptables (default) Creates iptables rules for each Service → Pod mapping. Kernel-level packet routing. Good — random pod selection. Scales to ~5K services. iptables -t nat -L -n | grep <service-clusterip>
ipvs Uses IPVS kernel module with hash tables. More efficient routing algorithms. Better — supports round-robin, least-conn, etc. Scales to 10K+ services. ipvsadm -Ln
nftables (v1.31+) Successor to iptables using nftables kernel subsystem. More efficient rule processing. Best — modern kernel-level routing with better rule optimization. nft list ruleset
💡 Diagnosing kube-proxy Issues

If services exist but traffic doesn't reach pods (and endpoints ARE populated), the problem may be kube-proxy. Check: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy. If kube-proxy is down, iptables rules become stale and services stop routing to new pods.

Chapter 33: Logging and Monitoring

Application logging, cluster-level logging architecture, Metrics Server, and monitoring resource usage. Essential for debugging and capacity planning.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~1 hour 📊 Monitoring 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Application logging with kubectl logs — all flags and patterns
  • ✅ Cluster-level logging architecture — node agents, sidecar containers
  • ✅ Metrics Server installation and resource monitoring
  • ✅ Monitoring resource usage — kubectl top, capacity planning
🎓 CKA Relevance
Troubleshooting 30%

33.1 Application Logging

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Containers write to stdout/stderr, and the container runtime captures these streams as log files on the node. kubectl logs retrieves this output.

📋 kubectl logs — Complete Reference

Master every flag for the CKA exam:

Command Purpose When to Use
kubectl logs <pod> Current container stdout/stderr First check for running pods
kubectl logs <pod> --previous Previous crashed container output CrashLoopBackOff debugging
kubectl logs <pod> -f Follow / stream live Watch realtime during testing
kubectl logs <pod> --tail=100 Last N lines only Quick check without scrolling
kubectl logs <pod> --since=1h Logs from last hour Recent errors only
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> Specific container Multi-container pods (sidecars)
kubectl logs <pod> --all-containers All containers in a pod Sidecar/init container logs
kubectl logs -l app=name All pods matching label Deployments with multiple replicas
Application Logging Examples 
# Basic logs
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj

# Previous crashed container ⭐ (CKA critical)
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj --previous

# Follow live (useful during testing)
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj -f

# Multi-container pod: specify the container
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj -c django
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj -c nginx-sidecar

# All containers at once
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj --all-containers

# Logs from all pods with label app=anihpj
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj -n anihpj --tail=50

# Combine flags: last 100 lines from last hour, follow live
kubectl logs anihpj-web-xxxx --tail=100 --since=1h -f
📖 Where Do Container Logs Actually Live?

On the node, container logs are stored at:
/var/log/containers/<pod-name>_<namespace>_<container>-<id>.log
/var/log/pods/<namespace>_<pod-name>_<uid>/<container>/
These are symlinks managed by the container runtime. kubectl logs reads these files via kubelet.

33.2 Cluster-Level Logging

Node-level logging only keeps logs on individual nodes. For production, you need a centralized logging architecture — a DaemonSet log agent that ships logs to a central store.

Kubernetes Logging Architecture — From Container to Central Store

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  KUBERNETES LOGGING ARCHITECTURE                             │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────── Worker Node 1 ─────────────────────────────┐                 │
│  │                                                         │                 │
│  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐             │                 │
│  │  │  Pod A   │  │  Pod B   │  │  Pod C   │             │                 │
│  │  │ stdout→  │  │ stdout→  │  │ stdout→  │             │                 │
│  │  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘             │                 │
│  │       │              │              │                   │                 │
│  │       ▼              ▼              ▼                   │                 │
│  │  /var/log/containers/*.log                              │                 │
│  │  /var/log/pods/<namespace>_<pod>/                       │                 │
│  │       │                                                 │                 │
│  │       ▼                                                 │                 │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐                       │                 │
│  │  │ 📋 DaemonSet Log Agent     │   fluentd /            │                 │
│  │  │    (1 per node)             │   filebeat /           │                 │
│  │  │                             │   fluent-bit           │                 │
│  │  └──────────────┬──────────────┘                       │                 │
│  └─────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┘                 │
│                    │                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────┼── Worker Node 2 ──────────────────────┐                │
│  │  (same pattern) │                                        │                │
│  │  Pods → /var/log/ → DaemonSet Agent ───────────────┤    │                │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────┘                │
│                                                        │                     │
│              ┌─────────────────────────────────────────▼──────────┐          │
│              │  🗄️  CENTRAL LOG STORE                              │          │
│              │                                                     │          │
│              │  Elasticsearch / Loki / CloudWatch / Splunk        │          │
│              │         │                                          │          │
│              │         ▼                                          │          │
│              │  📊 Kibana / Grafana   (visualization & alerts)   │          │
│              └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                                                                              │
│  THREE LOGGING PATTERNS:                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────────┐             │
│  │ Node-level Agent │ Sidecar Streaming│ Sidecar with Agent   │             │
│  │ (DaemonSet)      │ (sidecar → stdout│ (sidecar ships logs) │             │
│  │ ✅ Recommended   │  → node agent)   │ ⚠️ Resource heavy   │             │
│  │ 1 agent per node │ Multi-format logs│ Direct to backend    │             │
│  └──────────────────┴──────────────────┴──────────────────────┘             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA Scope: Logging

The CKA exam tests kubectl logs commands, not deploying full logging stacks. Know the architecture concepts (DaemonSet agent, node-level logs, sidecar patterns) but focus your practice on the kubectl logs flags in section 33.1.

Sidecar Logging Pattern — When Apps Don't Log to stdout

📖 Why Sidecar Logging?

Some applications write logs to files instead of stdout. Since kubectl logs only captures stdout/stderr, you need a sidecar container that reads the log file and streams it to its own stdout — making it visible via kubectl logs.

sidecar-logging.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
  # Main application — writes logs to a file
  - name: django
    image: anihpj:v1
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/django

  # Sidecar — streams the log file to stdout
  - name: log-streamer
    image: busybox:1.36
    args: ['sh', '-c', 'tail -F /var/log/django/app.log']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/django

  volumes:
  - name: log-volume
    emptyDir: {}

# Now you can read the app logs via:
# kubectl logs anihpj-web -c log-streamer

Container Log Rotation

Setting Default Purpose
containerLogMaxSize 10Mi Max size of a single log file before rotation
containerLogMaxFiles 5 Max number of rotated log files kept per container
💡 Log Rotation Config

These are set in the kubelet configuration (/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml). Default: each container can use up to 50Mi of disk for logs (10Mi × 5 files). If a node has disk pressure, check if log rotation is properly configured.

33.3 Monitoring with Metrics Server

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Metrics Server is a lightweight, in-cluster component that collects CPU and memory usage from kubelet on each node. It powers kubectl top and is required for HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler).

Metrics Server — How It Works

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     METRICS SERVER ARCHITECTURE                          │
│                                                                          │
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐                            │
│  │ kubelet  │   │ kubelet  │   │ kubelet  │                            │
│  │ (node 1) │   │ (node 2) │   │ (node 3) │                            │
│  │          │   │          │   │          │                            │
│  │ cAdvisor │   │ cAdvisor │   │ cAdvisor │   ← built into kubelet     │
│  │ collects │   │ collects │   │ collects │     collects container      │
│  │ metrics  │   │ metrics  │   │ metrics  │     CPU/memory metrics     │
│  └────┬─────┘   └────┬─────┘   └────┬─────┘                            │
│       │              │              │                                    │
│       └──────────────┼──────────────┘                                    │
│                      ▼                                                   │
│            ┌──────────────────┐                                         │
│            │  Metrics Server  │   ← Deployment in kube-system           │
│            │  (kube-system)   │     Scrapes /metrics/resource from      │
│            │                  │     each kubelet every 15 seconds       │
│            └────────┬─────────┘                                         │
│                     │ exposes                                            │
│                     ▼                                                    │
│            ┌──────────────────┐                                         │
│            │ metrics.k8s.io   │   ← Kubernetes API extension            │
│            │  API endpoint    │                                         │
│            └────────┬─────────┘                                         │
│                     │                                                    │
│           ┌─────────┼──────────────┐                                    │
│           ▼                        ▼                                    │
│  ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────────┐                           │
│  │ kubectl top  │       │ HPA Controller   │                           │
│  │ pods / nodes │       │ (auto-scaling)   │                           │
│  └──────────────┘       └──────────────────┘                           │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Metrics Server Installation & Usage 
# ═══ INSTALL METRICS SERVER ═══
# Download and apply the official manifest
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml

# For local clusters (kubeadm, kind, minikube) — add insecure TLS flag:
# Edit the Deployment and add to args:
# --kubelet-insecure-tls
kubectl edit deployment metrics-server -n kube-system

# Verify metrics server is running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server
kubectl get apiservices | grep metrics     # Should show v1beta1.metrics.k8s.io

# ═══ KUBECTL TOP — RESOURCE USAGE ═══
# Node-level metrics
kubectl top nodes                          # CPU and memory per node
kubectl top nodes --sort-by=cpu            # Sort by CPU usage

# Pod-level metrics
kubectl top pods                           # CPU and memory per pod
kubectl top pods -A                        # All namespaces
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory          # Sort by memory usage
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu             # Sort by CPU usage
kubectl top pods -n kube-system            # Specific namespace

# Pod with containers breakdown
kubectl top pods --containers              # Per-container metrics

Interpreting Resource Metrics

Metric Unit Example What It Means
CPU millicores (m) 250m 25% of one CPU core
CPU cores 1 100% of one CPU core
Memory Mi (mebibytes) 128Mi ~134 MB of RAM
Memory Gi (gibibytes) 2Gi ~2.1 GB of RAM

⚠️ Metrics Server Gotchas

  • ✗ Wrong Expecting kubectl top to work without Metrics Server installed
  • ✓ Right Install Metrics Server first — it's not included by default in kubeadm clusters
  • ✗ Wrong Thinking Metrics Server stores historical data
  • ✓ Right Metrics Server provides current snapshot only — for history use Prometheus
  • ✗ Wrong Forgetting --kubelet-insecure-tls in lab environments
  • ✓ Right Add this flag in kubeadm/kind/minikube — otherwise Metrics Server can't scrape kubelet

33.4 Monitoring anihpj Resource Usage

Let's apply monitoring to our anihpj project — find resource-hungry pods, identify OOM risks, and set appropriate limits.

Monitoring anihpj — Finding Resource Issues 
# Which pods are using the most CPU?
kubectl top pods -n anihpj --sort-by=cpu
# NAME                          CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
# anihpj-web-7d4f8c5-abc12      250m         128Mi
# anihpj-db-0                   100m         512Mi     ← DB uses most memory
# anihpj-worker-xxxx            50m          64Mi

# Which pods are using the most memory?
kubectl top pods -n anihpj --sort-by=memory

# Per-container breakdown (useful for sidecar pods)
kubectl top pods -n anihpj --containers
# NAME                  CONTAINER   CPU    MEMORY
# anihpj-web-xxx        django      200m   100Mi
# anihpj-web-xxx        nginx       50m    28Mi

# Node-level — is any node under pressure?
kubectl top nodes
# NAME           CPU(cores)  CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)  MEMORY%
# controlplane   350m        17%    1200Mi         62%
# worker01       800m        40%    3200Mi         80%     ← ⚠️ High memory!

# Compare against resource requests/limits
kubectl describe pod anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj | grep -A8 "Requests\|Limits"

Resource Monitoring — Requests vs Limits vs Actual Usage

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  anihpj-web pod: CPU usage over time                                     │
│                                                                          │
│  CPU (millicores)                                                        │
│  500m ┤                                                                  │
│       │                                                                  │
│  400m ┤                              ╔═══════════════╗ ← LIMIT (400m)   │
│       │                        ┌─────╨───┐           ║                   │
│  300m ┤                   ┌────┘         └───┐       ║                   │
│       │              ┌────┘                  │       ║                   │
│  250m ┤─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─│─ ─ ─ ─║─ ← REQUEST (250m)│
│       │         ┌────┘                       └───┐   ║                   │
│  200m ┤    ┌────┘                                └───║──┐                │
│       │────┘                                         ║  └───             │
│  100m ┤                                              ║                   │
│       │                                              ║                   │
│    0m ┤──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶ Time  │
│       │                                                                  │
│  REQUEST = guaranteed minimum (scheduler uses this for placement)        │
│  LIMIT   = maximum allowed (container is throttled/killed above this)    │
│  ACTUAL  = the wavy line (what kubectl top shows)                        │
│                                                                          │
│  ⚠️ If ACTUAL exceeds MEMORY LIMIT → OOMKilled (exit 137)              │
│  ⚠️ If ACTUAL exceeds CPU LIMIT → Throttled (not killed)               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🏋️ Practice Drill: Monitoring Commands

1
Install Metrics Server and verify: kubectl top nodes returns data
2
Find the pod using the most CPU: kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=cpu | head -5
3
Find the node with highest memory usage: kubectl top nodes --sort-by=memory

Chapter 34: CKA Troubleshooting Scenarios

10 must-practice scenarios with step-by-step solutions and a systematic troubleshooting methodology. Practice each scenario until you can solve it under 5 minutes — that's exam speed.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~3 hours 🎯 Scenarios 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Troubleshooting methodology — the 5-step IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY flow
  • ✅ 10 must-practice scenarios — Node NotReady, Pod Pending, CrashLoopBackOff, DNS, etcd, certs, and more
  • ✅ Symptom-to-solution mapping — fast lookup from what you see to what you fix
  • ✅ CKA exam strategy — time management, speed tips, and common traps to avoid
  • ✅ Essential command reference — the exact commands for each troubleshooting step
CKA Exam Domains Covered:
Troubleshooting 30%
🎯 This Chapter = Your CKA Practice Lab

These 10 scenarios cover the most common troubleshooting tasks on the CKA exam. Each follows the 5-step method: IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY. Train yourself to follow this flow automatically.

34.1 Scenario Collection — 10 Must-Practice Scenarios

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Each scenario presents a broken cluster state and walks you through the exact commands and thinking process to diagnose and fix it. These mirror real CKA exam tasks.

Scenario Difficulty Map

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  10 MUST-PRACTICE CKA TROUBLESHOOTING SCENARIOS                             │
│                                                                              │
│  INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER                                                        │
│  ├── Scenario 1:  Worker Node NotReady        ████████░░  Difficulty: 8/10  │
│  ├── Scenario 6:  etcd Cluster Unhealthy      █████████░  Difficulty: 9/10  │
│  ├── Scenario 7:  Certificates Expired        ████████░░  Difficulty: 8/10  │
│  └── Scenario 10: kubeconfig Issues           ██████░░░░  Difficulty: 6/10  │
│                                                                              │
│  WORKLOAD LAYER                                                              │
│  ├── Scenario 2:  Pod Stuck in Pending        ██████░░░░  Difficulty: 6/10  │
│  ├── Scenario 4:  CrashLoopBackOff            ███████░░░  Difficulty: 7/10  │
│  └── Scenario 9:  PVC Not Binding             ██████░░░░  Difficulty: 6/10  │
│                                                                              │
│  NETWORKING LAYER                                                            │
│  ├── Scenario 3:  Service Not Accessible      ███████░░░  Difficulty: 7/10  │
│  ├── Scenario 5:  DNS Resolution Failing      ████████░░  Difficulty: 8/10  │
│  └── Scenario 8:  Network Policy Blocking     ███████░░░  Difficulty: 7/10  │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 Practice order: 2 → 4 → 3 → 10 → 9 → 1 → 8 → 5 → 7 → 6              │
│     (easiest first, build confidence before the hard ones)                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 1: Worker Node NotReady

⚠️ Problem

kubectl get nodes shows worker01 NotReady. Pods on this node are being evicted.

🔴 Worker Node NotReady — Step-by-Step Walkthrough

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get nodes                                                 │
│     NAME       STATUS     ROLES    AGE   VERSION                        │
│     master     Ready      master   30d   v1.31.0                        │
│     worker01   NotReady   <none>   30d   v1.31.0    ◄── PROBLEM        │
