│     worker02   Ready      <none>   30d   v1.31.0                        │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Describe the node:                                         │
│     $ kubectl describe node worker01                                    │
│     Conditions:                                                         │
│       Ready   False   KubeletNotReady   container runtime not running   │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — SSH to the node:                                          │
│     $ ssh worker01                                                      │
│     $ systemctl status kubelet                                          │
│     → Active: inactive (dead)    ◄── kubelet is not running!            │
│                                                                         │
│     $ systemctl status containerd                                       │
│     → Active: inactive (dead)    ◄── container runtime also down!       │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Restart services:                                             │
│     $ systemctl restart containerd                                      │
│     $ systemctl restart kubelet                                         │
│     $ systemctl enable kubelet    (ensure it starts on boot)            │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY — Back on the master:                                        │
│     $ kubectl get nodes                                                 │
│     worker01   Ready   <none>   30d   v1.31.0   ← ✅ Fixed!            │
│                                                                         │
│  ⚠️  OTHER COMMON CAUSES:                                               │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │ • kubelet config wrong → /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml   │            │
│  │ • swap enabled → swapoff -a                             │            │
│  │ • disk pressure → df -h (clear space)                   │            │
│  │ • CNI plugin missing → ls /etc/cni/net.d/               │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 2: Pod Stuck in Pending

⚠️ Problem

anihpj-web pod in the anihpj namespace is stuck in Pending state and won't schedule.

🟡 Pod Pending — Scheduling Failure Walkthrough

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     NAME                 READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE               │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx      0/1     Pending   0          5m                │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Describe the pod (Events are KEY):                         │
│     $ kubectl describe pod anihpj-web-xxxx -n anihpj                    │
│     Events:                                                             │
│       Warning  FailedScheduling  0/3 nodes are available:               │
│         1 node(s) had taint {node-role.kubernetes.io/master: },    ◄──  │
│         that the pod didn't tolerate,                                   │
│         2 Insufficient cpu.                                        ◄──  │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Two issues revealed:                                      │
│     a) Master node has taint → pod can't schedule there                 │
│     b) Worker nodes don't have enough CPU for the request               │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Reduce resource requests:                                     │
│     $ kubectl edit deployment anihpj-web -n anihpj                      │
│     resources:                                                          │
│       requests:                                                         │
│         cpu: "100m"       ← was "2000m", reduced to fit                 │
│         memory: "256Mi"                                                 │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj -w                                     │
│     anihpj-web-xxxx   1/1   Running   0   15s   ← ✅ Scheduled!        │
│                                                                         │
│  📋 COMMON PENDING CAUSES:                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┐           │
│  │ Event Message            │ Fix                           │           │
│  ├──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤           │
│  │ Insufficient cpu/memory  │ Reduce requests or add nodes  │           │
│  │ pod didn't tolerate      │ Add toleration or remove taint│           │
│  │ no nodes match selector  │ Fix nodeSelector or label node│           │
│  │ PVC not found            │ Create the missing PVC        │           │
│  └──────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 3: Service Not Accessible

⚠️ Problem

Can't reach anihpj via the NodePort service. Requests to http://<node-ip>:<nodeport> time out.

🔵 Service Not Accessible — Label Mismatch Walkthrough

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Check the service:                                       │
│     $ kubectl get svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj                              │
│     NAME         TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    PORT(S)        AGE            │
│     anihpj-svc   NodePort   10.96.45.12   80:30080/TCP   2h             │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check if endpoints exist:                                  │
│     $ kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj                        │
│     NAME         ENDPOINTS   AGE                                        │
│     anihpj-svc   <none>      2h   ◄── NO ENDPOINTS! Service has        │
│                                         no matching pods!               │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Compare selector vs pod labels:                           │
│     $ kubectl get svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj -o yaml | grep -A3 selector │
│       selector:                                                         │
│         app: anihpj-web       ◄── Service looks for this label          │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj --show-labels                          │
│       anihpj-web-xxxx   Running   app=anihpj   ◄── Pod has THIS label  │
│                                                                         │
│     → MISMATCH! Service: "anihpj-web" vs Pod: "anihpj"                 │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Correct the service selector:                                 │
│     $ kubectl edit svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj                             │
│     selector:                                                           │
│       app: anihpj         ← changed from "anihpj-web" to "anihpj"      │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj                        │
│     ENDPOINTS: 10.244.1.5:8000   ← ✅ Pod is now connected!            │
│                                                                         │
│     $ curl http://<node-ip>:30080                                       │
│     → ✅ anihpj responds!                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 4: CrashLoopBackOff

⚠️ Problem

anihpj-db pod keeps crashing — restart count is climbing and backoff interval increasing.

🔴 CrashLoopBackOff — Missing Environment Variable

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj                                        │
│     NAME         READY   STATUS             RESTARTS      AGE           │
│     anihpj-db-0  0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   7 (3m ago)    15m           │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Logs from the PREVIOUS crash:                              │
│     $ kubectl logs anihpj-db-0 -n anihpj --previous                     │
│                                                                         │
│     Error: Database is uninitialized and superuser password              │
│     is not specified. You must specify POSTGRES_PASSWORD to a    ◄──    │
│     non-empty value for the superuser.                                  │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Check pod spec:                                           │
│     $ kubectl describe pod anihpj-db-0 -n anihpj                        │
│     Containers:                                                         │
│       postgres:                                                         │
│         Image: postgres:15                                              │
│         Environment: <none>    ◄── No env vars set! Missing            │
│                                    POSTGRES_PASSWORD                     │
│     Last State: Terminated (Exit Code 1)  ← App error                   │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Add the required env var via Secret:                          │
│     $ kubectl create secret generic db-creds \                          │
│         --from-literal=POSTGRES_PASSWORD=securepass123 -n anihpj        │
│                                                                         │
│     Then patch the StatefulSet:                                          │
│     $ kubectl edit statefulset anihpj-db -n anihpj                       │
│     env:                                                                │
│     - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD                                           │
│       valueFrom:                                                        │
│         secretKeyRef:                                                   │
│           name: db-creds                                                │
│           key: POSTGRES_PASSWORD                                        │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj -w                                     │
│     anihpj-db-0   1/1   Running   0   20s   ← ✅ No more crashes!      │
│     $ kubectl logs anihpj-db-0 -n anihpj                                │
│     → "database system is ready to accept connections"                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 5: DNS Resolution Failing

⚠️ Problem

Pods can't resolve service names. anihpj-web can't reach anihpj-db by hostname.

🔴 DNS Failure — CoreDNS Troubleshooting

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Test DNS from inside a pod:                              │
│     $ kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=    │
│       Never -- nslookup kubernetes.default                              │
│     → ;; connection timed out; no servers could be reached     ◄──     │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check CoreDNS pods:                                       │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns              │
│     NAME                       READY   STATUS             RESTARTS     │
│     coredns-5644d7b6d9-abc12   0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   4            │
│     coredns-5644d7b6d9-def34   0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   4            │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Check CoreDNS logs:                                      │
│     $ kubectl logs -n kube-system coredns-5644d7b6d9-abc12              │
│     → plugin/forward: no nameservers found                              │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get cm coredns -n kube-system -o yaml                     │
│     → Corefile has forward . /etc/resolv.conf                           │
│     → But /etc/resolv.conf in the pod is empty or wrong                 │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Correct the CoreDNS ConfigMap:                               │
│     $ kubectl edit cm coredns -n kube-system                            │
│     forward . 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4    ← use specific DNS servers            │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system         │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox:1.36 --rm -it --restart=    │
│       Never -- nslookup kubernetes.default                              │
│     Name:    kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local                       │
│     Address: 10.96.0.1   ← ✅ DNS is working!                          │
│                                                                         │
│  🔍 ALSO CHECK:                                                        │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │ • CoreDNS service IP:  kubectl get svc kube-dns         │            │
│  │   -n kube-system (must match pod's /etc/resolv.conf)    │            │
│  │ • Network plugin: is the CNI functional?                │            │
│  │ • Firewall rules blocking UDP port 53?                  │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 6: etcd Cluster Unhealthy

⚠️ Problem

API server is intermittently unavailable. kubectl commands sometimes time out. Suspected etcd issue.

🔴 etcd Unhealthy — Diagnosis and Recovery

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Check etcd health:                                       │
│     $ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health \                           │
│       --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \                              │
│       --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \                        │
│       --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \                      │
│       --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key                         │
│                                                                         │
│     https://127.0.0.1:2379 is unhealthy: failed to commit    ◄──       │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check etcd member list and logs:                           │
│     $ etcdctl member list (with same cert flags above)                  │
│     $ kubectl logs etcd-master -n kube-system                           │
│     OR if API is down:                                                  │
│     $ crictl logs $(crictl ps -a | grep etcd | awk '{print $1}')       │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Common causes:                                           │
│     → Disk too slow (WAL write latency)                                 │
│     → Member removed/added incorrectly                                  │
│     → Data corruption                                                   │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — If data corruption, restore from backup:                     │
│     a) Move API server manifest out to stop it:                         │
│        $ mv /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml /tmp/         │
│                                                                         │
│     b) Restore etcd from snapshot:                                      │
│        $ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /backup/snapshot.db \   │
│          --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                              │
│                                                                         │
│     c) Update etcd manifest with new data-dir:                          │
│        $ vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml                         │
│        → Change --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored                       │
│        → Update hostPath volume to /var/lib/etcd-restored               │
│                                                                         │
│     d) Move API server manifest back:                                   │
│        $ mv /tmp/kube-apiserver.yaml /etc/kubernetes/manifests/         │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get nodes        ← API responds                          │
│     $ etcdctl endpoint health  ← etcd is healthy                       │
│     ✅ Cluster recovered!                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ CKA Gotcha: etcd Restore Flags

When restoring etcd, you must use a new data-dir path (--data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored). Don't restore in-place to /var/lib/etcd — it will fail if the existing data conflicts. Also update both the --data-dir flag AND the hostPath volume in the etcd static pod manifest.

Scenario 7: Certificates Expired

⚠️ Problem

kubectl commands fail with "Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate has expired".

🔴 Certificates Expired — Renewal Walkthrough

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Check certificate expiry:                                │
│     $ kubeadm certs check-expiration                                    │
│                                                                         │
│     CERTIFICATE                EXPIRES                  RESIDUAL TIME   │
│     admin.conf                 Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired    ◄── │
│     apiserver                  Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired    ◄── │
│     apiserver-kubelet-client   Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired        │
│     controller-manager.conf    Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired        │
│     front-proxy-client         Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired        │
│     scheduler.conf             Jan 15, 2024 09:30 UTC   expired        │
│                                                                         │
│  2. FIX — Renew all certificates:                                      │
│     $ kubeadm certs renew all                                           │
│                                                                         │
│     certificate embedded in admin.conf renewed                          │
│     certificate for serving the Kubernetes API renewed                  │
│     certificate for the API server to connect to kubelet renewed        │
│     ... (all renewed)                                                   │
│                                                                         │
│  3. RESTART — Control plane components:                                │
│     $ systemctl restart kubelet                                         │
│     (Static pods will automatically restart and pick up new certs)      │
│                                                                         │
│  4. UPDATE — kubeconfig with new certs:                                │
│     $ cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config                      │
│     $ chown $(id -u):$(id -g) ~/.kube/config                           │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get nodes                                                 │
│     → Should work without cert errors   ← ✅ Fixed!                    │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubeadm certs check-expiration                                    │
│     → All certs show ~1 year residual time                              │
│                                                                         │
│  💡 PREVENTION: kubeadm auto-renews on control plane upgrade.          │
│     Set a calendar reminder before cert expiry!                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 8: Network Policy Blocking Traffic

⚠️ Problem

Frontend pods can't reach backend pods after a NetworkPolicy was applied. curl backend:80 from frontend times out.

🟠 Network Policy Blocking — Label Selector Fix

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Confirm the connectivity issue:                          │
│     $ kubectl exec -n anihpj frontend-pod -- curl -s --max-time 3      │
│       http://backend-svc:80                                             │
│     → curl: (28) Connection timed out   ◄── blocked!                   │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — List and inspect NetworkPolicies:                          │
│     $ kubectl get networkpolicy -n anihpj                               │
│     NAME             POD-SELECTOR    AGE                                │
│     backend-policy   app=backend     10m                                │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl describe netpol backend-policy -n anihpj                  │
│     Spec:                                                               │
│       PodSelector:   app=backend                                        │
│       Allowing ingress traffic:                                         │
│         From:                                                           │
│           PodSelector: role=api       ◄── Only allows "role=api" pods  │
│       Policy Types: Ingress                                             │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Check frontend pod labels:                               │
│     $ kubectl get pods -n anihpj --show-labels                          │
│     frontend-pod   Running   app=frontend,tier=web   ◄── No "role=api" │
│                                                                         │
│     → The policy allows ingress from pods with "role=api"               │
│     → Frontend has "app=frontend,tier=web" — NO MATCH!                 │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Either add the label or update the policy:                   │
│     Option A: Add label to frontend pod                                 │
│     $ kubectl label pod frontend-pod -n anihpj role=api                 │
│                                                                         │
│     Option B: Fix the NetworkPolicy selector                            │
│     $ kubectl edit netpol backend-policy -n anihpj                      │
│     ingress:                                                            │
│     - from:                                                             │
│       - podSelector:                                                    │
│           matchLabels:                                                  │
│             app: frontend      ← match actual frontend labels          │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl exec -n anihpj frontend-pod -- curl -s --max-time 3      │
│       http://backend-svc:80                                             │
│     → ✅ Response received! Traffic is flowing.                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 9: PVC Not Binding

⚠️ Problem

anihpj-db PVC is stuck in Pending state. The database pod can't start without its volume.

🟡 PVC Not Binding — StorageClass Mismatch

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY:                                                           │
│     $ kubectl get pvc -n anihpj                                         │
│     NAME          STATUS    VOLUME   CAPACITY   STORAGECLASS   AGE      │
│     anihpj-data   Pending                       fast-ssd       10m ◄── │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Describe PVC for events:                                  │
│     $ kubectl describe pvc anihpj-data -n anihpj                        │
│     Events:                                                             │
│       Warning  ProvisioningFailed  no persistent volumes available      │
│       for this claim and no storage class "fast-ssd" found     ◄──     │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — Check available storage classes and PVs:                 │
│     $ kubectl get storageclass                                          │
│     NAME              PROVISIONER          RECLAIMPOLICY               │
│     standard (def.)   rancher.io/local     Delete                      │
│     → "fast-ssd" doesn't exist! PVC asks for a non-existent class     │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get pv                                                    │
│     NAME     CAPACITY   ACCESS   RECLAIM   STATUS      STORAGECLASS    │
│     pv-01    10Gi       RWO      Retain    Available   standard        │
│     → PV exists but with "standard" class, not "fast-ssd"             │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Option A: Change PVC to use "standard":                      │
│     (PVC storageClassName is immutable — recreate it)                   │
│     $ kubectl delete pvc anihpj-data -n anihpj                          │
│     $ cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -                                    │
│     apiVersion: v1                                                      │
│     kind: PersistentVolumeClaim                                         │
│     metadata:                                                           │
│       name: anihpj-data                                                 │
│       namespace: anihpj                                                 │
│     spec:                                                               │
│       accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]                                    │
│       storageClassName: standard                                        │
│       resources:                                                        │
│         requests:                                                       │
│           storage: 5Gi                                                  │
│     EOF                                                                 │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl get pvc -n anihpj                                         │
│     anihpj-data   Bound   pv-01   10Gi   RWO   standard   ← ✅ Bound! │
│                                                                         │
│  💡 "WaitForFirstConsumer" is NOT an error — PV binds only when        │
│     a pod actually mounts the PVC. This is normal behavior.            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scenario 10: kubeconfig Issues

⚠️ Problem

kubectl can't connect to the cluster: "The connection to the server localhost:8080 was refused".

🟠 kubeconfig Issues — Connection Diagnosis

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. IDENTIFY — Check current config:                                    │
│     $ kubectl config current-context                                    │
│     error: current-context is not set   ◄── no context configured!     │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl config view                                               │
│     apiVersion: v1                                                      │
│     clusters: null                                                      │
│     contexts: null       ◄── kubeconfig is empty/wrong                 │
│                                                                         │
│  2. GATHER — Check where kubectl looks for config:                     │
│     $ echo $KUBECONFIG                                                  │
│     → empty (uses default ~/.kube/config)                               │
│                                                                         │
│     $ ls -la ~/.kube/config                                             │
│     → No such file or directory   ◄── config file is missing!          │
│                                                                         │
│  3. ANALYZE — The kubeconfig wasn't copied after cluster setup.        │
│     On a kubeadm cluster, the admin config is on the master node.       │
│                                                                         │
│  4. FIX — Copy the kubeconfig from the master:                         │
│     $ mkdir -p ~/.kube                                                  │
│     $ cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config                      │
│     $ chown $(id -u):$(id -g) ~/.kube/config                           │
│                                                                         │
│     If on a different machine, SCP from master:                         │
│     $ scp master:/etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config              │
│                                                                         │
│  5. VERIFY:                                                             │
│     $ kubectl cluster-info                                              │
│     Kubernetes control plane is running at https://10.0.0.10:6443       │
│     CoreDNS is running at https://10.0.0.10:6443/api/v1/...            │
│                                                                         │
│     $ kubectl get nodes                                                 │
│     → All nodes listed   ← ✅ Connected!                               │
│                                                                         │
│  📋 OTHER KUBECONFIG ISSUES:                                            │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┐         │
│  │ Symptom                      │ Fix                         │         │
│  ├──────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┤         │
│  │ Wrong context active         │ kubectl config use-context  │         │
│  │ Wrong server URL             │ Edit server: in config      │         │
│  │ KUBECONFIG env overriding    │ unset KUBECONFIG            │         │
│  │ Permission denied            │ chown the config file       │         │
│  └──────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┘         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🏋️ Practice Drill: Time Yourself on All 10 Scenarios

Set up a practice cluster (kubeadm or kind). Intentionally break it in each of these 10 ways, then fix it. Target: solve each scenario in under 5 minutes. On the CKA exam, speed matters.

  1. Stop kubelet on a worker node → diagnose and fix NotReady
  2. Set impossible resource requests → fix Pending pod
  3. Mismatch service selector → fix endpoints
  4. Remove required env var → fix CrashLoopBackOff
  5. Break CoreDNS config → fix DNS resolution
  6. Corrupt etcd data → restore from snapshot
  7. Let certs expire (kubeadm certs) → renew and recover
  8. Apply overly restrictive NetworkPolicy → fix traffic flow
  9. Create PVC with wrong StorageClass → fix binding
  10. Delete kubeconfig → recreate from admin.conf

Symptom → First Response Quick-Reference

Symptom You See Most Likely Cause First Command to Run Scenario #
Node NotReady kubelet stopped / misconfigured ssh <node>; systemctl status kubelet #1
Pod Pending Insufficient resources / node affinity kubectl describe pod <name> → Events #2
Service returns no response Selector mismatch / wrong port kubectl get endpoints <svc> #3
CrashLoopBackOff Missing config / bad entrypoint kubectl logs <pod> --previous #4
DNS not resolving CoreDNS crash / bad ConfigMap kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns #5
API server errors / cluster unstable etcd unhealthy or data loss etcdctl endpoint health (with certs) #6
x509: certificate has expired Cluster certificates expired kubeadm certs check-expiration #7
Pod-to-pod traffic blocked NetworkPolicy too restrictive kubectl get netpol -A #8
PVC Pending StorageClass mismatch / no provisioner kubectl get sc + kubectl describe pvc #9
connection refused from kubectl kubeconfig missing or corrupted ls -la ~/.kube/config #10
⚠️ Common Gotcha: Multiple Symptoms, One Root Cause

On the CKA exam, a single failure (e.g., kubelet down) can produce many symptoms at once — Pods in Terminating state, Services returning errors, DNS failing. Always start at the infrastructure layer (nodes → control plane → workloads) and work upward. Fixing the root cause often resolves several visible symptoms simultaneously.

34.2 Troubleshooting Methodology Checklist

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Every troubleshooting task — on the exam or in production — follows the same 5-step systematic method. Internalize this flow and you'll never freeze under pressure.

The 5-Step Troubleshooting Method

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   THE 5-STEP TROUBLESHOOTING METHOD                          │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────┐    ┌──────────┐  │
│  │    1.     │    │    2.    │    │    3.     │    │  4.  │    │    5.    │  │
│  │ IDENTIFY │───▶│ GATHER  │───▶│ ANALYZE  │───▶│ FIX  │───▶│ VERIFY  │  │
│  │          │    │         │    │          │    │      │    │         │  │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────┘    └──────────┘  │
│       │               │              │              │             │         │
│       ▼               ▼              ▼              ▼             ▼         │
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐  │
│  │ What is  │   │ kubectl  │   │ What     │   │ Apply    │ │ Did the  │  │
│  │ broken?  │   │ describe │   │ changed? │   │ smallest │ │ fix      │  │
│  │ What's   │   │ kubectl  │   │ What's   │   │ possible │ │ work?    │  │
│  │ the      │   │ logs     │   │ diff-    │   │ change   │ │ Are      │  │
│  │ symptom? │   │ kubectl  │   │ erent?   │   │          │ │ there    │  │
│  │          │   │ exec     │   │          │   │          │ │ side     │  │
│  │ get pods │   │ events   │   │ Compare  │   │ Don't    │ │ effects? │  │
│  │ get nodes│   │ journal  │   │ working  │   │ change   │ │          │  │
│  │ get svc  │   │ systemctl│   │ vs broken│   │ too much │ │ Re-run   │  │
│  │ get events│  │ crictl   │   │          │   │ at once  │ │ step 1   │  │
│  └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘ └──────────┘  │
│                                                                              │
│  ⚠️  NEVER SKIP STEP 5!  Many CKA points are lost because candidates        │
│     fix the issue but don't verify, and the fix was incomplete.             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Essential Troubleshooting Commands — Quick Reference

Step Command Purpose
1. IDENTIFY kubectl get pods -A -o wide See all pods across all namespaces with node placement
kubectl get nodes -o wide Node status, IP, OS, container runtime
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' Recent cluster-wide events timeline
2. GATHER kubectl describe pod <name> Detailed pod info — Events section at bottom!
kubectl logs <pod> --previous Logs from the last crashed container
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> Logs from a specific container in multi-container pod
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh Shell into a running container to inspect
3. ANALYZE kubectl describe node <name> Node conditions, taints, allocated resources
journalctl -u kubelet --no-pager -l kubelet logs on a node (SSH first)
crictl ps -a Container runtime status (when API is down)
4. FIX kubectl edit <resource> <name> Quick in-place fix of a resource
systemctl restart kubelet Restart kubelet after fixing node issues
5. VERIFY kubectl get pods -w Watch pods transition to Running
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=Started Confirm containers started successfully
💡 Part 9 Key Takeaways
  • Troubleshooting flow: kubectl get → describe → logs → exec → events. ALWAYS follow this order.
  • Pod statuses: CrashLoopBackOff (app crashes), ImagePullBackOff (wrong image/tag), Pending (no node/resources), Error (exit non-zero)
  • Node troubleshooting: Check kubelet (systemctl status), certificates (kubeadm certs), connectivity
  • Control plane down? Check static pod manifests in /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ and kubelet logs
  • 5-step method: IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY — never skip VERIFY!
  • Use --previous to see logs from crashed containers. Use -f to follow logs in real-time.
  • CKA weight: 30% — the heaviest domain! Practice every scenario until you can solve it in under 5 minutes.
✅ Part 9 Competency Checklist
  • Can you debug a CrashLoopBackOff pod in under 3 minutes?
  • Can you diagnose a NotReady node and bring it back?
  • Can you restore etcd from a snapshot backup?
  • Can you fix a broken service with no endpoints?
  • Can you troubleshoot DNS failure by inspecting CoreDNS?
  • Can you renew expired certificates and restore API access?
  • Can you identify and fix NetworkPolicy blocking traffic?
  • Do you follow IDENTIFY → GATHER → ANALYZE → FIX → VERIFY every time?

CKA Exam Time Management — Troubleshooting Questions

CKA Exam Strategy — 2 Hours, ~17 Questions

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CKA EXAM TIME MANAGEMENT STRATEGY                                          │
│                                                                              │
│  Total: 2 hours (120 minutes) │ ~17 questions │ Pass: 66%                   │
│  Troubleshooting: ~30% = approximately 5-6 questions                        │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐                │
│  │  RECOMMENDED APPROACH                                    │                │
│  │                                                          │                │
│  │  1. FIRST PASS (0-90 min):                               │                │
│  │     → Answer every question you can solve quickly         │                │
│  │     → Target: ~5 min per question                        │                │
│  │     → Skip any question that takes > 8 min              │                │
│  │     → Flag skipped questions for second pass              │                │
│  │                                                          │                │
│  │  2. SECOND PASS (90-120 min):                             │                │
│  │     → Return to flagged/skipped questions                │                │
│  │     → Spend remaining time on highest-weight questions   │                │
│  │     → Partial answers still earn partial credit          │                │
│  │                                                          │                │
│  │  3. TROUBLESHOOTING SPEED TIPS:                           │                │
│  │     → Always check the NAMESPACE first (exam specifies)  │                │
│  │     → Use kubectl config set-context --current            │                │
│  │       --namespace=<ns> to avoid -n flag every time       │                │
│  │     → Have your debug sequence memorized:                 │                │
│  │       get → describe → logs → exec                       │                │
│  │     → Use kubectl explain <resource> for field reference │                │
│  │     → Copy-paste from kubernetes.io docs (allowed!)      │                │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘                │
│                                                                              │
│  ⚠️  COMMON TIME WASTERS:                                                   │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │ Trap                           │ Solution                        │       │
│  ├────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤       │
│  │ Typing long YAML from memory  │ Use --dry-run=client -o yaml    │       │
│  │ Searching docs endlessly      │ Bookmark key pages before exam  │       │
│  │ Forgetting to switch context  │ Read question header FIRST      │       │
│  │ Not verifying after fix       │ Budget 30s for verification     │       │
│  │ Perfecting a low-score Q      │ Move on, return if time permits │       │
│  └────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Troubleshooting Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

🔥 One-Page Troubleshooting Cheat Sheet

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STATUS → FIRST COMMAND TO RUN                                               │
│  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  │
│                                                                              │
│  Pod Pending          → kubectl describe pod <name>     (check Events)     │
│  CrashLoopBackOff     → kubectl logs <pod> --previous   (last crash log)   │
│  ImagePullBackOff     → kubectl describe pod <name>     (check image name) │
│  CreateContainerError → kubectl describe pod <name>     (missing CM/Secret)│
│  Running but unhealthy→ kubectl logs <pod> -f           (app errors)       │
│  OOMKilled (exit 137) → increase resources.limits.memory                    │
│                                                                              │
│  Node NotReady        → ssh node; systemctl status kubelet                  │
│  API Server down      → crictl ps -a; crictl logs <id>                     │
│  Scheduler broken     → cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml   │
│  etcd broken          → etcdctl endpoint health (with cert flags)           │
│                                                                              │
│  Service no endpoints → kubectl get endpoints <svc>   (selector mismatch) │
│  DNS not resolving    → kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns│
│  NetworkPolicy block  → kubectl describe netpol <name> (check selectors)  │
│  PVC Pending          → kubectl get storageclass       (class mismatch)    │
│                                                                              │
│  USEFUL SHORTCUTS:                                                           │
│  ─────────────────                                                           │
│  alias k=kubectl                          # Already set on CKA exam         │
│  export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"     # Quick YAML generation          │
│  export now="--force --grace-period 0"    # Instant pod deletion            │
│  k config set-context --current --namespace=X  # Set default namespace     │
│  k explain pod.spec.containers --recursive     # Field reference            │
│                                                                              │
│  FILE LOCATIONS TO REMEMBER:                                                 │
│  ─────────────────────────────                                               │
│  /etc/kubernetes/manifests/    → Static pod YAML files (control plane)      │
│  /etc/kubernetes/pki/          → Cluster certificates                       │
│  /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml  → kubelet configuration                      │
│  /var/log/containers/          → Container log files on node                │
│  /etc/cni/net.d/               → CNI plugin configuration                  │
│  /var/lib/etcd/                → etcd data directory                        │
│  ~/.kube/config                → kubectl configuration (kubeconfig)         │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔴 Part 10: Production and Beyond Chapters 35-37

Chapter 35: High Availability Clusters

HA architecture, stacked vs external etcd topologies, setting up HA with kubeadm, etcd quorum, leader election, load balancer configuration, and a production HA checklist for the anihpj Django application.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 🏗️ HA 📝 CKA EXAM

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ HA architecture — why 3+ control plane nodes and how they work together
  • ✅ Stacked vs external etcd — topologies, trade-offs, and when to use each
  • ✅ HA setup with kubeadm — step-by-step multi-master initialization
  • ✅ etcd quorum — the math behind fault tolerance and why odd numbers matter
  • ✅ Leader election — how scheduler and controller-manager elect active leaders
  • ✅ Load balancer config — HAProxy/nginx in front of API servers
  • ✅ Production checklist — everything needed for anihpj HA deployment
CKA Exam Domains Covered:
Cluster Architecture 25% Troubleshooting 30%
🎯 HA Concepts Appear Throughout the CKA Exam

While you won't set up a full HA cluster on the exam, you must understand etcd quorum, backup/restore (covered in Part 7), and how control plane components achieve high availability. Questions about etcd member count, leader election, and API server load balancing are common.

35.1 HA Architecture

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

A single control plane node is a single point of failure. If it goes down, the API server becomes unreachable, no new pods can be scheduled, and the cluster is essentially frozen. High Availability (HA) eliminates this by running multiple control plane nodes.

Single vs HA Control Plane — Why HA Matters

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                              │
│  ❌ SINGLE CONTROL PLANE                ✅ HIGH AVAILABILITY (HA)            │
│  ════════════════════════               ═══════════════════════════           │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐                       ┌────────────────────┐              │
│  │ Control Plane│ ← Single point        │   LOAD BALANCER    │              │
│  │              │    of failure!         │  (HAProxy/nginx)   │              │
│  │ API Server   │                       └───────┬────────────┘              │
│  │ etcd         │                      ┌────────┼────────┐                  │
│  │ Scheduler    │                      ▼        ▼        ▼                  │
│  │ Ctrl-Manager │               ┌──────────┐┌──────────┐┌──────────┐       │
│  └──────┬───────┘               │  CP-1    ││  CP-2    ││  CP-3    │       │
│         │                       │ API ✅    ││ API ✅    ││ API ✅    │       │
│    ┌────┼────┐                  │ etcd ✅   ││ etcd ✅   ││ etcd ✅   │       │
│    ▼    ▼    ▼                  │ sched ✅  ││ sched ⏸  ││ sched ⏸  │       │
│  W-1  W-2  W-3                  │ c-mgr ✅  ││ c-mgr ⏸  ││ c-mgr ⏸  │       │
│                                 └──────────┘└──────────┘└──────────┘       │
│  If CP goes down:                     (leader)   (standby)   (standby)      │
│  ❌ No new scheduling                                                        │
│  ❌ No API access                ┌────┼────┐                                │
│  ❌ No self-healing              ▼    ▼    ▼                                │
│  ❌ Cluster is FROZEN           W-1  W-2  W-3                               │
│                                                                              │
│  Existing pods keep running     If CP-1 goes down:                          │
│  but nothing new can happen     ✅ LB routes to CP-2/CP-3                   │
│                                 ✅ New leader elected in seconds             │
│                                 ✅ Full cluster operations continue          │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Key HA Principle

Only ONE scheduler and controller-manager are active at any time (the "leader"). The others are on hot standby — they watch and are ready to take over in seconds. All API servers, however, handle requests simultaneously (active-active behind the load balancer).

HA Cluster Architecture — Complete View

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       HA CLUSTER ARCHITECTURE                                │
│                                                                              │
│                    ┌──────────────────────────┐                              │
│                    │     LOAD BALANCER         │                              │
│                    │   (HAProxy / nginx)       │                              │
│                    │   VIP: 10.0.0.100:6443    │                              │
│                    └────────────┬─────────────┘                              │
│                     ┌──────────┼──────────┐                                  │
│                     │          │          │                                   │
│                     ▼          ▼          ▼                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │                  CONTROL PLANE TIER                                  │     │
│  │                                                                     │     │
│  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐             │     │
│  │  │ cp-1 (10.0.1)│  │ cp-2 (10.0.2)│  │ cp-3 (10.0.3)│             │     │
│  │  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤             │     │
│  │  │ kube-apiserver│  │ kube-apiserver│  │ kube-apiserver│             │     │
│  │  │ (active)     │  │ (active)     │  │ (active)     │             │     │
│  │  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤             │     │
│  │  │ kube-scheduler│  │ kube-scheduler│  │ kube-scheduler│             │     │
│  │  │ ★ LEADER     │  │ ⏸ standby    │  │ ⏸ standby    │             │     │
│  │  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤             │     │
│  │  │ ctrl-manager │  │ ctrl-manager │  │ ctrl-manager │             │     │
│  │  │ ★ LEADER     │  │ ⏸ standby    │  │ ⏸ standby    │             │     │
│  │  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤  ├──────────────┤             │     │
│  │  │ etcd         │  │ etcd         │  │ etcd         │             │     │
│  │  │ (peer)       │  │ (peer)       │  │ (peer)       │             │     │
│  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘             │     │
│  │                                                                     │     │
│  │  etcd replication: cp-1 ◀──▶ cp-2 ◀──▶ cp-3 (Raft consensus)      │     │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │                   WORKER NODE TIER                                   │     │
│  │                                                                     │     │
│  │  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐       │     │
│  │  │ worker-1  │  │ worker-2  │  │ worker-3  │  │ worker-N  │       │     │
│  │  │┌─────────┐│  │┌─────────┐│  │┌─────────┐│  │┌─────────┐│       │     │
│  │  ││ anihpj  ││  ││ anihpj  ││  ││ anihpj  ││  ││ jobpost ││       │     │
│  │  ││ web-1   ││  ││ web-2   ││  ││ db-0    ││  ││ worker  ││       │     │
│  │  │└─────────┘│  │└─────────┘│  │└─────────┘│  │└─────────┘│       │     │
│  │  │ kubelet   │  │ kubelet   │  │ kubelet   │  │ kubelet   │       │     │
│  │  │ kube-proxy│  │ kube-proxy│  │ kube-proxy│  │ kube-proxy│       │     │
│  │  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘       │     │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯 CKA Exam: Know the Components

You must know what runs on control plane nodes vs worker nodes. API server is active-active (all serve requests). Scheduler and controller-manager are active-passive (only the leader acts). etcd uses Raft consensus (all peers participate but one is the Raft leader).

35.2 Stacked vs External etcd

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

There are two ways to deploy etcd in an HA cluster. The choice affects fault tolerance, complexity, and cost. You must understand both for the CKA exam.

Stacked etcd Topology — etcd on Control Plane Nodes

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STACKED etcd TOPOLOGY (Default with kubeadm)                                │
│                                                                              │
│  Each control plane node runs BOTH Kubernetes components AND etcd            │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐                │
│  │  CP Node 1    │    │  CP Node 2    │    │  CP Node 3    │                │
│  │ ┌───────────┐ │    │ ┌───────────┐ │    │ ┌───────────┐ │                │
│  │ │API Server │ │    │ │API Server │ │    │ │API Server │ │                │
│  │ │Scheduler  │ │    │ │Scheduler  │ │    │ │Scheduler  │ │                │
│  │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │    │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │    │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │                │
│  │ ├───────────┤ │    │ ├───────────┤ │    │ ├───────────┤ │                │
│  │ │  etcd  📀 │ │    │ │  etcd  📀 │ │    │ │  etcd  📀 │ │                │
│  │ └───────────┘ │    │ └───────────┘ │    │ └───────────┘ │                │
│  └───────┬───────┘    └───────┬───────┘    └───────┬───────┘                │
│          │                    │                    │                          │
│          └─────── Raft Replication ───────────────┘                          │
│                                                                              │
│  ✅ Simpler setup — fewer nodes to manage                                   │
│  ✅ Default kubeadm topology                                                │
│  ⚠️ Risk: If a node dies, you lose both CP and an etcd member              │
│  📊 Minimum: 3 nodes (for etcd quorum)                                     │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

External etcd Topology — etcd on Dedicated Servers

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EXTERNAL etcd TOPOLOGY                                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  etcd runs on SEPARATE dedicated nodes — decoupled from control plane        │
│                                                                              │
│  Control Plane Tier:                                                         │
│  ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐                │
│  │  CP Node 1    │    │  CP Node 2    │    │  CP Node 3    │                │
│  │ ┌───────────┐ │    │ ┌───────────┐ │    │ ┌───────────┐ │                │
│  │ │API Server │ │    │ │API Server │ │    │ │API Server │ │                │
│  │ │Scheduler  │ │    │ │Scheduler  │ │    │ │Scheduler  │ │                │
│  │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │    │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │    │ │Ctrl-Mgr   │ │                │
│  │ └───────────┘ │    │ └───────────┘ │    │ └───────────┘ │                │
│  └───────┬───────┘    └───────┬───────┘    └───────┬───────┘                │
│          └────────────┬───────┘                    │                          │
│                       │  Connect via TLS            │                          │
│          ┌────────────┼────────────────────────────┘                          │
│          ▼            ▼            ▼                                          │
│  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐                                │
│  │ etcd-1 📀 │  │ etcd-2 📀 │  │ etcd-3 📀 │  ← Dedicated etcd cluster    │
│  └─────┬─────┘  └─────┬─────┘  └─────┬─────┘                                │
│        └───── Raft Replication ───────┘                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  ✅ etcd survives control plane failures                                    │
│  ✅ Can scale etcd independently                                            │
│  ⚠️ More complex — requires 6+ nodes minimum (3 CP + 3 etcd)               │
│  ⚠️ More certs to manage (API→etcd TLS)                                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Topology Comparison

Aspect Stacked (default) External etcd
etcd location On control plane node Separate dedicated servers
Setup complexity Easier — kubeadm init handles it Harder — manual etcd cluster setup
Fault isolation ⚠️ CP failure = etcd member lost too ✅ etcd survives CP node failure
Minimum nodes 3 (combined CP + etcd) 6 (3 CP + 3 etcd)
Cost Lower — fewer servers Higher — 2× the infrastructure
CKA Exam Most tested topology ✅ Conceptual understanding needed
Best for Most production clusters Critical workloads with strict SLAs
⚠️ CKA Gotcha: Which Topology Is on the Exam?

The CKA exam uses stacked etcd topology (kubeadm default). When asked to backup/restore etcd, the etcd data dir is on the control plane node at /var/lib/etcd. The API server connects to etcd on localhost:2379. Know both topologies conceptually, but practice stacked for the exam.

35.3 Setting Up HA with kubeadm

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

Setting up an HA cluster with kubeadm follows a specific sequence: configure the load balancer first, initialize the first control plane, then join additional control planes and workers.

HA Setup Sequence — Step by Step

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    HA CLUSTER SETUP SEQUENCE                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  Step 1          Step 2           Step 3           Step 4                    │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐              │
│  │ Setup    │    │ Init 1st │    │ Join     │    │ Join     │              │
│  │ Load     │───▶│ Control  │───▶│ More CP  │───▶│ Worker   │              │
│  │ Balancer │    │ Plane    │    │ Nodes    │    │ Nodes    │              │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘              │
│  HAProxy/nginx    kubeadm init    kubeadm join    kubeadm join              │
│  on :6443         --control-      --control-      (no flag)                 │
│                   plane-endpoint  plane                                      │
│                   =LB_IP:6443                                                │
│                                                                              │
│  Step 5          Step 6           Step 7                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐                               │
│  │ Install  │    │ Verify   │    │ Test     │                               │
│  │ CNI      │───▶│ etcd     │───▶│ Failover │                               │
│  │ Plugin   │    │ Health   │    │          │                               │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘                               │
│  Calico/Cilium    etcdctl         Kill a CP node                             │
│                   endpoint        → verify LB                                │
│                   health          reroutes                                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Step 1: HAProxy Configuration

/etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg 
# HAProxy configuration for Kubernetes API Server HA
frontend kubernetes-api
    bind *:6443
    mode tcp
    option tcplog
    default_backend kubernetes-api-backend

backend kubernetes-api-backend
    mode tcp
    option tcp-check
    balance roundrobin
    # Health check: API server /healthz endpoint
    server cp-1 10.0.0.1:6443 check fall 3 rise 2
    server cp-2 10.0.0.2:6443 check fall 3 rise 2
    server cp-3 10.0.0.3:6443 check fall 3 rise 2

Step 2: Initialize First Control Plane

Terminal — cp-1 (first control plane) 
# Initialize first control plane — point to the load balancer!
$ sudo kubeadm init \
    --control-plane-endpoint "10.0.0.100:6443" \
    --upload-certs \
    --pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16

# Output includes TWO join commands:
# 1. For additional control plane nodes (has --control-plane flag)
# 2. For worker nodes (no --control-plane flag)

# Copy kubeconfig
$ mkdir -p $HOME/.kube
$ sudo cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
$ sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config

Steps 3 & 4: Join Additional Nodes

Terminal — cp-2 and cp-3 
# Join as additional control plane (note --control-plane flag!)
$ sudo kubeadm join 10.0.0.100:6443 \
    --token <token> \
    --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash> \
    --control-plane \
    --certificate-key <cert-key>
Terminal — worker nodes 
# Join as worker node (NO --control-plane flag)
$ sudo kubeadm join 10.0.0.100:6443 \
    --token <token> \
    --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash>

Step 6: Verify Cluster Health

Terminal — verify HA cluster 
# Verify all nodes are Ready
$ kubectl get nodes
NAME   STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
cp-1   Ready    control-plane   10m   v1.31.0
cp-2   Ready    control-plane   8m    v1.31.0
cp-3   Ready    control-plane   6m    v1.31.0
w-1    Ready    <none>          4m    v1.31.0
w-2    Ready    <none>          3m    v1.31.0
w-3    Ready    <none>          2m    v1.31.0

# Verify etcd cluster health
$ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl member list \
    --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
    --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
    --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
    --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# Should show 3 members: cp-1, cp-2, cp-3

✅ Verify: HA Setup Checklist

  • kubectl get nodes — all control plane nodes show Ready
  • etcdctl member list — shows 3 etcd members
  • etcdctl endpoint health — all endpoints healthy
  • Load balancer health check — all backends UP
  • Kill one CP node → API still responds via LB

35.4 etcd Quorum and Leader Election

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

etcd uses the Raft consensus algorithm. A majority of etcd members must agree before any write is committed. This majority is called the quorum. Understanding quorum math is essential for CKA.

etcd Quorum Formula: (N/2) + 1

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ETCD QUORUM — THE MATH                                    │
│                                                                              │
│  Formula: Quorum = floor(N/2) + 1                                            │
│  Tolerated Failures = N - Quorum                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────┬────────┬─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Members │ Quorum │ Tolerated Failures  │ Recommendation               │  │
│  ├─────────┼────────┼─────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤  │
│  │    1    │   1    │         0           │ ❌ Dev/test only              │  │
│  │    2    │   2    │         0           │ ❌ NEVER — worse than 1!     │  │
│  │    3    │   2    │         1           │ ✅ Standard production       │  │
│  │    4    │   3    │         1           │ ❌ Same tolerance as 3!      │  │
│  │    5    │   3    │         2           │ ✅ Large-scale production    │  │
│  │    6    │   4    │         2           │ ❌ Same tolerance as 5!      │  │
│  │    7    │   4    │         3           │ ⚠️ Rare — more latency       │  │
│  └─────────┴────────┴─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                              │
│  🔑 KEY INSIGHT: Even numbers are WASTEFUL!                                 │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────                                   │
│  3 members → tolerate 1 failure                                              │
│  4 members → STILL only tolerate 1 failure (need 3 for quorum)              │
│  → You added a node but got ZERO extra fault tolerance!                      │
│                                                                              │
│  ALWAYS USE ODD NUMBERS: 1, 3, 5, 7                                        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Quorum in Action — What Happens During Failures

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  3-MEMBER CLUSTER FAILURE SCENARIOS                                          │
│                                                                              │
│  Normal (3/3 alive):         1 Node Down (2/3 alive):                        │
│  ┌────┐  ┌────┐  ┌────┐     ┌────┐  ┌────┐  ┌────┐                        │
│  │ E1 │  │ E2 │  │ E3 │     │ E1 │  │ E2 │  │ ╳╳ │  ← Node 3 down       │
│  │ ✅ │  │ ✅ │  │ ✅ │     │ ✅ │  │ ✅ │  │ ❌ │                        │
│  └────┘  └────┘  └────┘     └────┘  └────┘  └────┘                        │
│  Quorum: 2 of 3 = ✅         Quorum: 2 of 3 = ✅ (still works!)            │
│  Writes: ✅                  Writes: ✅                                      │
│  Reads:  ✅                  Reads:  ✅                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  2 Nodes Down (1/3 alive):   Split Brain (network partition):                │
│  ┌────┐  ┌────┐  ┌────┐     ┌────┐  │  ┌────┐  ┌────┐                     │
│  │ E1 │  │ ╳╳ │  │ ╳╳ │     │ E1 │  │  │ E2 │  │ E3 │                     │
│  │ ✅ │  │ ❌ │  │ ❌ │     │ ✅ │  │  │ ✅ │  │ ✅ │                     │
│  └────┘  └────┘  └────┘     └────┘  │  └────┘  └────┘                     │
│  Quorum: 1 of 3 = ❌         Side A: 1 (no quorum ❌)                       │
│  Writes: ❌ CLUSTER DOWN!    Side B: 2 (has quorum ✅)                       │
│  Reads:  ❌ (stale reads     → Side B continues serving                      │
│             may be possible)  → Side A becomes read-only                     │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯 CKA Exam Quick Recall

If asked "how many etcd node failures can a 5-member cluster tolerate?" — the answer is 2. Formula: floor(5/2) + 1 = 3 needed for quorum, so 5 - 3 = 2 failures tolerated. Always use odd numbers for etcd cluster size.

💡 Why Even Numbers Are Dangerous

A 4-member etcd cluster tolerates only 1 failure — the same as a 3-member cluster — but uses more resources. Worse, with 4 members a network partition can create two equal groups (2 vs 2), and neither has quorum, causing a total outage. Odd numbers eliminate this symmetric split risk.

etcd Backup & Restore in HA — Critical CKA Skill

Backing up and restoring etcd is a guaranteed CKA exam topic. In an HA cluster, you must back up from one member and restore to all members.

Terminal — etcd backup & restore (CKA exam critical) 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: BACKUP — Take snapshot from any healthy member
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /tmp/etcd-backup.db \
    --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
    --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
    --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
    --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# Verify the backup
$ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /tmp/etcd-backup.db --write-table
+----------+----------+------------+------------+
|   HASH   | REVISION | TOTAL KEYS | TOTAL SIZE |
+----------+----------+------------+------------+
| abc12def |   150234 |       1847 |     4.2 MB |
+----------+----------+------------+------------+

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: RESTORE — Stop API server, restore, restart
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# On EACH control plane node:
$ mv /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml /tmp/

# Restore with a NEW data directory (don't overwrite!)
$ ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /tmp/etcd-backup.db \
    --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored \
    --name=cp-1 \
    --initial-cluster="cp-1=https://10.0.0.1:2380,cp-2=https://10.0.0.2:2380,cp-3=https://10.0.0.3:2380" \
    --initial-advertise-peer-urls=https://10.0.0.1:2380

# Update etcd manifest to point to new data dir
$ sed -i 's|/var/lib/etcd|/var/lib/etcd-restored|g' \
    /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml

# Restart API server
$ mv /tmp/kube-apiserver.yaml /etc/kubernetes/manifests/

etcd Backup Strategy — When & Where

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  etcd BACKUP BEST PRACTICES                                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │  BACKUP SCHEDULE:                                         │               │
│  │                                                           │               │
│  │  CronJob every 30 min ──▶ /backups/etcd-{timestamp}.db   │               │
│  │  Keep last 48 backups (24 hours of 30-min snapshots)      │               │
│  │  Store offsite: S3 / Azure Blob / GCS bucket ✅           │               │
│  │  NEVER store backups only on the etcd nodes! ❌            │               │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                                              │
│  RESTORE SCENARIOS:                                                          │
│  ┌───────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ Scenario          │ Action                               │               │
│  ├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤               │
│  │ 1 etcd member     │ Remove & re-add member (simpler)   │               │
│  │ lost              │ etcdctl member add                   │               │
│  ├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤               │
│  │ Quorum lost       │ Full restore from snapshot on ALL    │               │
│  │ (majority down)   │ remaining members                    │               │
│  ├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤               │
│  │ Data corruption   │ Full restore from known-good         │               │
│  │                   │ snapshot + verify cluster health      │               │
│  ├───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤               │
│  │ Disaster recovery │ Restore snapshot to new cluster +    │               │
│  │                   │ update certificates if needed         │               │
│  └───────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                                              │
│  ⚠️  REMEMBER: etcd restore creates a NEW cluster ID — all members          │
│     must restore from the SAME snapshot to form a consistent cluster!        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ Common Mistake: Forgetting TLS Flags

The #1 reason etcdctl commands fail on the CKA exam: missing the --cacert, --cert, and --key flags. These certs are always at /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/. Also remember to set ETCDCTL_API=3 — version 2 has different commands!

35.5 Leader Election Deep Dive

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

In an HA cluster, only one instance of the kube-scheduler and kube-controller-manager is actively making decisions at any time. This is managed through leader election using the Kubernetes Lease API.

Leader Election — How It Works

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LEADER ELECTION MECHANISM                                                   │
│                                                                              │
│  Component        │ Active Instances │ Election Method                       │
│  ─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────               │
│  kube-apiserver   │ ALL (active-     │ No election needed —                  │
│                   │ active)          │ stateless, LB distributes             │
│  ─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────               │
│  kube-scheduler   │ ONE (leader)     │ Lease object in kube-system          │
│                   │                  │ --leader-elect=true                   │
│  ─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────               │
│  kube-controller  │ ONE (leader)     │ Lease object in kube-system          │
│  -manager         │                  │ --leader-elect=true                   │
│  ─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────────               │
│  etcd             │ ALL (Raft        │ Raft protocol — internal             │
│                   │ consensus)       │ leader for write ordering            │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │  LEASE-BASED LEADER ELECTION FLOW:                          │            │
│  │                                                              │            │
│  │  1. All instances try to acquire a Lease object              │            │
│  │  2. Winner writes its identity + timestamp to Lease          │            │
│  │  3. Leader renews Lease every ~2 seconds (leaseDuration)     │            │
│  │  4. Other instances watch — if Lease expires, they compete   │            │
│  │  5. Fastest to write wins → new leader in ~15 seconds        │            │
│  │                                                              │            │
│  │  Timeline:                                                   │            │
│  │  ─────────── Leader CP-1 ──────────── ╳ (dies)              │            │
│  │  ───── standby CP-2 ────────────────── ★ new leader ──────  │            │
│  │  ───── standby CP-3 ────────────────── standby ───────────  │            │
│  │                               ~15s gap                       │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Inspecting Leader Election

Terminal — check current leaders 
# List all leases in kube-system
$ kubectl get lease -n kube-system
NAME                      HOLDER       AGE
kube-scheduler            cp-1_xxx     3d
kube-controller-manager   cp-1_xxx     3d

# Describe scheduler lease — see who holds it
$ kubectl describe lease kube-scheduler -n kube-system
Name:         kube-scheduler
Namespace:    kube-system
Spec:
  Holder Identity:    cp-1_xxxxxxxx-xxxx  ← THIS is the active leader
  Lease Duration:     15s
  Renew Time:         2024-03-15T10:45:32Z

# Check leader-elect flags in scheduler manifest
$ cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-scheduler.yaml | grep leader
    - --leader-elect=true    ← Enables leader election (default)
💡 What Happens During Leader Failover

When the leader dies, there's a brief gap (~15 seconds) before a new leader is elected. During this time, no new pods are scheduled and no controller actions happen (like scaling Deployments). Existing pods continue running — they don't depend on the scheduler or controller-manager once scheduled.

🏋️ Practice Drill — Leader Election Investigation

Scenario: Your team reports that new pods aren't being scheduled. You suspect the scheduler leader may have failed over. Investigate and verify.

  1. Check which node currently holds the scheduler lease:
    kubectl get lease kube-scheduler -n kube-system -o yaml
  2. Check the controller-manager lease as well:
    kubectl get lease kube-controller-manager -n kube-system -o jsonpath='{.spec.holderIdentity}'
  3. Verify the lease renewTime is recent (within last ~15 seconds). If stale → the leader may have crashed
  4. Check scheduler pod logs for election events:
    kubectl logs -n kube-system kube-scheduler-cp-1 | grep -i "leader\|elected\|lost"
  5. Verify all control plane nodes are healthy:
    kubectl get nodes -l node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane

Expected outcome: You can identify which control plane node is the active leader and confirm the lease is being renewed.

35.6 Load Balancer Configuration for HA

The load balancer is the front door to your HA cluster. All kubectl commands, worker node registrations, and internal API calls go through it. If the LB fails, the entire cluster is unreachable — making the LB itself another critical HA component.

Load Balancer Health Check Flow

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LOAD BALANCER HEALTH CHECK & ROUTING                                       │
