│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐                                        │
│  │        kubectl / kubelet        │                                        │
│  │        All API requests         │                                        │
│  └───────────────┬─────────────────┘                                        │
│                  │                                                            │
│                  ▼ :6443                                                      │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐                                        │
│  │        LOAD BALANCER            │      Health Check:                      │
│  │      (HAProxy / nginx)          │      GET /healthz on :6443              │
│  │                                 │      Every 5 seconds                    │
│  │   Rules:                        │                                        │
│  │   - TCP mode (Layer 4)          │      Response 200 → Backend UP ✅      │
│  │   - Round-robin distribution    │      No response  → Remove from pool ❌│
│  │   - 3 failed checks → remove   │                                        │
│  │   - 2 passed checks → re-add   │                                        │
│  └──┬──────────┬──────────┬───────┘                                        │
│     │          │          │                                                  │
│     ▼          ▼          ▼                                                  │
│  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐                                              │
│  │CP-1  │  │CP-2  │  │CP-3  │                                              │
│  │:6443 │  │:6443 │  │:6443 │                                              │
│  │ ✅   │  │ ✅   │  │ ❌   │  ← CP-3 failed health check                 │
│  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘    → removed from pool                       │
│  ▲ 50%     ▲ 50%     (0%)       → traffic split between CP-1 & CP-2       │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

nginx Stream Proxy Configuration (Alternative to HAProxy)

/etc/nginx/nginx.conf — stream proxy for K8s API 
stream {
    upstream kubernetes_api {
        # Round-robin between control plane nodes
        server 10.0.0.1:6443 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
        server 10.0.0.2:6443 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
        server 10.0.0.3:6443 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
    }

    server {
        listen 6443;
        proxy_pass kubernetes_api;
        proxy_timeout 10m;
        proxy_connect_timeout 1s;
    }
}
⚠️ LB Single Point of Failure?

Yes — a single load balancer is itself a SPOF. In production, use keepalived + VRRP for LB failover (two LB hosts sharing a virtual IP), or use a cloud-managed load balancer (AWS NLB, Azure LB, GCP TCP LB) which is inherently HA.

keepalived + VRRP — Making the Load Balancer HA

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LOAD BALANCER HIGH AVAILABILITY WITH keepalived                             │
│                                                                              │
│  Problem: Single LB = Single Point of Failure                                │
│  Solution: Two LBs sharing a Virtual IP (VIP) via VRRP protocol             │
│                                                                              │
│                     ┌──────────────┐                                        │
│   kubectl ────▶     │  VIP:        │     ← Clients connect to VIP           │
│   kubelet           │  10.0.0.100  │        NOT individual LB IPs            │
│                     └──────┬───────┘                                        │
│                            │                                                 │
│              ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐                                  │
│              │                           │                                   │
│     ┌────────▼────────┐        ┌────────▼────────┐                          │
│     │  LB-1 (MASTER)  │  VRRP  │  LB-2 (BACKUP)  │                          │
│     │  10.0.0.10      │◄──────▶│  10.0.0.11       │                          │
│     │  Owns VIP ✅    │ health  │  Standby ⏳      │                          │
│     │  Priority: 101  │ checks  │  Priority: 100   │                          │
│     └────────┬────────┘        └────────┬─────────┘                          │
│              │                           │                                   │
│              └─────────┬─┬───────────────┘                                  │
│                        │ │ │                                                 │
│                   CP-1 CP-2 CP-3                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  Failover Flow:                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │  1. LB-1 sends VRRP advertisements every 1 second          │            │
│  │  2. LB-2 monitors — if no VRRP from LB-1 for 3 seconds    │            │
│  │  3. LB-2 claims the VIP → sends gratuitous ARP             │            │
│  │  4. Traffic redirects to LB-2 in <5 seconds ✅            │            │
│  │  5. When LB-1 recovers → reclaims VIP (preempt mode)       │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

35.7 Production HA Checklist for anihpj

Here's everything needed to deploy the anihpj Django application on a production HA Kubernetes cluster. Use this as your go-live checklist.

anihpj Production HA Architecture

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  anihpj PRODUCTION HA DEPLOYMENT                                             │
│                                                                              │
│                        ┌───────────────┐                                    │
│    Users ──────▶       │  Ingress /    │                                    │
│                        │  Cloud LB     │                                    │
│                        └───────┬───────┘                                    │
│                                │                                             │
│               ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐                            │
│               ▼                ▼                ▼                            │
│  ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐                   │
│  │  anihpj-web    │ │  anihpj-web    │ │  anihpj-web    │                   │
│  │  replica 1     │ │  replica 2     │ │  replica 3     │                   │
│  │  (worker-1)    │ │  (worker-2)    │ │  (worker-3)    │                   │
│  └───────┬────────┘ └───────┬────────┘ └───────┬────────┘                   │
│          └──────────┬───────┘                   │                            │
│                     ▼                           ▼                            │
│          ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐                      │
│          │  anihpj-db (PostgreSQL StatefulSet)        │                      │
│          │  Primary + Read Replica                     │                      │
│          │  PVC: 50Gi on fast-ssd StorageClass         │                      │
│          └────────────────────────────────────────────┘                      │
│                                                                              │
│  Infrastructure:                                                             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
│  │  3× Control Plane (HA)  │  3× Workers  │  HAProxy LB (HA)    │          │
│  │  etcd backup: 6h cycle  │  autoscaler  │  keepalived + VIP   │          │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Production Readiness Checklist

Category Requirement Status
Control Plane 3+ CP nodes across availability zones ✅ Required
etcd backup CronJob every 6 hours ✅ Required
Load balancer with health checks on /healthz ✅ Required
Workloads PodDisruptionBudget on anihpj (minAvailable: 2) ✅ Required
Anti-affinity: spread anihpj pods across nodes ✅ Required
Resource requests/limits on all containers ✅ Required
Liveness and readiness probes configured ✅ Required
Monitoring Alerts for node NotReady and pod crash loops ✅ Required
Central logging (Fluentd/Fluent Bit → ELK/Loki) ✅ Required
Security RBAC: least-privilege ServiceAccounts ✅ Required
NetworkPolicies: restrict inter-namespace traffic ✅ Required
Secrets encrypted at rest in etcd ✅ Required
Anti-Patterns ❌ Never run single-replica Deployments in prod ⚠️ Avoid
❌ Never run without resource limits ⚠️ Avoid
❌ Never skip PodDisruptionBudgets ⚠️ Avoid
anihpj-pdb.yaml — PodDisruptionBudget 
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-pdb
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  minAvailable: 2          # At least 2 pods must always be running
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web
---
# Anti-affinity in Deployment to spread pods across nodes
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj-web
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100
            podAffinityTerm:
              labelSelector:
                matchExpressions:
                - key: app
                  operator: In
                  values:
                  - anihpj-web
              topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      containers:
      - name: anihpj
        image: anihpj/web:v2.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 15
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10

✅ Chapter 35 Competency Checklist

  • Can you explain the difference between stacked and external etcd topologies?
  • Can you initialize a multi-master cluster with kubeadm init --control-plane-endpoint?
  • Can you calculate etcd quorum for any cluster size?
  • Can you find the current scheduler leader using kubectl get lease?
  • Can you configure HAProxy/nginx to load-balance API servers?
  • Can you set up PodDisruptionBudgets and anti-affinity for production workloads?

Chapter 36: Cloud Kubernetes — EKS, GKE, AKS

Managed vs self-managed, cloud storage/networking, identity, deploying anihpj.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~1.5 hours ☁️ Cloud 🎯 Production

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Compare managed Kubernetes services — EKS, GKE, AKS features, pricing, and trade-offs
  • ✅ Map CKA concepts to cloud — which skills transfer directly and which differ
  • ✅ Configure cloud-native storage — StorageClass with CSI drivers for AWS, Azure, GCP
  • ✅ Integrate cloud identity — IRSA, Workload Identity, Managed Identity for pod-level access
  • ✅ Deploy anihpj to any cloud — complete walkthrough from cluster creation to running app
  • ✅ Configure Cluster Autoscaler — automatically scale nodes based on pod demand
CKA Exam Relevance
Cluster Architecture, Installation & Configuration — 25% Storage — 10%
🎯 CKA Exam Note

The CKA exam uses a vanilla Kubernetes cluster, not managed cloud. However, understanding cloud Kubernetes helps you recognize what kubeadm automates vs what cloud providers abstract away. These are skills you need the day after passing CKA.

36.1 Managed vs Self-Managed Kubernetes

CONCEPT

All three major cloud providers offer managed Kubernetes — they handle the control plane while you manage the worker nodes and workloads. Here's how they compare.

Feature EKS (AWS) GKE (Google) AKS (Azure)
Control Plane Managed ($0.10/hr) Managed (free tier avail.) Managed (free!)
Worker Nodes Self-managed / Fargate Standard / Autopilot VMSS node pools
Networking VPC CNI (AWS native) VPC-native / Calico Azure CNI / kubenet
Load Balancer ALB / NLB GCP TCP/HTTP LB Azure LB / App GW
Storage EBS CSI driver PD CSI driver Azure Disk CSI
Identity IAM Roles (IRSA) Workload Identity Managed Identity
CKA Relevance Concepts apply ✅ Concepts apply ✅ Concepts apply ✅

Managed vs Self-Managed — Responsibility Model

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SELF-MANAGED (kubeadm)          │  CLOUD-MANAGED (EKS/GKE/AKS)           │
│                                   │                                         │
│  YOU manage:                      │  CLOUD manages:                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────┐      │  ┌─────────────────────────┐           │
│  │  Control Plane           │      │  │  Control Plane    ☁️    │           │
│  │  ├── API Server          │      │  │  ├── API Server (HA)    │           │
│  │  ├── Scheduler           │      │  │  ├── Scheduler          │           │
│  │  ├── Controller Mgr      │      │  │  ├── Controller Mgr     │           │
│  │  ├── etcd (backup/HA)    │      │  │  └── etcd (auto-backup) │           │
│  │  └── Certificates        │      │  └─────────────────────────┘           │
│  ├─────────────────────────┤      │                                         │
│  │  Worker Nodes            │      │  YOU manage:                           │
│  │  ├── kubelet             │      │  ┌─────────────────────────┐           │
│  │  ├── kube-proxy          │      │  │  Worker Nodes            │           │
│  │  └── Container runtime   │      │  │  ├── Node pool config    │           │
│  ├─────────────────────────┤      │  │  └── Autoscaling rules   │           │
│  │  Networking              │      │  ├─────────────────────────┤           │
│  │  ├── CNI plugin          │      │  │  Workloads              │           │
│  │  └── Load Balancer       │      │  │  ├── Deployments         │           │
│  ├─────────────────────────┤      │  │  ├── Services             │           │
│  │  Upgrades                │      │  │  └── Config/Secrets      │           │
│  │  └── kubeadm upgrade     │      │  └─────────────────────────┘           │
│  └─────────────────────────┘      │                                         │
│                                   │  Upgrade: Click button / az aks upgrade │
│  Effort: ████████████ HIGH       │  Effort: ████░░░░░░░░ LOW               │
│                                   │                                         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

36.2 CKA Concepts in Cloud Kubernetes

🎯 CKA EXAM TOPIC

The good news: most CKA skills transfer directly to cloud Kubernetes. Your kubectl commands, YAML manifests, and troubleshooting skills work everywhere. Here's exactly what changes and what stays the same.

Self-Managed vs Cloud-Managed — What Changes?

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CKA SKILL                SELF-MANAGED     CLOUD-MANAGED                    │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────          │
│  kubeadm init/join         You do it ✋      Not needed ☁️                   │
│  etcd backup/restore       You do it ✋      Auto-managed ☁️                 │
│  Certificate rotation      You do it ✋      Auto-rotated ☁️                 │
│  Cluster upgrades          kubeadm ✋       Cloud console / CLI ☁️          │
│  Node scaling              Manual ✋         Cluster Autoscaler ☁️           │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────          │
│  kubectl commands          Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  YAML manifests            Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  RBAC                      Same ✅           Same ✅ + Cloud IAM             │
│  NetworkPolicies           Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  PV / PVC                  Same ✅           Same ✅ + Cloud Disks           │
│  Troubleshooting           Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  Pod scheduling            Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  Services / Ingress        Same ✅           Same ✅ + Cloud LB              │
│  Resource limits           Same ✅           Same ✅                          │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────          │
│                                                                              │
│  🔑  KEY INSIGHT: ~80% of CKA skills are IDENTICAL on cloud K8s.           │
│      The 20% that differ are infrastructure setup tasks that cloud          │
│      providers handle FOR you. That's WHY companies use managed K8s.        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CKA to Cloud Translation

When you see a CKA task like "backup etcd" — on EKS/GKE/AKS, this is handled automatically. But the concept of "etcd holds cluster state, and losing it means losing the cluster" still applies. The CKA teaches you why things matter; cloud just automates the how.

36.3 Cloud-Native Storage and Networking

Cloud providers use CSI (Container Storage Interface) drivers to connect Kubernetes PersistentVolumes to cloud block storage. You write the same PVC YAML — only the StorageClass provisioner changes.

CSI Architecture — How Cloud Storage Plugs In

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CSI (Container Storage Interface)                                           │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐              │
│  │  Pod      │      │  Kubernetes  │      │  Cloud Provider  │              │
│  │  (anihpj)│─────▶│  PVC → PV    │─────▶│  CSI Driver      │              │
│  └──────────┘      └──────────────┘      └────────┬─────────┘              │
│                                                    │                         │
│                                           ┌────────▼─────────┐              │
│                                           │  Cloud Disk API  │              │
│                                           │  (EBS / PD / AD) │              │
│                                           └──────────────────┘              │
│                                                                              │
│  Your YAML (same pattern):       StorageClass (cloud-specific):             │
│  ┌──────────────────────┐       ┌────────────────────────────────┐          │
│  │ apiVersion: v1        │       │ provisioner:                   │          │
│  │ kind: PVC             │       │   AWS:   ebs.csi.aws.com      │          │
│  │ spec:                 │       │   GCP:   pd.csi.storage.gke.io│          │
│  │   storageClass: fast  │       │   Azure: disk.csi.azure.com   │          │
│  │   resources:          │       │ parameters:                    │          │
│  │     requests:         │       │   type: gp3 / pd-ssd / Premium│          │
│  │       storage: 20Gi   │       └────────────────────────────────┘          │
│  └──────────────────────┘                                                   │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

StorageClass Configurations by Cloud Provider

storageclass-aws.yaml — AWS EBS StorageClass 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-ebs
provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com      # AWS EBS CSI driver
parameters:
  type: gp3                        # General Purpose SSD (gp3)
  encrypted: "true"                # Encrypt at rest
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer  # Schedule-aware binding
allowVolumeExpansion: true
storageclass-azure.yaml — Azure Disk StorageClass 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-azure
provisioner: disk.csi.azure.com    # Azure Disk CSI driver
parameters:
  skuName: Premium_LRS             # Premium SSD
  cachingmode: ReadOnly
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
allowVolumeExpansion: true
storageclass-gcp.yaml — GCE Persistent Disk StorageClass 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-gce
provisioner: pd.csi.storage.gke.io  # GCE PD CSI driver
parameters:
  type: pd-ssd                       # SSD persistent disk
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
allowVolumeExpansion: true
🎯 CKA Exam Relevance

The CKA tests you on PersistentVolume, PersistentVolumeClaim, and StorageClass concepts — the YAML structure is identical across all providers. Know the volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer pattern — it ensures the volume is created in the same zone as the pod.

36.4 Cloud Identity Integration

In cloud Kubernetes, pods can assume cloud IAM roles without storing credentials. This is the production-grade way to give your application access to cloud resources like databases, storage buckets, and message queues.

Pod → ServiceAccount → Cloud IAM Role → Cloud Resource

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CLOUD IDENTITY INTEGRATION — How Pods Access Cloud Resources               │
│                                                                              │
│  ❌ BAD: Hardcoded credentials (Secret with access keys)                    │
│  ┌──────┐   Secret: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID      ┌──────────┐                    │
│  │ Pod  │──────────────────────────────────▶│ S3 Bucket│  ← Key leaked!    │
│  └──────┘                                   └──────────┘                    │
│                                                                              │
│  ✅ GOOD: IAM Role via ServiceAccount (no credentials stored)              │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────┐          │
│  │ Pod  │───▶│ K8s Service  │───▶│ Cloud IAM   │───▶│ Cloud    │          │
│  │      │    │ Account      │    │ Role         │    │ Resource │          │
│  └──────┘    └──────────────┘    └─────────────┘    └──────────┘          │
│                                                                              │
│  How Each Cloud Does It:                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │  AWS (IRSA):                                                     │       │
│  │  Pod → ServiceAccount → IAM Role via OIDC → AWS Resources       │       │
│  │  (annotation: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn)                        │       │
│  │                                                                  │       │
│  │  Azure (Workload Identity):                                      │       │
│  │  Pod → ServiceAccount → Managed Identity → Azure Resources      │       │
│  │  (annotation: azure.workload.identity/client-id)                │       │
│  │                                                                  │       │
│  │  GCP (Workload Identity):                                        │       │
│  │  Pod → KSA → GSA binding → GCP Resources                        │       │
│  │  (annotation: iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account)                   │       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj Example:                                                             │
│  Pod (anihpj-web) → SA (anihpj-sa) → IAM Role → Cloud Database Access     │
│  No passwords in YAML! No secrets to rotate! ✅                              │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Why This Matters for anihpj

Instead of storing database passwords in Kubernetes Secrets, anihpj pods can authenticate to a cloud-managed PostgreSQL database using IAM roles. The ServiceAccount annotation is the only config needed — no credentials ever appear in your YAML manifests or container environment.

ServiceAccount Annotations — Per Cloud Provider

serviceaccount.yaml — Cloud IAM integration (all 3 providers) 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# AWS EKS — IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  annotations:
    # This links the K8s SA to an AWS IAM Role
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-db-role
---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# Azure AKS — Workload Identity
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  annotations:
    azure.workload.identity/client-id: "abc-def-123-456"
  labels:
    azure.workload.identity/use: "true"    # Required label!
---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# GCP GKE — Workload Identity Federation
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  annotations:
    iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account: anihpj-app@project-id.iam.gserviceaccount.com
deployment.yaml — Using the annotated ServiceAccount 
# Reference the SA in your Deployment — that's it!
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa    # ← Pod inherits cloud IAM role
      containers:
      - name: anihpj
        image: anihpj/web:v2.1
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          # No password needed — IAM auth handles it!
          value: "postgresql://anihpj-db.cluster-xyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com/anihpj"
🎯 CKA Focus — ServiceAccounts

The CKA exam tests serviceAccountName in pod specs. While cloud IAM annotations are provider-specific and not directly tested, understanding how ServiceAccounts bind to pods and that automountServiceAccountToken: false restricts API access is core CKA knowledge.

36.5 Deploying anihpj to Cloud Kubernetes

The same Kubernetes manifests you use locally work on cloud. The differences are how you create the cluster and how you connect kubectl. Here's the complete walkthrough for all three clouds.

Cloud Deployment Flow — 4 Steps on Any Cloud

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DEPLOYMENT FLOW (same for all clouds)                                       │
│                                                                              │
│  Step 1               Step 2               Step 3              Step 4       │
│  ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐   ┌───────────┐ │
│  │ Create      │     │ Configure   │     │ Apply same   │   │ Expose    │ │
│  │ Managed     │────▶│ kubectl     │────▶│ YAML as      │──▶│ via Cloud │ │
│  │ Cluster     │     │ Context     │     │ local env    │   │ LB/Ingress│ │
│  └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └──────────────┘   └───────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Step 1 & 2: Create Cluster + Configure kubectl

Terminal — create cluster and connect (all 3 clouds) 
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AWS EKS
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
$ eksctl create cluster --name anihpj-prod --region us-east-1 --nodes 3
$ aws eks update-kubeconfig --name anihpj-prod --region us-east-1

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Azure AKS
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
$ az aks create --resource-group anihpj-rg --name anihpj-prod \
    --node-count 3 --generate-ssh-keys
$ az aks get-credentials --resource-group anihpj-rg --name anihpj-prod

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Google GKE
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
$ gcloud container clusters create anihpj-prod --zone us-central1-a --num-nodes 3
$ gcloud container clusters get-credentials anihpj-prod --zone us-central1-a

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Verify connection (same for ALL clouds!)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
$ kubectl get nodes
NAME                  STATUS   ROLES   AGE   VERSION
node-pool-xxxx-001    Ready    <none>  2m    v1.29.0
node-pool-xxxx-002    Ready    <none>  2m    v1.29.0
node-pool-xxxx-003    Ready    <none>  2m    v1.29.0

Step 3 & 4: Deploy anihpj (Same YAML, Any Cloud)

Terminal — deploy anihpj to cloud cluster 
# Same commands whether on EKS, AKS, or GKE!
$ kubectl create namespace anihpj
$ kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml -n anihpj
$ kubectl apply -f anihpj-service.yaml -n anihpj

# Expose via cloud LoadBalancer (auto-provisions cloud LB)
$ kubectl expose deployment anihpj-web --type=LoadBalancer \
    --port=80 --target-port=8000 -n anihpj

# Wait for external IP
$ kubectl get svc -n anihpj -w
NAME          TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP      PORT(S)
anihpj-web    LoadBalancer   10.100.0.52   52.186.xxx.xxx   80:31234/TCP

# Test it! 🎉
$ curl http://52.186.xxx.xxx/
<!DOCTYPE html><html>...anihpj is running!...
🎯 Focus on CKA Core Concepts

The CKA exam won't ask you to create an EKS/AKS/GKE cluster. But it will test Deployments, Services, PVCs, and troubleshooting — which are identical on cloud and on-prem. Master the core concepts, and they transfer everywhere.

36.6 Cluster Autoscaler

The Cluster Autoscaler automatically adjusts the number of worker nodes in your cluster. When pods can't be scheduled (no node has enough resources), it adds nodes. When nodes are underutilized for 10+ minutes, it removes them.

Cluster Autoscaler Decision Flow

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CLUSTER AUTOSCALER — HOW IT WORKS                                          │
│                                                                              │
│  SCALE UP:                                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────┐                                                     │
│  │ New pod created    │                                                     │
│  │ (e.g. HPA scales  │                                                     │
│  │  anihpj to 10)    │                                                     │
│  └────────┬───────────┘                                                     │
│           │                                                                  │
│           ▼                                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│  │ Scheduler tries to │ NO  │ Pod stays PENDING   │                          │
│  │ place pod on node  │────▶│ (Insufficient CPU/  │                          │
│  └────────────────────┘     │  Memory)             │                          │
│                              └────────┬───────────┘                          │
│                                       │                                      │
│                                       ▼                                      │
│                              ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│                              │ Cluster Autoscaler  │                          │
│                              │ detects pending pod  │                          │
│                              └────────┬───────────┘                          │
│                                       │                                      │
│                                       ▼                                      │
│                              ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│                              │ Cloud API: Create   │                          │
│                              │ new node instance   │                          │
│                              └────────┬───────────┘                          │
│                                       │ (~2-5 min)                           │
│                                       ▼                                      │
│                              ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│                              │ Node Ready → Pod    │                          │
│                              │ scheduled ✅         │                          │
│                              └────────────────────┘                          │
│                                                                              │
│  SCALE DOWN:                                                                │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │  Node utilization < 50% for 10 minutes                     │             │
│  │       │                                                     │             │
│  │       ▼                                                     │             │
│  │  Check: Can all pods be rescheduled to other nodes?        │             │
│  │       │                                                     │             │
│  │       ▼  YES                                                │             │
│  │  Cordon → Drain → Terminate node instance                  │             │
│  │  (Respects PDB! Won't violate minAvailable)                │             │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ Autoscaler & PodDisruptionBudgets

The Cluster Autoscaler respects PDBs during scale-down. If removing a node would violate a PodDisruptionBudget (e.g., anihpj's minAvailable: 2), the node won't be removed. This is why PDBs are important even with autoscaling!

HPA + Cluster Autoscaler — The Two-Level Scaling Loop

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TWO-LEVEL AUTOSCALING — HPA (pods) + CA (nodes)                            │
│                                                                              │
│  Traffic increases → CPU hits 80% threshold                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─── Level 1: HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) ─────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                                                                       │   │
│  │  anihpj Deployment: replicas 3 → 5 → 8 → 12                         │   │
│  │                                                                       │   │
│  │  ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ... ┌───┐ │   │
│  │  │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │Pod│ │???│     │???│ │   │
│  │  └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘     └───┘ │   │
│  │  ──────── running ────────  ──scheduled──  ─── PENDING! ───────── │   │
│  │                                             no room on existing    │   │
│  │                                             nodes → triggers CA    │   │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                              │                                           │
│                              ▼                                           │
│  ┌─── Level 2: Cluster Autoscaler (CA) ──────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                                    │  │
│  │  Node pool: 3 nodes → 4 nodes → 5 nodes                          │  │
│  │                                                                    │  │
│  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌─────┐│  │
│  │  │ Node-1   │  │ Node-2   │  │ Node-3   │  │ Node-4   │  │ N-5 ││  │
│  │  │ 4 pods   │  │ 4 pods   │  │ 4 pods   │  │ 2 pods   │  │ NEW ││  │
│  │  │ ████████ │  │ ████████ │  │ ████████ │  │ ████░░░░ │  │ ... ││  │
│  │  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └─────┘│  │
│  │                                                                    │  │
│  │  CA calls cloud API → provisions new node → ~2-5 min warm-up     │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                          │
│  SCALING TIMELINE:                                                       │
│  ─────┬───────────┬───────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────        │
│   0s  │ HPA       │ HPA creates   │ CA provisions    │ All pods         │
│       │ detects   │ new pods      │ new node         │ running ✅       │
│       │ high CPU  │ (15-30s)      │ (2-5 min)        │                  │
│  ─────┴───────────┴───────────────┴──────────────────┴──────────        │
│                                                                          │
│  KEY INSIGHT: HPA scales pods FAST (seconds), CA scales nodes SLOW      │
│  (minutes). Always ensure enough headroom or use "pause pods" to        │
│  pre-provision node capacity for faster scaling.                         │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
hpa-anihpj.yaml — HPA triggers pod scaling, which triggers CA 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-hpa
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
  minReplicas: 3            # Minimum for HA
  maxReplicas: 20           # Max pods — budget limit
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70   # Scale when CPU > 70%
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80   # Also scale on memory pressure
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60    # Wait 1 min before scaling up
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 4
        periodSeconds: 60               # Add max 4 pods per minute
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300   # Wait 5 min before scaling down
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 25
        periodSeconds: 60               # Remove max 25% per minute
💡 Pause Pods — Pre-warming Node Capacity

Node provisioning takes 2-5 minutes. To avoid cold-start delays, some teams deploy "pause pods" — low-priority pods that reserve node capacity but do nothing. When real pods need space, the pause pods are evicted (they have PriorityClass: low), and real pods are scheduled instantly. The CA then provisions new nodes to replace the displaced pause pods in the background.

✅ Chapter 36 Competency Checklist

  • Can you explain the difference between managed and self-managed Kubernetes?
  • Can you identify which CKA skills transfer directly to cloud K8s?
  • Can you write a cloud-provider StorageClass with CSI provisioner?
  • Can you explain how pods access cloud resources via IAM without secrets?
  • Can you create a managed cluster and deploy workloads on any of the 3 clouds?
  • Can you describe how the Cluster Autoscaler scales nodes up and down?

Chapter 37: CI/CD and GitOps

CI/CD pipelines, ArgoCD, Helm, Kustomize — automating Kubernetes deployments.

🔴 Advanced ⏱️ ~2 hours 🔄 CI/CD 🎯 Production

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Build a CI/CD pipeline — from code push to deployed pods, end-to-end
  • ✅ Understand GitOps principles — Git as single source of truth with ArgoCD
  • ✅ Deploy anihpj with ArgoCD — practical GitOps setup and sync workflow
  • ✅ Package with Helm — create reusable, configurable Kubernetes charts
  • ✅ Customize with Kustomize — base + overlay pattern for multi-environment deployment
  • ✅ Choose Helm vs Kustomize — when to use each and how they complement each other
CKA Exam Relevance
Workloads & Scheduling — 15% Application Lifecycle Management
🎯 CKA Exam Note

CI/CD and GitOps are not directly tested on the CKA exam. However, Helm and Kustomize appear increasingly in CKA environments as deployment tools. More importantly, these are skills every CKA-certified engineer uses daily in production.

37.1 CI/CD Pipeline for Kubernetes

CONCEPT

CI/CD automates the path from code commit to running pods. Every push to Git triggers a pipeline: build, test, package as container image, push to registry, and deploy to Kubernetes.

CI/CD Pipeline — End to End

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CI/CD PIPELINE FOR KUBERNETES                                               │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐    │
│  │  📝  │  │  🔨  │  │  ✅  │  │  🏷️  │  │  📦  │  │  ☸️  │  │  🔍  │    │
│  │ CODE │→│BUILD │→│ TEST │→│ TAG  │→│ PUSH │→│DEPLOY│→│VERIFY│    │
│  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘    │
│                                                                              │
│  Git       Docker    Unit      Docker    Container  kubectl    Smoke        │
│  Push      Build     Tests     Tag       Registry   apply     Tests         │
│                      + Lint    v1.2.3    (ECR/ACR/  -f deploy  curl /health │
│                                          GCR/GHCR)                          │
│                                                                              │
│  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
│                                                                              │
│  EXAMPLE: anihpj CI/CD with GitHub Actions                                   │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │  on: push to main                                               │        │
│  │                                                                  │        │
│  │  jobs:                                                           │        │
│  │    build:                                                        │        │
│  │      ├── Checkout code                                           │        │
│  │      ├── Run Django tests (python manage.py test)               │        │
│  │      ├── Build Docker image                                      │        │
│  │      ├── Tag: ghcr.io/user/anihpj:${{ github.sha }}            │        │
│  │      └── Push to GitHub Container Registry                       │        │
│  │                                                                  │        │
│  │    deploy:                                                       │        │
│  │      ├── Update image tag in deployment YAML                    │        │
│  │      ├── kubectl apply -f k8s/                                   │        │
│  │      └── kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-web           │        │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 CI vs CD vs CD

CI (Continuous Integration) = build + test on every commit. CD (Continuous Delivery) = automatically prepare a deployable artifact. CD (Continuous Deployment) = automatically deploy to production. Most teams do CI + Continuous Delivery, with manual approval before production.

Complete GitHub Actions Workflow for anihpj

.github/workflows/ci-cd.yaml — Full pipeline 
name: anihpj CI/CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }}/anihpj-web

jobs:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 1: Test (CI)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4

    - name: Set up Python
      uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      with:
        python-version: "3.12"

    - name: Install dependencies
      run: pip install -r requirements.txt

    - name: Run Django tests
      run: python manage.py test --verbosity=2

    - name: Run linter
      run: flake8 . --max-line-length=120

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 2: Build & Push Image (CD prep)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  build:
    needs: test           # Only build if tests pass
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'   # Only on main
    permissions:
      packages: write     # Push to GHCR

    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4

    - name: Log in to Container Registry
      uses: docker/login-action@v3
      with:
        registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
        username: ${{ github.actor }}
        password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

    - name: Build and push Docker image
      uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
      with:
        context: .
        push: true
        tags: |
          ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }}
          ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:latest

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 3: Update manifest repo (GitOps trigger)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════
  update-manifests:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - name: Checkout manifest repo
      uses: actions/checkout@v4
      with:
        repository: youruser/anihpj-k8s-manifests
        token: ${{ secrets.MANIFEST_REPO_TOKEN }}

    - name: Update image tag
      run: |
        cd overlays/production
        kustomize edit set image \
          anihpj-web=${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }}

    - name: Commit and push
      run: |
        git config user.name "GitHub Actions"
        git config user.email "actions@github.com"
        git commit -am "Deploy anihpj ${{ github.sha }}"
        git push    # ArgoCD picks this up automatically!
🎯 Key Takeaway for CKA

The CKA won't ask you to write CI/CD YAML, but understanding this pipeline helps you grasp why image tags matter (they trigger rolling updates), why container registries matter (image pull policies, imagePullSecrets), and how deployments update — all directly tested on the CKA.

37.2 GitOps with ArgoCD

CONCEPT

GitOps means Git is the single source of truth for your cluster state. Instead of running kubectl apply from a CI pipeline (push-based), a GitOps tool like ArgoCD watches your Git repo and syncs changes automatically (pull-based).

Traditional CI/CD (Push) vs GitOps (Pull)

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TRADITIONAL CI/CD (Push-Based):       GITOPS (Pull-Based):                 │
│                                                                              │
│  Developer pushes code                 Developer pushes code                 │
│       │                                     │                                │
│       ▼                                     ▼                                │
│  CI builds & tests                    CI builds & tests                    │
│       │                                     │                                │
│       ▼                                     ▼                                │
│  CD runs kubectl apply ⚠️              CD updates manifest                   │
│  (push to cluster directly)            in Git repo                           │
│       │                                     │                                │
│       ▼                                     ▼                                │
│  Deployed. But...                      ArgoCD detects Git change             │
│  - Who ran it? 🤷                            │                                │
│  - What changed? 🤷                          ▼                                │
│  - How to rollback? 😬                 ArgoCD syncs to cluster ✅            │
│  - Drift undetected 😱                                                       │
│                                         Benefits:                            │
│                                         ✅ Full audit trail (Git log)       │
│                                         ✅ Rollback = git revert             │
│                                         ✅ Drift detection & auto-fix      │
│                                         ✅ PR-based deployments              │
│                                         ✅ No cluster creds in CI           │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

ArgoCD Architecture

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ARGOCD SYNC LOOP                                                            │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐      Poll every 3min     ┌──────────────────┐            │
│  │              │   ◀─────────────────────  │                  │            │
│  │  Git Repo    │                           │    ArgoCD        │            │
│  │  (manifests) │   Detect diff?            │    Controller    │            │
│  │              │   ─────────────────────▶  │                  │            │
│  └──────────────┘                           └────────┬─────────┘            │
│                                                       │                      │
│         Git state ≠ Cluster state?                   │ Sync!                │
│         → OutOfSync status                           │                      │
│         → Auto-sync or manual approval               ▼                      │
│                                              ┌──────────────────┐           │
│                                              │   K8s Cluster    │           │
│                                              │   ┌──────────┐   │           │
│                                              │   │ anihpj   │   │           │
│                                              │   │ pods     │   │           │
│                                              │   └──────────┘   │           │
│                                              │   Status: Synced │           │
│                                              │   Health: Healthy │           │
│                                              └──────────────────┘           │
│                                                                              │
│  ArgoCD UI Dashboard:                                                        │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │ App: anihpj     │ Sync: ✅ Synced │ Health: 💚 Healthy    │             │
│  │ Repo: github.com/user/anihpj-k8s   │ Revision: abc1234   │             │
│  │ Target: anihpj namespace            │ Auto-Sync: ON       │             │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

37.3 ArgoCD for anihpj — Practical Setup

Here's a complete walkthrough of setting up ArgoCD to deploy anihpj using GitOps.

Step 1: Install ArgoCD

Terminal — install ArgoCD 
# Create namespace and install ArgoCD
$ kubectl create namespace argocd
$ kubectl apply -n argocd \
    -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml

# Wait for all pods to be ready
$ kubectl -n argocd get pods -w
NAME                                  READY   STATUS    RESTARTS
argocd-server-xxxxx                   1/1     Running   0
argocd-repo-server-xxxxx              1/1     Running   0
argocd-application-controller-xxxxx   1/1     Running   0
argocd-redis-xxxxx                    1/1     Running   0

# Get the initial admin password
$ kubectl -n argocd get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret \
    -o jsonpath="{.data.password}" | base64 -d

# Access the UI (port-forward for local access)
$ kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd 8080:443

Step 2: Create ArgoCD Application for anihpj

anihpj-argocd-app.yaml — ArgoCD Application 
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/youruser/anihpj-k8s-manifests
    targetRevision: main          # Track main branch
    path: overlays/production     # Kustomize overlay path
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: anihpj
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true                 # Delete resources removed from Git
      selfHeal: true              # Fix manual changes (drift)
    syncOptions:
    - CreateNamespace=true        # Auto-create namespace

anihpj GitOps Workflow — Day-to-Day

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DAILY GITOPS WORKFLOW FOR anihpj                                            │
│                                                                              │
│  1. Developer updates anihpj code                                            │
│     └── CI builds new image: anihpj/web:v2.1                                │
│                                                                              │
│  2. Developer updates manifest repo (PR)                                     │
│     └── Change: image: anihpj/web:v2.0 → image: anihpj/web:v2.1           │
│                                                                              │
│  3. PR reviewed & merged to main                                           │
│     └── Git log: "Bump anihpj to v2.1 — fix login bug"                     │
│                                                                              │
│  4. ArgoCD detects change (3 min polling or webhook)                        │
│     └── Status: OutOfSync → Syncing → Synced ✅                              │
│                                                                              │
│  5. ArgoCD applies changes to cluster                                        │
│     └── Rolling update: v2.0 pods → v2.1 pods                              │
│                                                                              │
│  6. Something wrong? Rollback in seconds!                                    │
│     └── $ git revert HEAD && git push                                     │
│     └── ArgoCD syncs back to v2.0 ✅                                         │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

ArgoCD Troubleshooting — Common Issues

Terminal — ArgoCD debugging commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# CHECK APPLICATION STATUS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app get anihpj
Name:               anihpj
Project:            default
Server:             https://kubernetes.default.svc
Namespace:          anihpj
Sync Status:        OutOfSync     ← Drift detected!
Health Status:      Degraded       ← Something's wrong

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# SEE WHAT'S DIFFERENT (drift detection)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app diff anihpj
===== apps/Deployment anihpj/anihpj-web ======
  replicas: 3               # Git says 3
- replicas: 5               # Cluster has 5 (someone used kubectl scale!)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# FORCE SYNC (override manual changes)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app sync anihpj --force --prune

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# CHECK SYNC HISTORY  
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app history anihpj
ID  DATE                  REVISION
1   2024-03-10 09:00:00   abc1234 (Deploy v2.0)
2   2024-03-12 14:30:00   def5678 (Fix login bug)
3   2024-03-15 11:00:00   ghi9012 (Scale to 3 replicas)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# ROLLBACK TO PREVIOUS SYNC
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ argocd app rollback anihpj 2    # Go back to revision 2

ArgoCD Status Cheat Sheet

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ARGOCD STATUS REFERENCE                                                     │
│                                                                              │
│  SYNC STATUS:                                                                │
│  ┌──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Synced     ✅    │ Cluster matches Git — everything is good         │    │
│  │ OutOfSync  ⚠️    │ Cluster differs from Git — needs sync            │    │
│  │ Unknown    ❓    │ ArgoCD can't determine state                      │    │
│  └──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                              │
│  HEALTH STATUS:                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Healthy    💚    │ All resources are running correctly               │    │
│  │ Progressing ⏳   │ Resource is updating (rolling update in progress) │    │
│  │ Degraded   🟡    │ Resource is unhealthy (CrashLoop, pending)       │    │
│  │ Suspended  ⏸️    │ Resource is suspended (scaled to 0)              │    │
│  │ Missing    ❌    │ Resource exists in Git but not in cluster         │    │
│  └──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                              │
│  COMMON TROUBLESHOOTING:                                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │ OutOfSync + Healthy        │ Someone used kubectl directly —    │        │
│  │                            │ sync to restore Git state          │        │
│  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤        │
│  │ Synced + Degraded          │ Git state applied but pods         │        │
│  │                            │ crashing — check image/config      │        │
│  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤        │
│  │ OutOfSync + Missing        │ New resource in Git not yet        │        │
│  │                            │ synced — run sync                  │        │
│  ├────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤        │
│  │ Unknown + Unknown          │ ArgoCD can't reach repo or         │        │
│  │                            │ cluster — check credentials        │        │
│  └────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

37.4 Helm — Kubernetes Package Manager

CONCEPT

Helm packages Kubernetes manifests into charts — reusable, versioned, configurable bundles. Think of it as apt or pip for Kubernetes. Values files let you customize the same chart for dev, staging, and production.

Helm Chart Structure — anihpj Example

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  HELM CHART DIRECTORY STRUCTURE                                              │
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj-chart/                                                               │
│  ├── Chart.yaml              # Chart metadata (name, version, description)  │
│  ├── values.yaml             # Default configuration values                  │
│  ├── values-prod.yaml        # Production overrides                          │
│  ├── templates/                                                              │
│  │   ├── _helpers.tpl        # Template helper functions                     │
│  │   ├── deployment.yaml     # {{ .Values.replicas }}                       │
│  │   ├── service.yaml        # {{ .Values.serviceType }}                    │
│  │   ├── ingress.yaml        # {{ .Values.host }}                           │
│  │   ├── configmap.yaml      # {{ .Values.dbHost }}                         │
│  │   ├── secret.yaml         # {{ .Values.dbPassword }}                     │
│  │   └── hpa.yaml            # {{ .Values.autoscaling.enabled }}            │
│  └── charts/                 # Sub-chart dependencies                        │
│                                                                              │
│  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
│                                                                              │
│  values.yaml (defaults):       values-prod.yaml (overrides):                │
│  ┌─────────────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────────────┐                  │
│  │ replicas: 1              │   │ replicas: 3              │                  │
│  │ image:                   │   │ image:                   │                  │
│  │   tag: latest            │   │   tag: v2.1-stable       │                  │
│  │ serviceType: ClusterIP   │   │ serviceType: LoadBalancer│                  │
│  │ resources:               │   │ resources:               │                  │
│  │   requests:              │   │   requests:              │                  │
│  │     cpu: 100m            │   │     cpu: 500m            │                  │
│  │     memory: 128Mi        │   │     memory: 512Mi        │                  │
│  │ ingress:                 │   │ ingress:                 │                  │
│  │   enabled: false         │   │   enabled: true          │                  │
│  │   host: ""               │   │   host: anihpj.com      │                  │
│  └─────────────────────────┘   └─────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                                                              │
│  Same chart, different values → different environments! 🎯                  │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Helm Essential Commands

Terminal — Helm workflow 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# CHART REPOSITORIES
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
$ helm repo update
$ helm search repo nginx

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# INSTALL / UPGRADE / ROLLBACK
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# Install anihpj chart with production values
$ helm install anihpj ./anihpj-chart --values values-prod.yaml -n anihpj

# Upgrade (change image tag to v2.2)
$ helm upgrade anihpj ./anihpj-chart --values values-prod.yaml \
    --set image.tag=v2.2 -n anihpj

# Oops! Rollback to previous release
$ helm rollback anihpj 1 -n anihpj

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# INSPECT / DEBUG
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
$ helm list -A                    # List all releases
$ helm history anihpj -n anihpj   # Release history
$ helm get values anihpj -n anihpj  # Current values
$ helm template ./anihpj-chart    # Render without installing (dry-run)

Helm Go Template Syntax — Crash Course

Helm templates use Go's text/template language. Here's how a real Helm deployment template looks for anihpj:

templates/deployment.yaml — Helm Go template syntax 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  # {{ .Release.Name }} = helm install NAME → "anihpj"
  name: {{ .Release.Name }}-web
  labels:
    {{- include "anihpj.labels" . | nindent 4 }}
spec:
  replicas: {{ .Values.replicas | default 1 }}
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: {{ .Release.Name }}
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: {{ .Release.Name }}
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: {{ .Chart.Name }}
        image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}"
        ports:
        - containerPort: {{ .Values.containerPort | default 8000 }}
        {{- if .Values.resources }}
        resources:
          {{- toYaml .Values.resources | nindent 10 }}
        {{- end }}
        {{- if .Values.env }}
        env:
        {{- range .Values.env }}
        - name: {{ .name }}
          value: {{ .value | quote }}
        {{- end }}
        {{- end }}
      {{- if .Values.nodeSelector }}
      nodeSelector:
        {{- toYaml .Values.nodeSelector | nindent 8 }}
      {{- end }}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GO TEMPLATE CHEAT SHEET:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# {{ .Values.xxx }}           → Read from values.yaml
# {{ .Release.Name }}         → Helm release name
# {{ .Chart.Name }}           → From Chart.yaml
# {{ .Release.Namespace }}    → Target namespace
# {{- ... }}                  → Trim left whitespace
# {{ ... -}}                  → Trim right whitespace
# {{ if .Values.x }}...{{ end }}  → Conditional
# {{ range .Values.list }}        → Loop
# {{ include "tpl" . }}           → Include named template
# {{ toYaml .Values.x | nindent 4 }} → Render as YAML
# {{ .Values.x | default "val" }}     → Default value
# {{ .Values.x | quote }}        → Wrap in quotes
⚠️ Helm Debugging Tips

Template not rendering correctly? Use helm template ./chart --debug to see the rendered YAML. Common errors: missing {{- }} whitespace control causes extra blank lines, and forgetting | nindent N causes YAML indentation errors. Always validate with helm lint ./chart before installing.

37.5 Kustomize — Built-in Manifest Customization

CONCEPT

Kustomize is built into kubectl — no separate binary needed. It uses a base + overlays pattern: write your YAML once (base), and patch it per environment (overlays). No templates, no Go syntax — just strategic merge patches on plain YAML.

Kustomize Directory Structure — anihpj

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  KUSTOMIZE BASE + OVERLAYS PATTERN                                          │
│                                                                              │
│  anihpj-kustomize/                                                           │
│  ├── base/                          # Shared across ALL environments         │
│  │   ├── kustomization.yaml         # Lists resources to include             │
│  │   ├── deployment.yaml            # anihpj base: replicas: 1              │
│  │   ├── service.yaml               # ClusterIP service                     │
│  │   └── configmap.yaml             # Shared config values                  │
│  │                                                                           │
│  └── overlays/                      # Per-environment patches                │
│      ├── dev/                                                                │
│      │   ├── kustomization.yaml     # bases: [../../base]                   │
│      │   └── patch-replicas.yaml    # replicas: 1, cpu: 100m               │
│      │                                                                       │
│      ├── staging/                                                            │
│      │   ├── kustomization.yaml     # bases: [../../base]                   │
│      │   └── patch-resources.yaml   # replicas: 2, cpu: 250m               │
│      │                                                                       │
│      └── production/                                                         │
│          ├── kustomization.yaml     # bases: [../../base]                   │
│          ├── patch-replicas.yaml    # replicas: 5                           │
│          └── patch-resources.yaml   # cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi             │
│                                                                              │
│  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
│                                                                              │
│  Deploy:   $ kubectl apply -k overlays/production/                           │
│  Preview:  $ kubectl kustomize overlays/production/                          │
│                                                                              │
│  Result: base/deployment.yaml MERGED with production patches                 │
│  → replicas: 5, cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi                                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Kustomize Files for anihpj

base/kustomization.yaml & overlays/production/kustomization.yaml 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# base/kustomization.yaml
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- deployment.yaml
- service.yaml
- configmap.yaml
commonLabels:
  app: anihpj

---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# overlays/production/kustomization.yaml
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- ../../base
namePrefix: prod-              # All resources get prod- prefix
namespace: anihpj-production
patches:
- path: patch-replicas.yaml
  target:
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
images:
- name: anihpj/web
  newTag: v2.1-stable          # Override image tag for prod

---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# overlays/production/patch-replicas.yaml
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
spec:
  replicas: 5                  # Production: 5 replicas
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: anihpj
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1000m"
            memory: "1Gi"

Kustomize Power Features — Generators & Transformers

kustomization.yaml — Generators, labels, and config management 
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- ../../base

namespace: anihpj-production

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# CONFIGMAP GENERATOR — auto-creates ConfigMaps from files/literals
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
configMapGenerator:
- name: anihpj-config
  literals:
  - DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production
  - ALLOWED_HOSTS=anihpj.com
  - DEBUG=false

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# SECRET GENERATOR — auto-creates Secrets
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
secretGenerator:
- name: anihpj-db-creds
  literals:
  - DB_PASSWORD=supersecret   # In real life: use sealed-secrets or external-secrets
  type: Opaque

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# COMMON LABELS — applied to ALL resources
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
commonLabels:
  app: anihpj
  env: production
  team: backend

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# COMMON ANNOTATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
commonAnnotations:
  managed-by: kustomize

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# IMAGE TRANSFORMER — override image tags
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
images:
- name: anihpj/web
  newName: ghcr.io/youruser/anihpj-web
  newTag: v2.1-stable

# ═══════════════════════════════════════
# PATCHES — strategic merge or JSON patches
# ═══════════════════════════════════════
patches:
- path: patch-replicas.yaml
  target:
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
🏋️ Practice Drill — Kustomize Multi-Environment Deploy

Scenario: Deploy anihpj to staging and production using Kustomize overlays with different configurations.

  1. Preview what Kustomize will generate for production:
    kubectl kustomize overlays/production/
  2. Apply the production overlay:
    kubectl apply -k overlays/production/
  3. Verify resources were created in the correct namespace:
    kubectl get all -n anihpj-production
  4. Check that labels were applied correctly:
    kubectl get deploy -n anihpj-production --show-labels
  5. Verify the image tag matches the overlay:
    kubectl get deploy anihpj-web -n anihpj-production -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
  6. Compare with staging config:
    diff <(kubectl kustomize overlays/staging/) <(kubectl kustomize overlays/production/)

Key point: Same base YAML, but -k flag applies environment-specific patches. This is DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) for Kubernetes!

🎯 CKA Exam — Kustomize

The CKA exam may reference Kustomize in the exam environment. Know these two commands cold: kubectl apply -k <directory> applies Kustomize configuration, and kubectl kustomize <directory> previews without applying. The -k flag is your friend!

37.6 Helm vs Kustomize — When to Use Each

This is one of the most common questions in Kubernetes. The short answer: they serve different purposes and complement each other.

Feature Helm Kustomize
Approach Templates + values Patches on base YAML
Learning Curve Moderate (Go templates) Low (plain YAML patches)
Package Sharing ✅ Chart repos (Artifact Hub) ❌ Not designed for sharing
Complexity Go template syntax Strategic merge patches
Built into kubectl No (separate binary) Yes (kubectl apply -k)
Versioning Chart versions + release history Git-based versioning
Rollback helm rollback git revert
Best For Shared / public apps (nginx, Redis) Internal config management
anihpj Use Case Package for distribution Multi-env deployments

Decision Tree — Helm, Kustomize, or Both?

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DECISION TREE                                                               │
│                                                                              │
│  Need to share charts publicly?                                              │
│  ├── YES → Use Helm (chart repos)                                           │
│  └── NO                                                                      │
│      │                                                                       │
│      Need complex templating (conditionals, loops)?                         │
│      ├── YES → Use Helm (Go templates are powerful)                         │
│      └── NO                                                                  │
│          │                                                                   │
│          Just need different configs per environment?                        │
│          ├── YES → Use Kustomize (simplest solution)                        │
│          └── BOTH                                                            │
│              │                                                               │
│              Use Helm to render, Kustomize to patch!                        │
│              $ helm template ./chart | kubectl apply -k -                   │
│                                                                              │
│  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════     │
│                                                                              │
│  🔑 PRO TIP: Many teams use BOTH together:                                 │
│                                                                              │
│  ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐          │
│  │  Helm    │────▶│ Rendered │────▶│Kustomize │────▶│ Applied  │          │
│  │  Chart   │     │  YAML    │     │  Patches │     │ to K8s   │          │
│  └──────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────┘          │
│                                                                              │
│  ArgoCD natively supports both Helm and Kustomize as sources!               │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

✅ Chapter 37 Competency Checklist

  • Can you explain the stages of a CI/CD pipeline for Kubernetes?
  • Can you write a GitHub Actions workflow that builds, tests, and pushes a container image?
  • Can you articulate why GitOps (pull-based) is preferred over push-based CD?
  • Can you set up an ArgoCD Application pointing at a Git repo?
  • Can you troubleshoot ArgoCD sync/health status issues?
  • Can you create a Helm chart with Go templates and values files?
  • Can you read and write Helm Go template syntax (conditionals, loops, defaults)?
  • Can you use kubectl apply -k with a base + overlay Kustomize structure?
  • Can you use Kustomize generators (configMapGenerator, secretGenerator)?
  • Can you explain when to choose Helm vs Kustomize vs both?
💡 Real-World Stack for anihpj — Putting It All Together

Here's how a production team would combine everything from this chapter:

  • GitHub Actions — CI: run Django tests, build Docker image, push to GHCR
  • Kustomize — Manage dev/staging/prod overlays for anihpj manifests
  • ArgoCD — Watch the manifest repo, auto-sync to cluster, detect drift
  • Helm — Install third-party tools (nginx-ingress, cert-manager, Prometheus)
  • HPA + Cluster Autoscaler — Scale pods and nodes based on traffic

This is the modern Kubernetes deployment stack used by most teams. You now understand every layer.
